question: Which of the following compounds has the highest standard molar entropy?
explanation:
The standard molar entropy is the entropy of one mole of substance under standard state. Greater the degree of freedom of gas, greater will be the molar entropy.
Degree of freedom $=3 \mathrm{~N}$
here, $\mathrm{N}=$ number of molecules
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958008/preptracker/questions/q_104370_explanation_1764958008369.png
Hence, $\mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ has highest standard molar entropy.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Physical Meaning and Characteristics of Entropy

question: In which of the processes, the entropy will decrease?
explanation: For $\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(1)} \rightarrow \underset{\text { Ice }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})}$,  degree of  randomness  of   $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules decreases, when freezing of water into ice takes place, so, $\Delta \mathrm{S} < 0$.

options.text: Temperature of ice is raised from 10 K to 115 K

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes During Phase Transformation

question: For the reactions,     $2 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$   $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$  What are the signs of $\Delta \mathrm{S}$, respectively?
explanation:
(i) $2 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=1-2=-1$
So, $\Delta \mathrm{S} < 0$
(ii) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$;
$\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=\left(1+\dfrac{1}{2}\right)-1=\dfrac{1}{2}$
So, $\Delta \mathrm{S}>0$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes for an Ideal Gas During Different Thermodynamic Process

question: The increase in entropy in $\mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ of a substance when it absorbs 1 kJ of heat energy at 3 K is
explanation:
$\operatorname{Entropy} \,\,(\Delta {\text{S}}) = \frac{{{\text{ Heat }}({\text{q}})}}{{{\text{ Temperature }}({\text{T}})}} = \frac{{1000\,{\text{J}}}}{{3{\text{K}}}}$
Entropy $(\mathrm{S})=333.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Physical Meaning and Characteristics of Entropy

question:
Amongst the chemical reactions given below, the reactions with increasing entropy are
(i) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
(ii) $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$
(iii) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(iv) $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow$ Mixture of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$; system is changed from liquid to gas. So, entropy is increases.
(ii) $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$; in this reaction product has more number of gaseous moles. Thus, entropy increases.
(iii) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$; gaseous system is converted into liquid system. So, entropy decreases.
(iv) $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow$ mixture of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, on mixing total gaseous volume increases in comparison to their individual volume hence entropy increases.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes During Physical Process and Chemical Reactions

question:
In the process:
${{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{s}}, - {\rm{10}}^\circ {\rm{ C}},{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{atm}}) \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{l}},{\rm{10}}^\circ {\rm{ C}},{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{atm}})}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{P}}}$ for ice ${\mathrm{=9 \, \mathrm{cal} \, \mathrm{deg}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{C}_{P}}}$ for ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=18 \, \mathrm{cal} \, \mathrm{deg}^{-1}}}$ ${\mathrm{\mathrm{mol}^{-1}}}$. Latent heat of fusion of ice ${\mathrm{=1440 \, \mathrm{cal} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}}$ at ${\mathrm{0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$. The entropy change for the above process is ${\rm{6}}.{\rm{258}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{de}}{{\rm{g}}^{ - 1}}$ ${\mathrm{\mathrm{mol}^{-1}}}$.
Give the total number of steps in which the third law of thermodynamics is used.
explanation:
Step 1. (using the third law of thermodynamics):
(For changing
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O(s,}}\left( { - {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C,1\,atm}}} \right) \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}\left( {{\rm{s,}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C,}}\,{\rm{1\,atm}}} \right)$
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{1}}} = \int_{{\rm{ - 10}}}^{\rm{0}} {\rm{n}} \frac{{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{P}}}}}{{\rm{T}}}\,{\rm{dT}} = 1 \times 9 \times 2.3 \times {\rm{log}}\frac{{273}}{{263}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 0.336\,\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{de}}{{\rm{g}}^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
Step 2 (using the second law of thermodynamics):
${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})\left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\Delta S_{2}=\dfrac{q_{\text {rev }}}{T}=\dfrac{1440}{273}=5.258 \, \mathrm{cal} \, \mathrm{deg}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$
Step 3 (using the third law of themrodynamics):
${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\left(10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right)}}$
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{3}}} = \int_{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{10}}} {\rm{n}} \frac{{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{P}}}}}{{\rm{T}}}\,{\rm{dT}} = 1 \times 18 \times 2.3 \times {\rm{log}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{283}}{{273}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 0.647\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{de}}{{\rm{g}}^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
${\rm{\Delta S}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{3}}}$
$ = 0.336 + 5.258 + 0.647$
$ = 6.258\,\,{\rm{cal}}\,\,{\rm{de}}{{\rm{g}}^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
From above calculations, number of steps which is based on third law of thermodynamics are "2".
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes for an Ideal Gas During Different Thermodynamic Process

question:
Amongst the following, the total number of reactions processes in which the entropy increases are:
a.
$\begin{array}{l} {\rm{2NaHC}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}(\,{\rm{s}}) \to {\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}(\,{\rm{s}}){\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}})\\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{g}}) \end{array}$
b. A liquid cyrstallises into a solid.
c. Temperature of cystalline solid is raised from zero K to 100 K .
d. Hard boiling of an egg.
e. Devitrification of glass.
f. Stretching of a rubber band.
g. Desalination of water.
h. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}, 10 \mathrm{~atm}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}, 1}}$ atom ${\mathrm{)}}$
explanation: Entropy increases in (a), (b), (c), and (h).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes During Physical Process and Chemical Reactions

question: ${\rm{\Delta S}}$ will be highest for
explanation:
We know that,
${{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{gas }}}} > {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{liquid }}}} > {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{solid }}}}$
For a reaction, entropy change is given as :
${\rm{\Delta S}} = {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{(products) }}}} - {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{(reactants) }}}}$
Among the given options only option (b) will have the highest change in entropy ${\rm{(\Delta S)}}$ as solid reactant is getting converted into gaseous product.
subject: Chemistry

question: For which one of the following reactions will there be a positive $\Delta {\text{S}}$ ?
explanation:
$\Delta {\rm{S}}$ (entropy change) is the measure of randomness and thus in solid, liquid and gas, the order of entropy is gas $>$ liquid $>$ solid.
Thus, $\Delta {\rm{S}}$ is positive for the reaction given in option (3).

subtopic: Entropy Changes During Physical Process and Chemical Reactions

question: For which of the process, $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ is negative?
explanation: High pressure reduces volume and decreases entropy hence${\rm{\Delta S}}$ negative.

question: Entropy changes for the process, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$ at normal pressure and $274 \mathrm{~K}$ are given below $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {system }}=-22.13, \Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {surr }}=+22.05$, the process is non-spontaneous because
explanation:
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{universe}}}} = {\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{system}}}} + {\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{surroundings}}}}$
$ = - 22.13 + 22.05 = - 0.08$
For a spontaneous process,${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{u}}}$ must be positive,i.e.
$\Delta {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{universe }}}} = \Delta {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{system }}}} + \Delta {{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{surroundings }}}} > 0$
Thus, the process is non-spontaneous because $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{u}}$ is negative.

options.text:
$\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {system }} \neq \Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {surr }}$
('surr' stands for surroundings and 'u ' stands for universe)

question:
What are the signs of the entropy change (+ or - ) in the following cases.
I: A liquid crystallizes into a solid
II: Temperature of a crystalline solid is raised from 0 K to 115 K
III. $2{\rm{NaHC}}{{\rm{O}}_3}(\,{\rm{s}}) \to $
${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}({\rm{s}}) + {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) + {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{g}})$
IV. $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{g})$
explanation:
(1) When liquid crystallizes into soild, its entropy decreases. $\left(\mathrm{S}_{\text {gas }}>\mathrm{S}_{\text {liquid }}>\mathrm{S}_{\text {solid }}\right)$
(2) If temperature increases, entropy increases.
(3) If gaseous moles increases after reaction, then entropy increases.
(4) $1 \mathrm{H}_{2}$ molecule breaks into 2 gaseous H - atoms, so entropy increases.

subtopic: Entropy Changes During Phase Transformation

question:
In which of the following processes entropy increases?
A. A liquid evaporates to vapour.
B. Temperature of a crystalline solid lowered from 130 K to 0 K .
C.${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$
D. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
When a liquid evaporates to vapour entropy increases.
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$
In this change, number of gaseous product molecules increases, so entropy increases.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$
1 mole of gaseous ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}}$ molecules form 2 moles of gaseous Cl atoms. So entropy increases. When ' ${\mathrm{T}}$ ' decreases, entropy decreases.

question:
Assertion: Molar entropy of vaporisation of water is different from ethanol.

Reason: Water is more polar than ethanol.
explanation: The molar entropy of vaporization varies among substances due to differences in molecular structure and intermolecular forces. Water, with strong hydrogen bonding, exhibits a relatively high molar entropy during vaporization compared to ethanol, which, with weaker hydrogen bonding, generally has a lower molar entropy.So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

question:
Assertion: Chlorine when solidified does not have zero entropy even at absolute zero.


Reason: Chlorine is a pungent smelling gas and it is difficult to solidify it.
explanation: For substance to have zero entropy zero, it must be a perfectly substance. Chlorine is a diatomic molecule and does not form a perfect crystal structure when solidified. This means that even when chlorine is solidified, it does not achieve the perfect order required for its entropy to be zero. Therefore, the assertion is correct. $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ has a pungent smell and is difficult to be solidified. But, it does not explain why its entropy does not reach zero when solidified. So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Enthalpy of graphite is lower than that of diamond.


Reason: Entropy of graphite is greater than that of diamond.
explanation: The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond is more strong in graphite than in diamond, i.e. during combustion less heat is released from graphite and hence enthalpy of graphite is lower. Diamond is crystalline solid which is highly ordered structure while such arrangements are not found in the graphite. So, the degree of disorder is less in diamond. Hence, an entropy of the diamond is lesser than graphite. So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Consider the reactions at $300 \mathrm{~K}$
(a) $4{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + {{\text{O}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \to 2\,{{\text{N}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5}\left( {\text{g}} \right);\Delta {\text{H}} = - 111\,\,{\text{kJ}}$
(b) $4{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + {{\text{O}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \to 2 {{\text{N}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5}\left( s \right);\Delta {\text{H}} = - 219\,{\text{kJ}}$
Thus entropy change for the sublimation of one mole ${{\text{N}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5}\left( s \right)$ is :
explanation:
Reverse the reaction
(b) and add reaction
(a) to get $2\,\mathop {{{\text{N}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5}}\limits_{\left( {\text{s}} \right)} \to 2\,\mathop {{{\text{N}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5}}\limits_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)} {\mkern 1mu} ;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=+219-111=+108 \mathrm{~kJ}$ for 2 moles
$\therefore$ for $1 \mathrm{~mole}=+54 \mathrm{~kJ}=54000 \mathrm{~J}$
$\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {sub }}=\dfrac{\Delta_{\text {sub }} \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}}=\dfrac{54000}{300}=180 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$

question:

question: One mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{s})}$ on melting absorbed 30.5 $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ}}$ of heat and its entropy is increased by $\mathrm{28.8 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}}$. The melting point of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaCl}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{NaCl}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NaCl}_{(1)}}$
Given: $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=30.5 \mathrm{KJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{S}=28.8 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}=28.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{KJK}^{-1}}$
By using $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{30.5}{28.8 \times 10^{-3}}=1059 \mathrm{~K}}$

question:
For the gas phase reaction, $\mathrm{\mathrm{PCl}_{5}(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{PCl}_{3}(g)+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g)}$
Which of the following conditions are correct?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta H=\Delta E+\Delta n_{(g)} R T}$
For the reaction,
$\rm{ \mathrm{PCl}_{5}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}(g)+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g) \\ \Delta n=2-1=1}$
Thus, the value of $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ is positive or $\mathrm{>0}$.
$\rm{\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S}$
For a spontaneous reaction, $\mathrm{\Delta G}$ must be negative. Since in this reaction $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ is positive, so for thenegative value of $\mathrm{\Delta G, \Delta S}$ must be positive or $\mathrm{>0}$.
Hence $\mathrm{\Delta H>0, \Delta S>0}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes During Physical Process and Chemical Reactions

question: Enthalpy of vaporization of benzene is +35.3 $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$ at its boiling point, $\mathrm{80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$. The entropy change in the transition of the vapour to liquid at its boiling point $\left( {{\rm{inJ}}{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$ is
explanation:
According to Gibbs' formula,
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}}$
At equilibrium, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}=0}$
And entropy decreases on going from vapour to liquid.i.e., $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}}$
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{S}=-\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}}=-\dfrac{35300}{80+273}=-100 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Entropy Changes During Phase Transformation

question: When the same quantity of heat is absorbed by a system at two different temperatures $\mathrm{T_{1}}$ and $\mathrm{T_{2}}$, such that $\mathrm{T_{1}>T_{2}}$, change in entropies are $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}_{1}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}_{2}}$ respectively. Then
explanation:
We know that, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}=\dfrac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{T}}}$
As same quantity of heat is absorbed by a system, it means $\mathrm{q}$ is same.
So, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S} \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
As $\mathrm{\mathrm{T}_{1}>\mathrm{T}_{2}}$, therefore $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}_{1} < \Delta \mathrm{S}_{2}}$

question: When 2 moles of an ideal gas $\mathrm{\left(C_{p, m}=\left(\dfrac{5}{2}\right) R\right)}$ is heated from $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ to $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~K}}$ at constant pressure, then the change in entropy of gas $\mathrm{(\Delta S)}$ is

question:
Calculate $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{sys}}^{o}}$ for the following reaction at 373 $\mathrm{\mathrm{KCO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-4.1 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J}, \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\text {univ }}^{o}=56 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\text {universe }}^{o}=56=\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\text {system }}^{o}+\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\text {surroundings }}^{o}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\text {surroundings }}^{o}=\dfrac{-\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_{\text {system }}^{o}}{373}=\dfrac{4.1 \times 10^{4}}{373}=109.9 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{\text {system }}^{o}=56-109.9=-53.9 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}}$

question: Given the following entropy values (in $\mathrm{\mathrm{J} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ ) at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ and 1 atm: $\mathrm{H_{2}(g): 130.6, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g): 223.0, \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g}): 186.7}$. explanation:

$\mathrm{=19.8 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$

question: Calculate the entropy change in surroundings when $\mathrm{1.00 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ is formed under standard conditions. $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation:
Enthalpy change for the formation of 1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$,
$\rm{ \mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)} ; \\ \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Energy released in the above reaction, is absorbed by the surroundings. It means $\mathrm{q_{\text {surr }}=+286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
$\mathrm{\Delta S=\dfrac{q_{\text {surr }}}{T}=\dfrac{+286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{298 \mathrm{~K}}}$

question: The enthalpy of vaporisation of chloroform is $\mathrm{29.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ at its normal boiling point of $\mathrm{61.7^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$. What is the entropy of condensation of chloroform at this temperature ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta_{v a p} H^{\circ}=29.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ and
$\rm{T_{\text {boiling }}=334.7 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta_{v a p} S^{\circ}=\dfrac{\Delta_{v a p} H^{\circ}}{T_{\text {boiling }}}=87.84 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}=10.6 \mathrm{R}}$
$\rm{\left(R=8.314 J^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta_{\text {condensation }} S^{\circ}=-10.6 R}$

question: A reaction, $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}+\mathrm{q}}$ is found to have a positive entropy change. The reaction will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S}$
For the given reaction, $\mathrm{\Delta H=-v e}$ as it is exothermic, $\mathrm{\Delta S=+v e}$ then $\mathrm{\Delta G=-v e}$. So, the reaction will be spontaneous at all temperatures.

question:
The work done when 2 moles of an ideal gas
expands reversibly and isothermally from a
volume of 1 L to 10 L at 300 K is
( $\mathrm{R}=0.0083 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{Kmol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \mathrm{V}_{2} / \mathrm{V}_{1}$
$\begin{aligned}& =-2.303 \times 2 \times 0.0083 \times 300 \times \log (10 / 1) \\& =11.5 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$

question: At 61 K , one mole of an ideal gas of 1.0 L volume expands isothermally and reversibly to a final volume of 10.0 L . What is the work done in the expansion?
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{V}_{1}=1.0 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=10.0 \mathrm{~L}$,
$\mathrm{T}=61 \mathrm{~K}, \quad \mathrm{n}=1, \quad \mathrm{R}=0.0821 \mathrm{Latm} / \mathrm{molK}$
Work done for Isothermal and reversible expansion, ${\rm{W}} = {\rm{nRT}}\ln \left( {{{\rm{V}}_1}/{{\rm{V}}_2}} \right)\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Substituting the value & in the equation (1)
${\rm{W}} = 1 \times 0.0821 \times 61\,\,\,\ln \left( {\frac{1}{{10}}} \right)$
${\text{W}} = 5.0081 \times - 2.302 = - 11.52\,{\text{L}}\,{\text{atm}}.$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question:
At $300 \mathrm{~K}, 3.0$ moles of an ideal gas at 3.0 atm pressure is compressed isothermally to one half of its volume by an external pressure of 6.0 atm. The work done (in kJ ) is
(Given,$\mathrm{R}=0.082 \mathrm{Latm} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
($1 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm}=101.3 \mathrm{~J}$ )
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{~T}=300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{P}=3 \mathrm{~atm}$
From Ideal gas equation ; $\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT}$
$\mathrm{V}=\dfrac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{P}}$
$=\dfrac{3 \text { mole } \times 0.082 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}{3 \mathrm{~atm}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{1}=24.6 \mathrm{~L}$
$\therefore \mathrm{V}_{2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{24.6}{2}=12.3$
$\Delta \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}=12.3-24.6=-12.3 \mathrm{~L}$
$P_{e x t}=6 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}(\Delta \mathrm{V})=-6 \times(-12.3) \mathrm{atmL}$
$=73.8 \mathrm{atmL}$
$\mathrm{W}($ in kJ$)=\dfrac{73.8 \times 101.3 \mathrm{~kJ}}{1000}$
$\mathrm{W}=7.476 \mathrm{~kJ}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}) 2$ mole of an ideal gas is allowed to expand reversibly and isothermally from a pressure of 10 atmospheres to 1 atmosphere. The work done (in kJ ) is ( $\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Work done for isothermal and reversible process is given by
$\mathrm{W}=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{1}}{\mathrm{p}_{2}}$
$=-2.303 \times 2 \times 8.3 \times \mathrm{T} \log \dfrac{10}{1}$
$=-382 \times \mathrm{TJ}=-3.82 \times 10^{-2} \times \mathrm{TkJ}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: An air bag on adiabatic expansion undergoes $5 \%$ increase in its volume. The percentage change in pressure is $\left[\gamma_{\text {air }}=1.4\right]$
explanation:
In an adiabatic expansion, $\mathrm{pV}^{\gamma}=$ constant where, $\mathrm{p}=$ pressure, $\mathrm{V}=$ volume $\gamma=\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}\right)$
Differentiating above equation,
$\mathrm{dp} \cdot \mathrm{V}^{\gamma}+\gamma \mathrm{V}^{\gamma-1} \cdot \mathrm{pdV}=0$
$\dfrac{d p}{p}=\dfrac{-\gamma d V}{V} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d p}{p}=-(1.4) \times 5=-7 \%$
$\therefore$ Percentage change in pressure is $7\,\% .$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done During Reversible and Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion or Compression Process of an Ideal Gas

question: How many times the volume of a diatomic gas should be increased reversibly and adiabatically in order to reduce its rms velocity to half of its initial value?
explanation:
${\text{As}},{\text{ }}{{\text{u}}_{{\text{rms}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3{\text{RT}}}}{{\text{M}}}} $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\left( {{{\text{u}}_{{\text{rms}}}}} \right)}_1}}}{{{{\left( {{{\text{u}}_{{\text{rms}}}}} \right)}_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{{\text{T}}_1}}}{{\,{{\text{T}}_2}}}} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\mathrm{T}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}^{\gamma-1}=\mathrm{T}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}^{\gamma-1}$
$\frac{{{{\text{T}}_1}}}{{\,{{\text{T}}_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{\,{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right)^{\gamma - 1}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$\frac{{{{\left( {{{\text{u}}_{{\text{rms}}}}} \right)}_1}}}{{{{\left( {\,{{\text{u}}_{{\text{rms}}}}} \right)}_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{\,{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{{\gamma - 1}}{2}}} = {\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{\,{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right]^{\left( {\frac{7}{5} - 1} \right) \times \frac{1}{2}}}$
$ = {\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right]^{\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{1}{2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{5}}}$
Given, ${({{\text{u}}_{rms}})_2} = \frac{1}{2}{({{\text{u}}_{rms}})_1}$
$\frac{{{{({{\text{u}}_{rms}})}_1}}}{{{{({{\text{u}}_{rms}})}_2}}} = 2$
${\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{5}}} = 2$
$\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{{\text{V}}_1}}} = {2^5} = 32$
${{\text{V}}_2} = 32\,{{\text{V}}_1}$
A diatomic gas should be expanded 32 times adiabatically, so as to reduce the root mean square velocity to half.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Comparison between Isothermal Reversible and Adiabatic Reversible Expansion of an Ideal Gas

question: If 2.5 moles of an ideal gas at a certain temperature are allowed to expand isothermally and reversibly from an initial volume of $2 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ to $20 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$, the work done by the gas is –16.5 kJ . The temperature (in K ) of the gas is (Round off to the nearest value) $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
Given $n=2.5$ moles,
$\mathrm{V}_{1}=2 \mathrm{dm}^{3} ; \mathrm{V}_{2}=20 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$
$\mathrm{W}=-16.5 \mathrm{~kJ}=-16.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J}$,
$\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{T}=$ ?
Using the formula,
$\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{nRT} \ln \dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}$
$-16.5 \times 10^{3}=-2.5 \times 8.314 \times \operatorname{Tln}\left(\dfrac{20}{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{T}=\dfrac{16.5 \times 10^{3}}{2.5 \times 2.303 \times 8.314}=344.7 \mathrm{~K} \approx 345 \mathrm{~K}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Comparison between Isothermal Reversible and Adiabatic Reversible Expansion of an Ideal Gas

question: If 92 g Na reacts with water in open vessel at 300 K . What is the value of work done?[Assume ideal nature of the gaseous product]
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(1) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
4 moles of $\mathrm{Na}=92 \mathrm{~g}$ of Na
2 moles of Na reacts with 2 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to form 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.
4 moles of Na will react with 4 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to form 2 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.
Work done by expansion of gas $=-\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{RT}$
$=2 \times 8.314 \times 300=-4988.4 \mathrm{~J}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A certain mass of a gas was brought from state A to B by following three different paths, namely 1,2 and 3 , respectively. Which of the following relations is correct for the work done?
explanation:
Work done is area under ${\text{p}}\Delta {\text{V}}$ curve.
Greater the area more will be the work done. Work is a path function.
Since the area under $\mathrm{p} \Delta \mathrm{V}$ curve is largest for path 3 and smallest for path 1, therefore,
${{\text{W}}_1} < {{\text{W}}_2} < {{\text{W}}_3}.$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done Analysis Using PV Graph

question: The following graph indicates the system containing 1 mole of gas involving various steps. When it moves from Z to X, the type of undergoing process is
explanation:
At ${\text{X}} \Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_1} = 300{\text{K}},{{\text{V}}_1} = 20{\text{L}},$ pressure $ = {{\text{p}}_1}$
At, ${\text{Z}} \Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_2} = 600\,{\text{K}},{{\text{V}}_2} = 40\,{\text{L,}}$ pressure = ?
According to universal gas law
$\mathrm{pV}=\mathrm{nRT} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pV} / \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{nR} \quad(\mathrm{nR}=$ constant $)$
Then, $\mathrm{p}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1} / \mathrm{T}_{1}=\mathrm{p}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} / \mathrm{T}_{2}$
$\frac{{{{\text{p}}_1} \times 20}}{{300}} = \frac{{{{\text{p}}_2} \times 40}}{{600}} \Rightarrow {{\text{p}}_1} = {{\text{p}}_2}$ i. e. process is isobaric.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done Analysis Using PV Graph

explanation: According to first law of thermodynamics, the total energy of an isolated system remains constant, though it may change from one form to another. Hence, option (3) is the correct answer.

options.text: The energy of the isolated system plus the energy of the surrounding is constant.

question: Which of the following does not follow first law of thermodynamics? ( $\mathrm{W}=$ work; $\mathrm{q}=$ heat;  $\Delta \mathrm{U}=$ change in internal energy)
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta {\text{U}} = {\text{q}} + {\text{W}}$
For $\mathrm{W}>0, \mathrm{q}>0, \Delta \mathrm{U}=+\mathrm{ve}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{U}>0$
So that $\Delta {\text{U}} \ne {\text{q}} + {\text{W}}$ if ${\text{W}} > 0$ and ${\text{q}} > 0$.
Hence, it doesn't obey first law of thermodynamics ( $\because \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}$ ).

subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: An ideal gas undergoes following process from $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{D} \rightarrow \mathrm{E}$.  Find work done.
explanation:
$|\mathrm{W}|=$ Area under the curve
$ = 2{{\text{P}}_0} \times \left[ {3{{\text{V}}_0} - {{\text{V}}_0}} \right] - {{\text{P}}_0} \times \left[ {3{{\text{V}}_0} - 2{{\text{V}}_0}} \right] = 3{{\text{P}}_0}{{\text{V}}_0}$
Net work is done on the system.

question: A liquid when kept inside a thermally insulated closed vessel at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was mechanically stirred from outside. What will be the correct option for the following thermodynamic parameters?
explanation:
In closed insulated container a liquid stirred with a paddle to increase the temperature, it behaves as an adiabatic container, $\mathrm{q}=0$
From First law of thermodynamics
$\Delta {\rm{U}} = {\rm{q}} + {\rm{w}}\,\,;\,\,{\rm{q}} = 0$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{w}$ (but not zero); $\mathrm{w}>0, \Delta \mathrm{U}>0$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic conditions from the following.
explanation:
For adiabatic expansion ${\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=0}}$
For Free expansion, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}=0}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{W}=0}}$
As, ${\rm{W = }} - {{\rm{p}}_{{\rm{ext }}}}.\Delta {\rm{v}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{dU}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w} \quad\left[1^{\text {st }}\right.}}$ law of thermodynamics ${\mathrm{]}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=0, \mathrm{w}=0, \mathrm{dU}=0}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{dU}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}}\left(\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{0=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}}\left(\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{T}_{1}=\mathrm{T}_{2}}}$ i.e., ${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{T}=0}}$
So ${\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=0, \Delta \mathrm{T}=0, \mathrm{w}=0}}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done During Reversible and Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion or Compression Process of an Ideal Gas

question: A system has internal energy equal to 200 J . 500 J of work is done on the system when 400 J of heat is lost. The final internal energy of the system is ____ J.
explanation:
${\mathrm{w=+500 \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{q}=-400 \mathrm{~J}}}$
$\Delta {\text{U = }}{{\text{U}}_{\text{2}}} - {{\text{U}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = q + w}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{U}_{2}=\mathrm{U}_{1}+\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w}}}$
${\mathrm{=200+-400+500}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{U}_{2}=300 \mathrm{~J}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question:
In an isothermal reversible expansion of 2 moles of ideal gas at 300 K from 1.10 L to 11.0 L , the maximum work done is ____ kcal.
( ${\mathrm{\left.\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)}}$
explanation:
${\mathrm{w=-2.303 n R T \log _{10} \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}}}$
${\mathrm{=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \dfrac{11.0}{1.10}}}$
${\mathrm{=-2.303 \times 2 \times 2 \times 300 \log _{10} 10}}$
${\mathrm{=-2764 \mathrm{cal}=-2.76 \mathrm{kcal}}}$

question: When 1 mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}$ occupying volume $10 \mathrm{~L}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is expanded under adiabatic condition, temperature falls to $150 \mathrm{~K}$. Hence, final volume is
explanation:
For adiabatic expansion, $\frac{{{{\rm{T}}_2}}}{{{T_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{\rm{V}}_1}}}{{{{\rm{V}}_2}}}} \right)^{{\rm{\gamma }} - 1}}$
Here, for $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ (triatomic gas), ${\rm{\gamma }} = 1.33$
$\therefore \left( {\frac{{150}}{{300}}} \right) = {\left( {\frac{{10}}{{{{\rm{V}}_2}}}} \right)^{0.33}}$ or $\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) = {\left( {\frac{{10}}{{{{\rm{V}}_2}}}} \right)^{0.33}}{\rm{ or }}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = \frac{{10}}{{{{\rm{V}}_2}}}$
$\frac{1}{8} = \frac{{10}}{{{{\rm{V}}_2}}} \Rightarrow {{\rm{V}}_2} = 80\,{\rm{L}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done During Reversible and Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion or Compression Process of an Ideal Gas

question: Temperature of 5 moles of a gas is decreased by $2 \mathrm{~K}$ at constant pressure. Indicate the correct statement
explanation:
For 5 moles of gas at temperature T,
${\rm{P}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = 5RT}}$
For 5 moles of gas at temperature ${\rm{T - 2}}$,
${\rm{P}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 5R(T - 2)}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\rm{P}}\left( {{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right){\rm{ = 5R(T - 2 - T)}}\,{\rm{;}}$
${\rm{P\Delta V = - 10R,}}\,\,\,{\rm{ - P\Delta V = 10R}}$
When ${\rm{\Delta V}}$ is negative, W is +ve.

options.text: Work done over the gas is $ = 10{\rm{R}}$

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: In adiabatic conditions, 2 moles of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is expanded such that its volume becomes 27 times. Then, the work done is $\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=6 \mathrm{calmol}^{-1}\right.$ and $\left.\gamma=1.33\right)$
explanation:
Given, $T_{1}=300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=27 \mathrm{~V}_{1}$
$\mathrm{n}=1 \mathrm{~mol}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=6 \mathrm{calmol}^{-1}, \gamma=1.33$
In adiabatic conditions, $\dfrac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}}\right)^{\gamma-1}$
${\rm{ or, }}\frac{{{{\rm{T}}_2}}}{{\,{{\rm{T}}_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{{27}}} \right)^{1.33 - 1}}$
$ = {\left( {\frac{1}{{27}}} \right)^{0.33}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{{27}}} \right)^{1/3}} = \frac{1}{3}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or, }}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {{\rm{T}}_2} = 300 \times \frac{1}{3} = 100\,{\rm{K}}$
Thus, $\mathrm{T}_{2} < \mathrm{T}_{1}$ hence, cooling takes place due to expansion under adiabatic condition.
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}$
$\mathrm{q}=0$ for adiabatic change
$\Delta U=-$ ve because gas expands.
Then, $\mathrm{W}=-\Delta \mathrm{U}$
$ = - {{\rm{C}}_{\rm{V}}}\left( {{{\rm{T}}_2} - {{\rm{T}}_1}} \right)$
$ = - 6(100 - 300) = 1200\,{\rm{cal}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Comparison between Isothermal Reversible and Adiabatic Reversible Expansion of an Ideal Gas

question: To calculate the amount of work done in joules during reversible expansion of an ideal gas, the volume must be expressed in
explanation:
Work done (W)$
=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2}$ are in ratio in the relation. So, unit may be expressed in any one of $\mathrm{m}^{3}, \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ or $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: The work done during the expansion of a gas from a volume of  ${\rm{4}}\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}\,{\rm{to}}\,\,{\rm{6}}\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ against a constant external pressure of $3 \mathrm{~atm}$ is
explanation:
Work done $(\mathrm{W})=-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ext}}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)$
$= - 3 \times (6 - 4) = - 6\,{\rm{L}} - {\rm{atm}}$
$ = - 6 \times 101.32\,{\rm{J}}$$(\because {\text{IL}} - {\text{atm}} = 101.32\,{\text{J}})$
$ = - 607.92 \approx - 608\,{\rm{J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Calculate the work done when 1 mole of an ideal gas is compressed reversibly from 1 bar to 4 bar at a constant temperature of $300 \mathrm{~K}$
explanation:
Work done $=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}}$
$= - 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300\log \frac{1}{4}$
$ = - 3458.3\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{or}}\,3.458\,{\rm{kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: If 1 mole of an ideal gas expands isothermally at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ from $15 \mathrm{~L}$ to $25 \mathrm{~L}$, the maximum work obtained is
explanation:
${{\rm{W}}_{{\rm{rev }}}} = - 2.303\,{\rm{nRT}}\,{\rm{log}}\,\,\frac{{{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}}}$
$=-2.303 \times 1 \times 0.0821 \times(273+37) \log (25 / 15)$
$=-2.303 \times 0.0821 \times 310 \log \dfrac{5}{3}$
$=-13.003 \mathrm{~J}$
(-ve sign shows work of expansion)This value is closer to the value given in option (1).
$\therefore$ Option (1) is correct.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: The four curves $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{A}_{4}$ are shown on $\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram. Which of the curves represents adiabatic process?
explanation: An adiabatic process $(\mathrm{dq}=0)$ curve in a $\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram approaches both $\mathrm{V}$-axis and $\mathrm{p}$-axis. It represents a steep hyperbola, i.e. $\mathrm{A}_{3}$ in the given diagram.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done Analysis Using PV Graph

question:
The work done during reversible isothermal expansion of one mole of hydrogen gas at ${\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$ from pressure of 20 atmosphere to 10 atmosphere is
(Given ${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=2.0 \,\mathrm{cal} \,\mathrm{K}^{-1} \,\mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$ )
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{W}_{\text {rev, iso }}=-2.303 \,\mathrm{nRT} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}}}$
${\mathrm{=-2.303 \times 1 \times 2 \times 298 \times \log 2}}$
${\mathrm{=-2.303 \times 1 \times 2 \times 298 \times 0.3}}$
${\mathrm{=-413.14}}$ calories
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question:
Assertion: If a refrigerator's door is kept open, room gets warmed.


Reason: Material kept inside the refrigerator remains cool.
explanation: In a refrigerator, heat removal from the interior is achieved by the appliance actively transferring heat to the outside. However, leaving the door open allows warm room air to enter inside preventing effective cooling and causing the refrigerator to act as a source of heat, raising both its internal temperature and the room temperature.So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question:
Assertion: At constant temperature and pressure, whatever heat is absorbed by the system is used in doing work.


Reason: Internal energy change is zero.
explanation: ${\text{q}} =  - {\text{w (if }}\Delta {\text{U}} = 0).$ So, option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question:
Assertion: For an isothermal reversible process, $\mathrm{q}=-\mathrm{w}$ i.e.,work done by the system equals the heat absorbed by the system.


Reason: Enthalpy change $(\Delta \mathrm{H})$ is not zero for isothermal process.
explanation:
In an isothermal process, there is no change in the internal energy $(\mathrm{U})$ as it is a function of temperature. Therefore, $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ is zero.
According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w}=\Delta \mathrm{U}$, this implies that if $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ is $0, \mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w}$ $=0$. Therefore, when $\left( {\Delta {\text{U}} = 0} \right), - {\text{w}} = {\text{q}}$. Thus work done by the system is equal to the heat absorbed by the system. Thus, option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question:
Assertion: First law of thermodynamics is identical to the law of conservation of energy.

Reason: It states that the total energy of the universe is constant.
explanation: The first law of thermodynamics and the law of conservation of energy are equivalent, stating that the total energy in an isolated system remains constant, undergoing only transformations or transfers between the system and its surroundings. Thus, option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: The volume of a gas decreases from $500 \mathrm{cc}$ to $300 \mathrm{cc}$ when a sample of gas is compressed by an average pressure of $0.6 \mathrm{~atm}$. During this process $10 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat is liberated. The change in internal energy is
explanation:
Change in
Volume $(\mathrm{V})=500-300=200 \mathrm{cc}=0.2$ litre,
Pressure $(\mathrm{P})=0.6 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\therefore$ Work done $(\mathrm{W})=\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{V}=(0.2 \times 0.6)=0.12$
litre-atm or $0.12 \times 101.3 \mathrm{~J}=12.156 \mathrm{~J}$
${\text{q = - 10J}}$ liberated heat $)$
$\therefore$ Acc to 1st law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}=-10+12.16=2.16 \mathrm{~J}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: A gas is allowed to expand in an insulated container against a constant external pressure of 2.5 bar from $4.5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ to $7 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$. What is the change in internal energy of the gas?
explanation:
$\Delta {\text{U = q + w}}$
Since the container is insulated, ${\text{q = 0}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{V}=-\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)$
Given: $\mathrm{P}=2.5$ bar, $\mathrm{V}_{2}=7 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$
$\begin{gathered}=7 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~L}=7 \mathrm{~L} \\\mathrm{~V}_{1}=4.5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}=4.5 \mathrm{~L} \\\therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{U}=-2.5(7-4.5) \mathrm{L} \text { bar }=-6.25 \mathrm{Lbar} \\=-6.25 \times 100 \mathrm{~J}=-625 \mathrm{~J}[1 \mathrm{~L} \text { bar }=100 \mathrm{~J}]\end{gathered}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: What is internal energy change when $62 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done on the sytem and $128 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat is transferred to surrounding?
explanation:
Workdone on the system, so ${\text{ + ve}}$ $\mathrm{W}=62 \mathrm{~J}$.
Heat is given out, so ${\text{ - ve}}$
$\mathrm{Q}=-128 \mathrm{~J}$
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W} \\& =-128+62=-66 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: When certain volume of gas expands against a constant external pressure of $2.40 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ to $2.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$. If the work obtained is $-0.048 \mathrm{~kJ}$. What is the initial volume of the gas?
explanation:
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}=2.40 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=2.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$
$\mathrm{W}=-0.048 \mathrm{~kJ}=-48 \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{~V}_{1}=$ ?
$\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right) \\& \therefore-48=-2.40 \times 10^{5}\left(2.2 \times 10^{-3}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right) \\& \therefore 48=5.28 \times 10^{2}-\left(2.40 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~V}_{1}\right) \\& \therefore\left(2.40 \times 10^{5}\right) \mathrm{V}_{1}=528-48 \\& \left(2.40 \times 10^{5}\right) \mathrm{V}_{1}=480 \\& \therefore \mathrm{V}_{1}=\dfrac{480}{2.40 \times 10^{5}}=200 \times 10^{-5} \\& \therefore \mathrm{V}_{1}=2.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: For isochoric process, the first law of thermodynamics can be expressed as
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V} \quad\left(\because \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V}\right)$
But for isochoric process, (volume is constant)
$\Delta \mathrm{V}=0$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{v}}$ (at constant volume)
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: When ${\text{x kJ}}$ heat is provided to a system, work equivalent to $\mathrm{yJ}$ is done on it. What is internal energy change during this operation?
explanation:
${\text{Q =+ x k J = 1000 x J, W = +y J}}$
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
$=(1000 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}) \mathrm{J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: In a process, a system performs $238 \mathrm{~J}$ of work on it's surrounding by absorbing $54 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat. What is the change in internal energy of system during this operation?
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}=-238 \mathrm{~J}$ (workdone by the system), $\mathrm{Q}=54 \mathrm{~J}$ (heat absorbed $)$
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
$=54-238=-184 \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: A gas is allowed to expand against a constant external pressure of 2.5 bar from an initial volume ' $\mathrm{x}$ ' $\mathrm{L}$ to final volume of $4.5 \mathrm{~L}$. If amount of work done is $5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ bar, what is the value of ' $\mathrm{x}$ '?
explanation:
${{\text{P}}_{{\text{ext }}}}{\text{ = 2}}{\text{.5}}\,{\text{bar, }}{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = x L,}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.5 L}}$
$\mathrm{W}=-5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ bar $=-5 \mathrm{~L}$ bar
Workdone, $\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)$
$\therefore-5 \mathrm{Lbar}=-2.5 \operatorname{bar}(4.5-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{L}$
$\therefore 5 \mathrm{Lbar}=11.25 \mathrm{~L}$ bar $-2.5 \times \mathrm{Lbar}$
$\therefore 2.5 \times \mathrm{Lbar}=(11.25-5) \mathrm{Lbar}$
$\therefore \mathrm{x}=\dfrac{6.25}{2.5}=2.5 \mathrm{~L}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: During a process, system absorbs $710 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat and increases the internal energy by $460 \mathrm{~J}$. What is the work performed by system?
explanation:
$\mathrm{Q}=710 \mathrm{~J}$ (heat given to system),
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=460 \mathrm{~J}($ increase in internal energy $)$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=460 \mathrm{~J}($ increase in internal energy $)$
According to first law of thermodynamics
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W} \\& \therefore \mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{U}-\mathrm{Q} \\& =460-710=-250 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: What is the constant external pressure of an ideal gas when expanded from $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$ to $3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$, if the work done by the gas is $-5.09 \mathrm{~kJ}$ ?
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A system does $394 \mathrm{~J}$ of work on surrounding by absorbing $701 \mathrm{~J}$ heat. What is the change in internal energy of the system?
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}=-394 \mathrm{~J}($ work done by the system),
$\mathrm{Q}=701 \mathrm{~J}$ (heat absorbed)
According to first law of thermodynamics
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
$=701-394=307 \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: A system gives out ${\text{x J}}$ of heat and does ${\text{y J}}$ of work on it's surrounding. What is the internal energy change?
explanation:
${\text{Q = - xJ, W = - yJ}}$
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y} \mathrm{J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: 1 mole of an ideal gas expands isothermally and reversibly by decreasing pressure from $210 \mathrm{kPa}$ to $105 \mathrm{kPa}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. What is the work done? $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{P}_{1}=210 \mathrm{kPa}, \mathrm{P}_{2}=105 \mathrm{kPa}, \mathrm{T}=300 \mathrm{~K}$.
$\mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{~W}=?$
Workdone in isothermal reversible expansion,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{W}_{\text {max }}=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}} \\& =-2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \log _{10} \dfrac{210}{105} \\& =-2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \log _{10} 2 \\& =2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 0.3010 \\& =1728.99 \mathrm{~J} \\& \mathrm{~W}_{\max }=1729 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: The expansion of gas having no opposing force is called as
explanation: The expansion of gas having no opposing force is called as free expansion.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Two moles of an ideal gas are expanded isothermally from $15 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ to $20 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$. If the amount of work done is $-6 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ bar, find external pressure needed to obtain this work.
explanation:
${{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = 15d}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{,}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 20d}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{,W = - 6d}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}\,\,{\text{bar}}$
$\Delta {\text{V}} = {{\text{V}}_2} - {{\text{V}}_1} = 20 - 15 = 5{\text{d}}{{\text{m}}^3}$
$\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
$\therefore \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}}=-\dfrac{\mathrm{W}}{\Delta \mathrm{V}}=\dfrac{-\left(-6 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{bar}\right)}{5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}=\dfrac{6}{5} \mathrm{bar}$
$=1.2 \mathrm{bar}=1.2 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: When 1 mole of gas is heated at constant volume, the temperature rises from $273 \mathrm{~K}$ to $546 \mathrm{~K}$. If heat supplied to the gas is $\mathrm{xJ}$, then find the correct statement from the following.
explanation:
At constant volume, $\Delta \mathrm{V}=0$
Workdone, $\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{V}=0$
According to first law of thermodynamics
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{x} \mathrm{J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: What is the work done when a gas is compressed from $2.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$ to $1.3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$ at constant external pressure of 4.05 bar?
explanation:
$\mathrm{V}_{1}=2.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{3}=25 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$,
$\mathrm{V}_{2}=1.3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{3}=13 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}=4.05$ bar, $\mathrm{W}=$ ?
Work done in compression,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right) \\& =-4.05 \mathrm{bar} \times(13-25) \mathrm{dm}^{3} \\& =48.6 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \mathrm{bar} \\& =48.6 \times 100 \mathrm{~J} \\& \therefore \mathrm{W}=4860 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A gas is allowed to expand in an insulated container against a constant external pressure of $2.5 \mathrm{~atm}$ form $2.5 \mathrm{~L}$ to $4.5 \mathrm{~L}$, the change in internal energy of the gas in joules is
explanation:
The change in internal energy is given by, $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}$
For insulated container, $\mathrm{Q}=0$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ex }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=-2.5 \mathrm{~atm}(4.5 \mathrm{~L}-2.5 \mathrm{~L})$
$=-5.0 \mathrm{~L} \cdot \mathrm{atm}$
$=-5.0 \times 101.325 \mathrm{~J} \quad(1 \mathrm{~L} \cdot \mathrm{atm}=101.325 \mathrm{~J})$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=-506.625 \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: An ideal gas on isothermal reverisble compression from  $10 \mathrm{~L}$ to $5 \mathrm{~L}$ performs $1730 \mathrm{~J}$ of work at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. Calculate the number of moles of gas involved in compression? $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{V}_{1}=10 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=5 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~W}=1730 \mathrm{~J}$,
$\mathrm{T}=300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{n}=\text { ? }$
Workdone in isothermal reversible compression, $\mathrm{W}_{\text {max }}=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}$
$\therefore 1730\,{\rm{J}} = - 2.303 \times {\rm{n}} \times 8.314{\rm{J}}{{\rm{K}}^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}} \times $
$300\,{\rm{K}} \times {\log _{{\rm{I0}}}}\left( {\frac{5}{{10}}} \right)$
$\therefore {\text{n}} = \frac{{1730\,{\text{J}}}}{{\left( \begin{gathered} - 2.303 \times 8.314\,{\text{J}}\,{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}} \times \hfill \\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,300\,{\text{K}} \times {\log _{10}}(0.5) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right)}}$
$\therefore {\text{n}} = \frac{{1730}}{{ - 5744.14 \times ( - 0.3010)}}{\text{mol}}$
$\therefore {\text{n}} = \frac{{1730}}{{1729}} \approx 1\,{\text{mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Which of the following reactions shows work of compression?
explanation:
When gases convert into solids, it is a work of compression.
$\mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{s})}$
In this reaction, gaseous reactants converted into solid product which results in a decrease of volume and shows work of compression.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: What is the change in the energy of system if $500 \mathrm{cal}$ of heat energy are added to a system and system does $350 \mathrm{cal}$ of work on the surroundings.
explanation:
Heat absorbed, ${\text{q = 500}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{cal}}$
Work done by the system, ${\text{w = - 350}}\,{\text{cal}}$
According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w}=500+(-350)=+150 \mathrm{cal}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: In an adiabatic process, no transfer of heat takes place between system and surroundings. Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following
explanation:
For free expansion, $\mathrm{W}=0($ as $\mathrm{P}=0)$ and for adiabatic process, $\mathrm{q}=0$.
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}=0$, this means internal energy remains constant.
$\Delta \mathrm{T}=0$ in ideal gas as there are no intermolecular attractions.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: The work done during combustion of $\mathrm{9 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg}}$ of ethane, $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ is (Given $\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{deg}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, atomic mass $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{H}}$ $\mathrm{=1)}$
explanation:
Work done in a chemical reaction,
$\rm{\mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{RT}}$
Where, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=}$ number of moles of gaseous products - number of moles of gaseous reactants combustion of ethane is as follows,
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-4.5=-2.5}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{W}=(2.5 \mathrm{~mol})\left(8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\rm{=6235.5 \mathrm{~kJ}=6.2355 \mathrm{~J}}$
Now, 1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}=30 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}$
$\rm{=6.2355 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
Work done for combustion of $\mathrm{30 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}=6.2355 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Work done for combustion of $\mathrm{90 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}$
$\rm{ =\dfrac{6.2355 \times 90}{30} \\ =18.7065 \mathrm{~kJ} \\ =18.71 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question:
Calculate the work done during combustion of $\mathrm{0.138 \mathrm{~kg}}$ of ethanol, $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(l)}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$.
Given: $\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, molar mass of ehtanol $\mathrm{=46 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation:
The combustion of ethanol, invovles the following reaction.
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(l)+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
Given,
Mass of ethanol $\mathrm{=0.138 \mathrm{~kg}=138 \mathrm{~g}}$
Temperature $\mathrm{=300 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Molar mass of ethanol $\mathrm{=46 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
No. of moles of ethanol $\mathrm{=}$ $\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{ethanol}}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}\,\,{\rm{mass}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{ethanol}}}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{138}{46}=3}$
Work done (W) during combustion of $\mathrm{0.138 \mathrm{~kg}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}=\mathrm{nRT}}$
$\rm{ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{nRT} \\ \mathrm{W}=3 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \\ \mathrm{~W}=7482.6 \mathrm{~J} \\ \approx 7482 \mathrm{~J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: An ideal gas expands isothermally and reversibly from $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ to $\mathrm{20 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$, performing $\mathrm{5.187 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of work on surrounding, Calculate number of moles of gas used.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{W}=-\int \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \mathrm{dV}}$
Since, the process is reversible, $\mathrm{\mathrm{p}_{\text {ext }}=\mathrm{p}}$, which gives $\mathrm{\mathrm{W}=-\int \mathrm{p} \cdot \mathrm{dV}}$
Substituting the ideal gas equation for $\mathrm{\mathrm{p}}$ leads to.
$\rm{\mathrm{W}=-\int \dfrac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{V}} \cdot \mathrm{dV}}$
Since the process is isothermal,
$\rm{\begin{gathered}\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{nRT} \operatorname{In}\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}\right) \\-5.187 \times 1000 \mathrm{~J}=-\mathrm{n} \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\\text { In }\left(\dfrac{20 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}{10 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}\right) \\\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\dfrac{5.187 \times 1000}{8.314 \times \operatorname{In} 2} \mathrm{~mol} \\=\dfrac{5.187 \times 1000}{8.314 \times 0.6931} \mathrm{~mol}=3 \mathrm{~mol}\end{gathered}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Three moles of an ideal gas are expanded isothermaly from a volume of $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}$ to $\mathrm{2.5 \mathrm{~L}}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ against a pressure of $\mathrm{1.9 \mathrm{~atm}}$. The work done in joules is
explanation:
Given,
$\rm{ \mathrm{V}_{1}=300 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{3} \\ \mathrm{~V}_{2}=2.5 \mathrm{~L}=25 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$
$\rm{ \mathrm{T}=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ \mathrm{p}=1.9 \mathrm{~atm}=1.9 \times 1.01325 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}}$
Now, work done during change in volume against constant presusre is
$\rm{ \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{p}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right) \\ =-1.9 \times 1.01325 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2} \\ \quad\left(25 \times 10^{-4}-3 \times 10^{-4}\right) \mathrm{m}^{3} \\ =-1.925 \times 10^{5}\left(22 \times 10^{-4}\right) \mathrm{Nm} \\ \Rightarrow-423.56 \mathrm{Nm}=-423.56 \mathrm{~J} \\ \qquad(\because 1 \mathrm{Nm}=1 \mathrm{~J})}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Work done when 2 mole of an ideal gas is compressed from a volume of $\mathrm{5 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ to $\mathrm{2.5 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$, under a pressure of ${\text{100}}\,{\text{k}}\,{\text{pa}}$ is
explanation:
Given,
Number of moles $\mathrm{(\mathrm{n})=2}$
Volume $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)=5 \mathrm{~m}^{3}=5000 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
Volume $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}\right)=2.5 \mathrm{~m}^{3}=2500 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
Pressure $\mathrm{(p)=100 \mathrm{kPa}}$
We havve, work done $\mathrm{(\mathrm{W})=-\mathrm{p}_{\text {ext }} \cdot \mathrm{dV}}$
$\rm{ =-100 \mathrm{kPa}(2500-5000) \\ =-100 \times-2500 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \\ =250000 \mathrm{~J}=250 \mathrm{~kJ} \simeq 248.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A gas expands from a volume of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ to a volume of $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ against an external pressure of $\mathrm{10^{5} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}}$. The work done by the gas will be
explanation:
Work done $\mathrm{(\mathrm{w})=\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P} \times\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)}$
$\rm{=10^{5} \times(2-1)=10^{5} \mathrm{~J} \text { or } 10^{2} \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: When $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of a gas is heated at constant volume, temperature is raised from 298 to 308 $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}}$. If heat supplied to the gas is $\mathrm{500 \mathrm{~J}}$, then which statement is correct?
explanation:
As volume is constant hence work done in this process is zero therefore heat supplied is equal to change in internal energy.
$\therefore \,\,{\rm{\Delta V}}\,\,{\rm{ = 0;}}\,\,{\rm{w = 0}}$
According to ${{\rm{1}}^{{\rm{st}}}}$ law of thermodynamics,
${\rm{\Delta U = q + w = q = 500}}\,{\rm{J}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Which of the following is the correct option for the expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition?
explanation:
For an adiabatic condition, $\mathrm{q=0}$
During expansion, $\mathrm{\Delta V>0}$ hence, $\mathrm{w=-p \Delta V < 0, w \neq 0}$
According to $\mathrm{1^{\text {st }}}$ of thermodynamics,
$\mathrm{\Delta U=q+w=w}$
As $\mathrm{w < 0, \Delta U < 0}$ i.e., $\mathrm{\Delta T < 0}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Based on first law of thermodynamics which of the following is correct ?
explanation:
In isobaric process, pressure is constant and volume increases from $\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{1}}$ to $\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{2}}$ due to the absorption of heat. According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}}$
At constant pressure, $\mathrm{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}-\mathrm{W}}$
${{\rm{q}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = }}\Delta {\rm{U - p}}\Delta {\rm{V}}$
${{\rm{q}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{U}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{U}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ - }}\left( {{\rm{ - p}}\left( {{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)} \right)$
${{\rm{q}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = }}\left( {{{\rm{U}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + p}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right){\rm{ - }}\left( {{{\rm{U}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + p}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)$
${{\rm{q}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{1}}}$
${{\rm{q}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = }}\Delta {\rm{H}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
As we know, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{p} \Delta \mathrm{V}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W}}$
Putting the value of $\Delta {\rm{H}}\,\,in\,\,(1)$
$\rm{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W}}$
Other corrected options are as follows:
$ \bullet $ For adibatic process, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{W}}$
$ \bullet $ For an isochoric process, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{v}}}$
$ \bullet $ For an isothermal process $\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=-\mathrm{W}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Which among the following equations represents the first law of thermodynamics under isobaric conditions?
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}}$. In case of isobaric conditions, change of state is brought about at constant pressure, i.e. $\mathrm{d p=0}$. Moreover, work done for a process is given as $\mathrm{\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{p}_{\text {ext. }} \Delta \mathrm{V}}$.
Substituting the value of ' $\mathrm{W}$ ' in first law of thermodynamics
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ext} .} \cdot \Delta \mathrm{V}}$
So, option (1) is correct.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: "The mass and energy both are conserved in an isolated system", is the statement of
explanation: "The mass and energy both are conserved in an isolated system", is the statement of modified first law of thermodynamics. According to this law, if a certain amount of one kind of energy is produced, an equal amount of some other kind of energy is consumed so that the total energy in the universe remains constant.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Two moles of an ideal gas are allowed to expand from a volume of $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ to $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ against a pressure of ${\text{101}}.{\text{325}}\,{\text{k}}\,{\text{Pa}}$. Calculate the work done
explanation:
Given,
Number of moles $\mathrm{(\mathrm{n})=2}$
Initial volume $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)=10 \mathrm{dm}^{3}=0.01 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$
$\mathrm{\left(\because 1 \mathrm{dm}^{3}=\dfrac{1}{1000} \mathrm{~m}^{3}\right)}$
Final volume $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}\right)=2 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ext}}=101.325 \mathrm{kPa}}$
Temperature $\mathrm{(\mathrm{T})=300 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm{\because}$ Work done due to change in volume against a constant pressure is given by
$\rm{ \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{p}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right) \\ \therefore \mathrm{W}=-101.325 \mathrm{kPa} \times(2-0.01) \mathrm{m}^{3} \\=-201.636 \mathrm{kPa} \mathrm{m}^{3} \text { or }-201.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \\\left(\because 1 \mathrm{~kJ}=1 \mathrm{kPam}^{3}\right)}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Given $\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, the work done during combustion of $\mathrm{0.090 \mathrm{~kg}}$ of ethane (molar mass $\mathrm{=30}$ ) at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ is
explanation:
Since, combustion takes place at constant pressure, hence $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=3 \mathrm{~W}}$
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{H} =\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{dT} \\\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~W}=\dfrac{0.090}{30} \times \dfrac{5}{2} \times 8.314 \times 300 \\ =18.7 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: What is the amount of work done when 0.5 mole of methane, $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})}$, is subjected to combustion at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ ? (Given, $\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ )
explanation:
$\rm{ 0.5 \mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 0.5 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \\ \Delta \mathrm{n}=0.5-1.5=-1}$
Work done
$\rm{=-\Delta \mathrm{nRT}=-(1)^{*} 8.314 \times 30=2494 \mathrm{~J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A gas performs $\mathrm{0.320 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ work on surrounding and absorbs $\mathrm{120 \mathrm{~J}}$ of heat from the surrounding. Hence, change in internal energy is
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}}$
Since, work is done on the surrounding.
So, $\mathrm{\quad \mathrm{W}=-0.320 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
${\rm{ = - 320}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{q = 120}}\,{\rm{J}}$
$\therefore \Delta {\rm{U = - 320 + 120 = - 200}}\,{\rm{J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: What is the amount of work done when two moles of ideal gas is compressed from a volume of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$ to $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ against a pressure of ${\rm{100}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{Pa}}$ ?
explanation:
Given, number of moles $\mathrm{=2}$ moles
$\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{1}=1 \mathrm{~m}^{3}=1000 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{2}=10 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ We have, work done $\mathrm{(\mathrm{W})=-\mathrm{p}_{\text {ext }} \mathrm{dV}}$
$\mathrm{=-100 \mathrm{kPa} \times(10-1000) \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
$\rm{ =-100 \mathrm{kPa} \times-999 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \\ =99900 \mathrm{~J} \\ =99.9 \mathrm{~J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Three moles of an ideal gas expanded spontaneously into vacuum. The work done will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{w=P \times \Delta V}$
$\rm{=0 \times \Delta V=0}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Which among the following is a feature of adiabatic expansion?
explanation:
For adiabatic expansion, $\mathrm{q=0}$
For expansion, $\mathrm{\Delta V>0}$, hence, the work done, $\mathrm{w < 0}$
According to $\mathrm{1^{\text {st }}}$ law of thermodynamics,
$\mathrm{\Delta U=q+w}$
$\mathrm{\Delta U < 0}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Mathematical equation of first law of thermodynamics for isochoric process is
explanation:
For an isochoric process, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{V}=0}$
In this process, the first law of thermodynamics
$\rm{ \mathrm{q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}-\mathrm{W} \\ \mathrm{q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}-\mathrm{p} \Delta \mathrm{V} \\ \text { As } \Delta \mathrm{V}=0 \\ \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{v}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: The first law of thermodynamics for isothermal process is
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}}$
where, $\mathrm{\Delta U=}$ Internal energy
$\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=}$ Heat; $\mathrm{\mathrm{w}=}$ Work done
For isothermal process, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{T}=0, \Delta \mathrm{U}=0}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{q}=-\mathrm{W}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: Which of the following $\mathrm{\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{V}}$ curve represents maximum work done?
explanation:
In $\mathrm{\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{V}}$ graph, area under the curve represent magnitude of work.
It is maximum in graph-1, So, correct answer is (1).
subject: Chemistry

question: The correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition is.
explanation:
For free expansion of ideal gas,
$\rm{P_{e x t}=0}$
$\rm{ w_{p v}=0 \\ q=0 \text { (adiabatic process }) \\ \Delta E=q+w \\ \Delta E=0 \\ \Delta E=n C_{v} \Delta T=0 \\ q=0, \Delta T=0, w=0}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done During Reversible and Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion or Compression Process of an Ideal Gas

question:

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
An ideal gas undergoes a cyclic pocess as shown in Figure
$\Delta {{\text{U}}_{{\text{BC}}}}{\text{ = - 5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{J}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{,}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{q}}_{{\text{AB}}}}{\text{ = 2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{J}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$
${{\text{W}}_{{\text{AB}}}}{\text{ = - 5}}\,{\text{k}}\,{\text{J}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{,}}\,{{\text{W}}_{{\text{CA}}}}{\text{ = 3}}\,{\text{k}}\,{\text{J}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$
Heat absorbed by the system during process CA is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959019/preptracker/questions/q_16961_explanation_1764959019004.png $\mathrm{A B \rightarrow}$ isobaric
$\mathrm{B C \rightarrow}$ Isochoric
$\mathrm{C A \rightarrow}$ not defined
$\mathrm{\Delta U_{A B}=q+W}$
$\mathrm{=2-5=-3}$

question: A gas undergoes change from state A to state B. In this process, the heat absorbed and work done by the gas is $\mathrm{5 \mathrm{~J}}$ and $\mathrm{8 \mathrm{~J}}$, respectively. Now gas is brought back to A by another process during which $\mathrm{3 \mathrm{~J}}$ of heat is evolved. In this reverse process of $\mathrm{\mathrm{B}}$ to $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}}$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959014/preptracker/questions/q_16949_explanation_1764959014468.png ${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{U}}_{{\rm{AB}}}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{q + w = + 5 + ( - 8) = - 3}}$
$\rm{q=-3, \Delta U_{A B}=+3}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow 3=-3+w \Rightarrow w=+6}$ (work done on the system).

options.text: $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~J}}$ of the work will be done by the gas.
$\mathrm{6 \mathrm{~J}}$ of the work will be done by the gas.
$\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~J}}$ of the work will be done by the surrounding on gas.
$\mathrm{6 \mathrm{~J}}$ of the work will be done by the surrounding on gas
