question:
If 100 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}$ decomposes at 1 bar and $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$, then the wok done ${\rm{(in}}\,\,{\rm{kJ)}}$ by one mole of $\mathrm{O_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$ as it expands against 1 bar pressure is:
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)}$
$\left( {{\rm{R = 8}}{\rm{.3}}\,\,{\rm{J}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(l) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)}$
$\rm{w=-P_{e x t}(\Delta V)=n_{O_{2}} \quad R T}$
$\mathrm{\because 100}$ mole $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}$ on decomposition gives 50 mole $\mathrm{O_{2}}$.
$\mathrm{\therefore w=-(50)(8.3)(300)=-124500 \mathrm{~J}=-124.5 \mathrm{~kJ}}$.
subject: Chemistry

For a system of constant volume heat involved directly changes to internal energy.

PV represents work done at constant temperature.
so, $\mathrm{\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{R}}$.

question: According to the first law of the thermodynamics, $\mathrm{\Delta U=q+w}$. In special cases the statement can be expressed in different ways. Which of the following is not a correct expression?
explanation:
Acc $\mathrm{1^{\text {st }}}$ law of thermodynamics
$\Delta {\rm{U = q + w}}$
$\Delta {\rm{U = q - P}}\Delta {\rm{V}}\,\,\left( {{\rm{w = - P}}\Delta {\rm{V}}} \right)$
Hence, option (3) is incorrect.

options.text: When no work is done, $\mathrm{\Delta U=q}$
When work is done by the system : $\mathrm{\Delta U=q+w}$

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: For an ideal gas, the work of reversible expansion under isothermal condition can be calculated by using the expression, $\mathrm{\mathrm{w}=-n \mathrm{RT} \ln \dfrac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}}$. A sample containing $\mathrm{1.0 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of an ideal gas is expanded isothermally and reversibly to ten times of its original volume, in two separate experiments. The expansion is carried out at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ and at $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~K}}$ respectively. Choose the correct option.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{600 \mathrm{~K}}}{\mathrm{w}_{300 \mathrm{~K}}}=\dfrac{1 \times \mathrm{R} \times 600 \mathrm{~K} \ln \dfrac{10}{1}}{1 \times \mathrm{R} \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \ln \dfrac{10}{1}}=\dfrac{600}{300}=2}$
For isothermal expansion of ideal gases,
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}=0}$ since temperature is constant this means there is no change in internal energy.

options.text: Work done at $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~K}}$ is 20 times the work done at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$.
Work done at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$ is twice the work done at $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~K}}$.
Work done at $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~K}}$ is twice the work done at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question:
The work done in an open vessel at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$, when $\mathrm{112 \mathrm{~g}}$ iron reacts with dil. $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ is
${\rm{(R = 2}}\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,}}{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{)}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{n_{F e}=\dfrac{112}{56}=2 \mathrm{~mol}}$
$\rm{\mathrm{Fe}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeCL}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol} \quad 1 \mathrm{~mol}}$
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~mol} \quad 2 \mathrm{~mol}}$
$\mathrm{V_{2}=V_{H_{2}}=2 ; V_{1}=0}$ (for solid $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}}$ )
replace all $\mathrm{\mathrm{v}}$ with $\mathrm{\mathrm{V}}$ in the solution
$\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{2}=\mathrm{V}_{H_{2}}=2 ; \mathrm{V}_{1}=0}$
$\mathrm{w=-P_{e x t}\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)}$
$\mathrm{=-n_{H_{2}} R T}$
$\mathrm{=-2 \times 2 \times 300}$
${\rm{ = - 1200}}\,{\rm{cal}}$ (expansion)
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: The gaseous mixture containing 2 mole of each of two ideal gases $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}, \mathrm{m}}=\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}\right)}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}, \mathrm{m}}=\dfrac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}\right)}$. Find out the average molar heat capacity at constant volume.
explanation:
Average $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}_{1}}+\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}_{2}}}{n_{1}+n_{2}}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{2 \times \dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}+2 \times \dfrac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}}{2+2}=2 \mathrm{R} .}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: 20 moles of an ideal gas expand isothermally and reversibly from a pressure of $\mathrm{5 \mathrm{~atm}}$ to 1 atm at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$. Calculate the largest mass that can be lifted through a height of 1 metre by this expansion.
explanation:
${{\rm{w}}_{{\rm{exp}}}}{\rm{ = - 2}}{\rm{.303}}\,{\rm{n}}\,{\rm{RT}}\,{\rm{log}}\frac{{{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{2}}}}}$
${\rm{ = - 2}}{\rm{.303 \times 20 \times 8}}{\rm{.314}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ \times 300\,K}}\,{\rm{log}}\frac{{\rm{5}}}{{\rm{1}}}$
${\rm{ = 80}}{\rm{.42 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{\,J}}$
Work done $\mathrm{=\mathrm{mgh}}$
$\rm{\begin{gathered}80.15 \times 10^{3}=\mathrm{m} \times 9.81 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2} \times 1 \mathrm{~m} \\\mathrm{~m}=\dfrac{80.15 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-2} \times 1}{9.81 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2} \times 1 \mathrm{~m}} \\=8205.23 \mathrm{~kg} .\end{gathered}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: What is the change in internal energy when a gas contracts from $\mathrm{877 \mathrm{~mL}}$ to $\mathrm{177 \mathrm{~mL}}$ under a constant pressure of 1520 torr, while at the same time being cooled by removing $\mathrm{124 \mathrm{~J}}$ heat ? ( 1 $\mathrm{\mathrm{L}}$ atom $\mathrm{=100 \mathrm{~J}}$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta U=q+w}$
$\mathrm{q=-124 J}$
$\mathrm{w=-P\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)=\dfrac{-1520}{760}(177-377)=40 \mathrm{~J}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta U=-124+40=-84 J}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: The change in internal energy of a system when no heat is absorbed by the system from the surroundings, but work $\mathrm{(\mathrm{w})}$ is done on the system is
explanation:
The process in which no heat is absorbed by the system from the surroundings known as adiabatic process i.e., $\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=0}$.
According to 1st law of thermodynamics,
$\rm{\Delta U=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w}}$
Upon substituting $\mathrm{\mathrm{q}=0}$, we get
$\rm{\Delta U=\mathrm{w}_{\text {adiabatic }}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{w}}$ is +ve sign as work is done on the system.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: The work done and the change in internal energy when 1 litre of ideal gas expands isothermally into vacuum until its total volume is 5 litres is, respectively
explanation:
$\mathrm{(-w)=p_{\text {ext }}\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)=0 \times(5-1)=0}$ For isothermal expansion, $\mathrm{q=0}$
By first law of thermodynamics
$\mathrm{q=\Delta U+(-w)}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow 0=\Delta U+0}$ so $\mathrm{\Delta U=0}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A system absorbs $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat and does $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of work. The internal energy of the system
explanation:
$\mathrm{q=+10 k J, w=-4 k J}$
$\mathrm{\Delta U=q+w=10-4=6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: What will be the work done on an ideal gas enclosed in a cylinder, when it is compressed by a constant external pressure, $\mathrm{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}}}$ in a single step as shown in fig?
explanation:
Cylinder, which contains one mole of an ideal gas fitted with a frictionless piston. Total volume of the gas $\mathrm{V_{i}}$ and pressure of the gas inside is $\mathrm{p}$. If external pressure is $\mathrm{p_{\text {ext }}}$ which is greater than $\mathrm{p}$, piston is moved inward till the pressure inside becomes equal to $\mathrm{p_{\text {ext }}}$. This change is achieved in a single step and the final volume be $\mathrm{V_{f}}$. During this compression, suppose piston moves a distance and its cross-sectional area of the piston is $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}}$. Then
Volume change $\mathrm{=l \times A=\Delta V=V_{f}-V_{i}}$
Pressure $\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { force }}{\text { area }}}$
Force on the piston $\mathrm{=p . A}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{W}}$ is the work done on the system by movement of the piston then
$\rm{ W=\text { Force } \times \text { distance }=p_{\text {ext }} \text {.A.l } \\ =p_{\text {ext }}(-\Delta V)=-p_{\text {ext }}\left(V_{f}-V_{i}\right)}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done Analysis Using PV Graph

question: The pressure-volume work for an ideal gas can be calculated by using the expression ${\rm{w =  - }}\int_{{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{i}}}}^{{{\rm{v}}_{\rm{f}}}} {{{\rm{p}}_{{\rm{ex}}}}} {\rm{dV}}$. The work can also be calculated from the $\mathrm{p \mathrm{~V}}$ - plot by using the area under the curve within the specified limits. When an ideal gas is compressed (A) reversibly or (B) irreversibly from volume $\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{i}}$ to $\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{f}}$ [Here, $\mathrm{P_{e x}}$ at each stage is equal to $\left[ {{\rm{V}}\left( {{{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{in }}}}{\rm{ + dp}}} \right)} \right]$. Choose the correct option.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{w}}$ (reversible) $\mathrm{ < \mathrm{w}}$ (irreversible)
Area under the curve is always more in irreversible compression.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done Analysis Using PV Graph

question: A gas expands isothermally and reversibly. The work done by the gas is
explanation: Work done in isothermal reversible expansion is the maximum work.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: A gas absorbs $\mathrm{250 \mathrm{~J}}$ of heat and expands from 1 litre to 10 litre at constant temperature against external pressure of $\mathrm{0.5 \mathrm{~atm}}$. The values of $\mathrm{\mathrm{w}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{U}}$ will be, respectively.
explanation:
$\mathrm{q=250 \mathrm{~J}}$
$\rm{ w=-P\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right) \\ =-0.5(10-1)=4.5 \mathrm{Latm} \\ =-4.5 \times 101 \mathrm{~J}=-455 \mathrm{~J} \\ \Delta U=q+w=250-455=-205 \mathrm{~J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: In a process, $\mathrm{701 \mathrm{~J}}$ of heat is absorbed by a system and $\mathrm{394 \mathrm{~J}}$ of work is done by the system. The change in internal energy for the process is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{q=+701 J}$ (heat is absorbed, hence $\mathrm{q}$ is positive)
$\mathrm{w=-394 J}$ (work is done by the system, hence $\mathrm{w}$ is negative)
By first law of thermodynamics;
Internal energy change,
$\rm{ \Delta U=q+w \\ =+701 \mathrm{~J}+(-394 \mathrm{~J})=+307 \mathrm{~J}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Statements and Mathematical Expressions of First Law for Different Processes

question: An ideal gas is allowed to expand against a constant pressure of 2 bar from $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~L}}$ to $\mathrm{50 \mathrm{~L}}$ in one step. Calculate the amount of work done by the gas. (Given that $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~L}}$ bar $\mathrm{=100 \mathrm{~J}}$ )
explanation:
$\rm{w=-p_{e x}\left(V_{f}-V_{i}\right)=-2 \times 40=-80 \mathrm{~L} \text { bar }=-8 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
The negative sign shows that work is done by the system on the surrounding.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: The work done in ergs for the reversible expansion of one mole of an ideal gas from a volume of 10 litres to 20 litres at $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{w=-2.303 n R T \log \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}}$
$\rm{ =-2.303 \times 1 \times 8.31 \times 10^{7} \times 298 \log \dfrac{20}{10} \\ =-298 \times 10^{7} \times 8.31 \times 2.303 \log 2}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Reversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: Two litres of an ideal gas at a pressure of 10 atm expands isothermally into a vacuum until its total volume is 10 litres. How much work is done in the expansion?
explanation:
We have $\mathrm{w=-p_{e x}(10-2)=0(8)=0}$
No work is done, since, external pressure is zero.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work Done or Heat Changes During Irreversible Isothermal Expansion or Compression of an Ideal Gas

question: If an ideal gas expansion were carried out both reversibly and irreversibly then
explanation: Work done will be more in the reversible expansion because internal pressure and external pressure are almost same at every step.

question: Match the following columns based on the characteristics related to thermodynamic properties and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A - Q) An exothermic reaction releases heat
(B - P) Negative $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ is indicative of a spontaneous process.
(C - S) At equilibrium $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$.
(D - R) Entropy is a measure of randomness. Correct option is (2).

options.text: A - S,     B - P, 	C - Q, 	D - R
A - Q,    B - P, 	C - S, 	D - R
A - R,     B - S, 	C - P, 	D - Q
A - Q,     B - R, 	C - S, 	D - P

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: If values of  ${\rm{\Delta H^\circ \&  \Delta S^\circ }}$ for a process/reaction are ${\mathrm{77.2 \mathrm{~kJ}, 48 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}}}$ respectively. Then find value of ${\mathrm{\log \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}}}$. Given : Temp is 300 K
explanation:
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}}$
${\mathrm{=77.2-\left(300 \times 48 \times 10^{-3}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{=77.2-14.4}}$
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=62.8 \mathrm{~kJ}}}$
${\mathrm{\Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K}}$
${\mathrm{-\log K=\dfrac{62.8}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 10^{-3} \times 300}}}$
${\mathrm{-\log \mathrm{K}}}$ or ${\mathrm{\log \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}=10.9=11}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: For a hypothetical reaction, ${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}}$ are ${\mathrm{-30 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$ and ${\mathrm{-100 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$ at 300 K . The equilibrium constant for the reaction at 298 K is ____ .
explanation:
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}}$
$ = \left( { - 30} \right) - \left( {300 \times - 100 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)$
$ = - 30 + 30 = 0$
$\Delta {\rm{G}}^\circ {\rm{ = - 2}}.{\rm{303RT}}\log {\rm{K}}$
$\therefore \quad {\rm{0 = - 2}}.{\rm{303RT}}\log {\rm{K}}$
$\therefore \quad \log {\rm{K = 0}}$
${\rm{K}} = {10^0} = 1$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question:
$\mathrm{K}$ for $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $1.754 \times 10^{-5}$. At $50^{\circ} \mathrm{CK}$ is $1.633 \times 10^{-5}$. What are ${\text{\Delta }}{{\text{H}}^{\text{^\circ }}}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$
for the ionisation of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ ?
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: If values of  $\Delta {\text{H}}^\circ {\text{ &   }}\Delta {\text{S}}^\circ $   for a process/reaction are $77.2 \mathrm{~kJ}, 48 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{k}$ respectively. Then find of $\log \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{k}}$. Given : Temp is $300 \mathrm{~K}$
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$
$ = 77.2 - \left( {300 \times 48 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)$
$=77.2-14.4$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=62.8 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ = - 2.303{\text{RT }}\log {\text{k}}$
$=77.2-\left(300 \times 48 \times 10^{-3}\right)$
$=77.2-14.4$
$\begin{aligned}& -\log \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{62.8}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 10^{-3}} \\& =0.0109 \times 10^{3} \\& =10.9 \simeq 11 \\& \log \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{k}}=11\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question:
For a hypothetical reaction:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$, if $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}>0$
explanation: As $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ for the formation of $\mathrm{C}$ is $+\mathrm{ve} ; \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ for the formation of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is -ve. Hence A and $\mathrm{B}$ predominate in the reaction mixture.

options.text: The reaction tends to proceed slowly towards the formation of  ${\text{C}}$.
The increase in temperature increases the yield of ${\text{C}}$.
${\text{A}}$ and  ${\text{B}}$ predominate in the reaction mixture.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: The equilibrium constant of a reaction is 0.008 at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$. The standard free energy change of the reaction at the same temperature is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{o}}=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 218 \log (0.008)}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-5705.84 \times-2.09}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=11964.65 \mathrm{~J}}$ or $\mathrm{11.96 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: For a reversible reaction: $\mathrm{X_{(\mathrm{g})}+3 \mathrm{Y}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Z}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$. $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=-40 \mathrm{~kJ}}$, the standard entropies of $\mathrm{\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Z}}$ are 60, 40 and ${\rm{50}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ respectively. The temperature at which the above reaction attains equilibrium is about
explanation:
$\mathrm{X_{(\mathrm{g})}+3 Y_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 Z_{(\mathrm{g})}}$
$\rm{ \Delta S^{\circ}=2 S^{\circ}(Z)-\left\{S^{\circ}(X)+3 S^{\circ}(Y)\right\} \\ =2 \times 50-\{60+3 \times 40\}=100-180 \\ =-80 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Given $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-40 \mathrm{KJ}=-40,000 \mathrm{~J}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{o}}=\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\mathrm{o}}}$
At equilibrium, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=0}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}$
or $\mathrm{\mathrm{T}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}=\dfrac{40000}{80}=500 \mathrm{~K}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question:
For the reversible reaction:
${{\text{A}}_{{\text{(s)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{B}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{C}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{D}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$ $:\Delta {{\rm{G}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ = - 350}}\,{\rm{kJ}}$
Which one of the following statements is true?
explanation: $\mathrm{\Delta G}$ has a negative value. So, the reaction is feasible in forward direction. So, equilibrium constant will have value greater than one.

options.text: Equilibrium constant is greater than one.
The reaction is thermodynamically not feasible.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question:
Hydrolysis of sucrose is given by the following reaction
Sucrose $\mathrm{+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons}$ Glucose + Fructose
If the equilibrium constant $\mathrm{\left(K_{c}\right)}$ is $\mathrm{2 \times 10^{13}}$ at $\mathrm{300 \mathrm{~K}}$, the value of $\mathrm{(\Delta)_{r} G^{\circ}}$ at the same temperature will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{K_{c}=2 \times 10^{13}}$
$\mathrm{T=300 K}$
$\mathrm{\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_{e q}}$
$\Delta {\rm{G = - 8}}.{\rm{314}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{J}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}} \times {\rm{300}}\,{\rm{K}} \times \ln \left( {{\rm{2}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{13}}}}} \right)$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: Calculate $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} G^{\circ}}$ for conversion of oxygen to ozone, $\mathrm{\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}}$ at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ if $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}$ for this conversion is $\mathrm{2.47 \times 10^{-29}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: At $\mathrm{60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$, dinitrogen tetroxide is fifty percent dissociated. The standard free energy change at this temperature and at one atmosphere is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
If $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}$ is $\mathrm{50 \%}$ dissociated, the mole fraction of both the substances is given by
$\mathrm{X_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}=\dfrac{1-0.5}{1+0.5} ; X_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}=\dfrac{2 \times 0.5}{1+0.5}}$
$\mathrm{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}=\dfrac{0.5}{1.5} \times 1 \mathrm{~atm}, p_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{1.5} \times 1 \mathrm{~atm}}$
The equilibrium constant $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}$ is given by
$\rm{K_{p}=\dfrac{\left(p_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}\right)^{2}}{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}}=\dfrac{1.5}{(1.5)^{2}(0.5)}=1.33 \mathrm{~atm}}$
Since, $\mathrm{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_{p}}$
$\rm{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}= \left(-8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times(333 \mathrm{~K}) \\ \times(2.303) \times(0.1239) \\= -763.8 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}.}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\text{Given}}}&{{\text{S}}_{\text{m}}^{\text{o}}\left[ {{\text{J/}}\left( {{\text{k}}\,{\text{mol}}} \right)} \right]}&{} \\ {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{4(l)}}}}}&{{\text{ - 135}}{\text{.4}}}&{{\text{215}}{\text{.4}}} \\ {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{4(g)}}}}}&{{\text{ - 103}}{\text{.0}}}&{{\text{308}}{\text{.7}}} \end{array}$
What is the boiling point of carbon tetrachloride?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CCl}_{4(g)}}$
$\rm{\begin{array}{lll}\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} & -135.4 & -103.0\end{array}}$
$\rm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-103-(-135.4)=32.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}^{\circ}=308-215.4=93.3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K} \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{T}_{\text {boiling }}=\dfrac{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}=\dfrac{32.4 \times 10^{3}}{93.3}=347.3 \mathrm{~K}=74.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: The incorrect expression among the following is:
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}}$
$\mathrm{-R T \ln K=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}}$
$\mathrm{\ln K=\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}}{R T}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: Standard entropy of $\mathrm{X_{2}, Y_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{X Y_{3}}$ are 60, 40 and $\mathrm{50 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, respectively. For the reaction, $\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} X_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} Y_{2} \rightarrow X Y_{3}, \Delta H=-30 \mathrm{~kJ}}$, to be at equilibrium, the temperature will be
explanation:
For a reaction to be at equilibrium $\mathrm{\Delta G=0}$.
Since $\mathrm{\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S}$ so at equilibrium $\mathrm{\Delta H-T \Delta S=0}$ or
$\mathrm{\Delta H=T \Delta S}$
For the reaction
$\rm{\dfrac{1}{2} X_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} Y_{2} \longrightarrow X Y_{3} ; \quad \Delta H=-30 \mathrm{~kJ} \text { (given) }}$
Calculating $\mathrm{\Delta S}$ for the above reaction, we get
$\rm{ \Delta S=50-\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times 60+\dfrac{3}{2} \times 40\right] J K^{-1} \\ =50-(30+60) J K^{-1}=-40 J K^{-1}}$
At equilibrium, $\mathrm{T \Delta S=\Delta H \quad[\because \Delta G=0]}$
$\rm{\therefore T \times(-40)=-30 \times 1000 \quad[\because 1 k J=1000 J]}$
or $\mathrm{T=\dfrac{-30 \times 1000}{-40}}$ or $\mathrm{750 \mathrm{~K}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question:
Industrial acetylene gas (ethyne, $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}}$ ) is made by the high temperature decomposition of ethane gas, $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}$, at $\mathrm{300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$, according to the following equation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2(g)} ; \Delta_{r} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=310 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
If the value of the thermodynamic equilibrium constant, $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}$, for reaction at $\mathrm{300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ is $\mathrm{1.68 \times 10^{-16}}$ which one of the following statements about this reaction is correct?
explanation:
$\mathrm{-\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\mathrm{RT} \ln \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=173 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ} < \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}^{\circ}>0}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: For a particular reversible reaction at temperature T, $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta S}$ were found to be both +ve. If $\mathrm{T_{e}}$ is the temperature at equilibrium, the reaction would be spontaneous when
explanation:
At equilibrium $\mathrm{\Delta G=0}$
Hence, $\mathrm{\Delta G=\Delta H-T_{e} \Delta S=0}$
$\rm{\therefore \quad \Delta H=T_{e} \Delta S \text { or } T_{e}=\dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}}$
For a spontaneous reaction
$\mathrm{\Delta G}$ must be negative which is possible only if
$\rm{\Delta H < T \Delta S \text { or } T < \dfrac{\Delta H}{S} ; \quad T_{e} < T}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: Which quantity out of $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}}$ will be zero at equilibrium?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta_{r} G}$ will always be zero
$\mathrm{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}}$ is zero for $\mathrm{K=1 \quad \because \Delta G^{o}=-R T \ln K}$
$\mathrm{\Delta G^{\circ}}$ will be non-zero for other values of $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}}$.

options.text: Both $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}}$ are zero at equilibrium

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

explanation: The reaction is spontaneous $\mathrm{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_{p}}$. Hence, $\mathrm{\Delta G}$ will be negative.

question:
Find out the value of equilibrium constant for the following reaction at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$.
$\rm{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{CONH}_{2(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})}}$
Standard Gibb's energy change, $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}}$ at the given temperature is $\mathrm{-13.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation:
We know, $\mathrm{\log K=\dfrac{-\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{\left(-13.6 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1}\right)}{2.303\left(8.314 J \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)(298 \mathrm{~K})}=2.38}$
Hence, $\mathrm{K=\operatorname{antilog} 2.38=2.4 \times 10^{2}=240}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

question: Gibbs energy change $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}}$ is related to equilibrium constant ' $\mathrm{K}$.' as
explanation:
The relationship between $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}, \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}}$ and Reaction quotient, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Q}}$ is as follows:
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{RT} \operatorname{In} \mathrm{Q}}$
At equilibrium, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{K}, \Delta \mathrm{G}=0}$
Upon substitution, we get
$\mathrm{0=\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}+\mathrm{RT} \operatorname{InK}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{RT} \operatorname{InK}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gibbs Energy Change and Equilibrium

The Gibbs energy change ( $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ ) is negative for endothermic reactions at high temperatures.

question: For the reaction at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{XO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$, $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ are 2.1 k cal and $20 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{K}$ respectively. What is  $\Delta {\text{G}}$  for the reaction at the same temperature?$\left(\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{calK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(l) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{XO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=2.1 \mathrm{kcal}, \Delta \mathrm{n}=2-0=2$
$\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{calK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Using formula,
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{RT}=2.1+[(2-0) \times 2 \times 298]$
$=2.1 \times 10^{3}+(2 \times 2 \times 298)=3292 \mathrm{cal}$
Gibbs energy change can be calculated as
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}=3292-(298 \times 20)$
$=-2668 \mathrm{cal}=-2.67 \mathrm{kcal}$
subject: Chemistry

subtopic: Gibbs Helmholtz Equation and Effect of Temperature on Spontaneity

question: Which of the following relation is correct?
explanation:
The Gibbs function is defined in terms of temperature (T), enthalpy (H), and entropy (S) as
${\text{G}} = {\text{H}} - {\text{TS}}$
In a thermodynamic process with no composition change, change in Gibbs energy is
${\text{dG}} = {\text{dH}} - {\text{TdS}} - {\text{SdT}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Using the combined first and second laws
$\mathrm{dU}=\mathrm{TdS}-\mathrm{pdV}$ and $\mathrm{dH}=\mathrm{TdS}+\mathrm{Vdp}$ in (1) gives
$\mathrm{dG}=\mathrm{Vdp}-\mathrm{SdT}$

question: $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ for a reaction are $+30.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $0.06 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ at 1 atm pressure. The temperature at which free energy change is equal to zero and nature of the reaction below this temperature are
explanation:
When $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0, \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$
${\rm{T}} = \frac{{\Delta {\rm{H}}}}{{\Delta {\rm{S}}}} = \frac{{30}}{{0.06}} = 500\,{\rm{K}} = 227^\circ {\rm{C}}$
Below this temperature, $\Delta \mathrm{G}=+\mathrm{ve}$ and process is non - spontaneous.

question:
Predict the feasibility of the given reactions in aqueous solution
(i) $\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{2-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{2+}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, is amphoteric, so dissolves both in acid and bases.
$\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \mathrm{HCl}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Be}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
$\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]$
or $\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \stackrel{\ominus}{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{2-}$
Hence, both reactions are feasible.

question: $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ in Joules for the conversion of $0.9 \mathrm{~g}$ of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ to steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure is ____ .
explanation:
The process may be broken into two steps:
Step I: water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm} \rightarrow$ steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$;
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=\mathrm{nRT} \ln \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}}$
Hence, $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}+\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}=0+(-107.5)=-107.5 \mathrm{~J}$

question: Which of the following conditions lead to a spontaneous process?
explanation:
For a spontaneous process, ${\rm{\Delta G}}$ should be negative, ${\rm{\Delta G}} = {\rm{\Delta H}} - {\rm{T\Delta S}}$.
Thus, for a spontaneous process ${\rm{(\Delta H}} - {\rm{T\Delta S)}}$ should be negative which is possible only in option (4) where ${\rm{\Delta H}}$ and ${\rm{T\Delta S}}$ both are positive but ${\rm{T\Delta S}} > {\rm{\Delta H}}$ resulting in negative ${\rm{\Delta G}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: When the heat of reaction at constant pressure is $-2.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{cal}$ and entropy change is 7.4 caldeg ${ }^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the reaction is predicted as
explanation:
For the reaction at constant pressure,$\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 2.5 \times {10^3}{\rm{cal}}$
${\rm{\Delta S}} = 7.4\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{de}}{{\rm{g}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
$\therefore \Delta {\rm{G}} = \Delta {\rm{H}} - {\rm{T}}\Delta {\rm{S}}$
$ = ( - )2.5 \times {10^3} - 298 \times 7.4$
$ = ( - )2.5 \times {10^3} - 2205.2\Delta {\rm{G}}$
${\rm{\Delta G}} = - 4705 \cdot 2\,{\rm{cal}}$
$\therefore$ Reaction is spontaneous.

question:
Which of the following relations are correct?
(A) $\Delta {\text{U}} = {\text{q}} + {\text{p}}\,\Delta \,{\text{V}}$
(B) $\Delta {\text{G}} = \Delta {\text{H}} - {\text{T}}\,\Delta \,{\text{S}}$
(C) $\Delta {\text{S}} = \frac{{{{\text{q}}_{{\text{rev}}}}}}{{\text{T}}}$
(D) $\Delta {\text{H}} = \Delta {\text{U}} - \Delta \,{\text{n}}\,{\text{RT}}$
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics
$\Delta {\text{U}} = {\text{q}} + {\text{w}}$ and ${\text{w}} = - {\text{P}}\,\Delta {\text{V}}$,
So, $\Delta {\text{U}} = {\text{q}} - {\text{P}}\,\Delta {\text{V}}$,
$\Delta {\text{H}} = \Delta {\text{U}} + \Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}{\text{RT}}$
$\Delta {\text{S}} = \frac{{{{\text{q}}_{{\text{rev}}}}}}{{\text{T}}}$,
$\Delta {\text{G}} = \Delta {\text{H}} - {\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}}$

question:
Assertion: Many endothermic reactions that are not spontaneous at room temperature become spontaneous at high temperature


Reason: Entropy of the system decreases with inerease in temperature.
explanation: Assertion is true but reason is false. The factor ${\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}}$ increase with increase in temperature. When $\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ is more than $\Delta \mathrm{H}$, $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ will become -ve and process become spontaneous and entropy is directly propotional to temperature. So, option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Salt such as $\mathrm{NaCl}$ dissolves, the $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions leaving the crystal lattice acquire far greater freedom.

Reason: In thermodyanamic terms, the formation of solution occurs with a favourable change in free energy, i.e., $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ has high positive value and ${\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}}$ has a low negative value.
explanation: In thermodynamic terms, the formation of a solution is associated with a favorable change in free energy, $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$, where $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ has a small positive value and $\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ has a large positive value. As a result, $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ becomes negative. So, option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: An exothermic process, non-spontaneous at high temperature, may become spontaneous at low temperature.

Reason: With decrease in temperature. randomness (entropy) increases.
explanation: An exothermic reaction can shift from non-spontaneous to spontaneous at lower temperatures due to the reduction in the product of temperature and entropy change $(\Delta \mathrm{S})$, making the Gibbs free energy change $(\Delta \mathrm{G})$ negative and overcoming the enthalpy change $(\Delta \mathrm{H})$.Entropy decreases with decrease in temperature. Therefore, option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Reason: The change in free energy is related to the change in enthalpy and change in entropy. The change in entropy for a process must always be positive if it is spontaneous.
explanation: The change in entropy can have either a positive or a negative value. So, option (3) is correct.

question: For a certain process, $\Delta \mathrm{H}=178 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}=160 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$. What is the minimum temperature at which the process is spontaneous (assuming that $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ do not vary with temperature?
explanation:
To be at equilibrium,
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$ and
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$
So, $\Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$
$\mathrm{T}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\Delta \mathrm{S}}=\dfrac{178000}{160}=1112.5 \mathrm{~K}$
So the process to be spontaneous, temperature should be more than 1112.5 K (in order to maintain $\Delta \mathrm{G}-\mathrm{ve})$ i.e., 2112.3 K

question:
Considering the reaction:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
the signs of $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}, \Delta \mathrm{S}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}}$ respectively are
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=-, \Delta \mathrm{S}=+, \Delta \mathrm{G}=-}$
Its an exothermic reaction. Therefore $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{ve}}$ as energy is released during an exothermic reaction. As its a spontaneous reaction, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}=-\mathrm{ve}}$. Also entropy or randomness increases while moving from LHS to RHS. Therefore $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}=+\mathrm{ve}}$.

Since $\mathrm{\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S}$
$\rm{=-v e-(+v e)=-v e}$

explanation:

$\rm{\Delta H>T \Delta S}$
Thus the reaction is exotherms and rate of reaction increases with decreases of temperature.

question: Calculate the change in molar Gibb's energy of carbon dioxide gas at $\mathrm{20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ when it is isothermally expanded from 1.0 to ${\rm{2}}{\rm{.0}}\,{\rm{bar}}$
explanation:
For an isothermal process : $\mathrm{d G=V d P}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{G}=\int \mathrm{VdP}=\int \dfrac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{P}} \cdot \mathrm{dP}}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{G}=\mathrm{nRT} \ln \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}}=1 \times 8.314 \times 293 \ln \dfrac{2}{1}}$
$\rm{=1.7 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \text {. }}$

$\mathrm{\Delta S_{\text {total }}=4010-217=3793}$ or $\mathrm{3.79 \times 10^{3}}$ ${\rm{J}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$

question: For oxidation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}, 4 \mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{s})}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}}$ entropy change is $\mathrm{-549.4 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$. The reaction is $\mathrm{\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\right.}$ for this reaction is $\mathrm{1648 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J} \quad \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ )
explanation:
For spontaneous process, $\mathrm{\Delta G=-\mathrm{v} e}$,
$\rm{\Delta G= \Delta H-T \Delta S \\= -1648 \times 10^{3}-298 \times(-549.4) \\ =-1484.278 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question: Given that $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=0}$ for mixing of two gases. The diffusion of these gases into each other in a closed container is
explanation: It is spontaneous process. Although enthalpy change is zero but randomness or disorder (i.e., $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{S}}$ ) increases. Therefore, in equation $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}}$, the term $\mathrm{\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}}$ will be negative. Hence, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{G}}$ will be negative.

question:
$\mathrm{\Delta G=-177 \mathrm{kcal}}$ for $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}}$ and $\mathrm{\Delta G=-19 \mathrm{kcal}}$ for $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}+\mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{s})}}$ $\mathrm{\rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~s})}}$
what is the Gibb's free energy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}}$ ?
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3(S)} ; \Delta G=-177 \mathrm{kcal}}$
(ii) $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3(s)}+\mathrm{Fe}_{(s)} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4(s)} \quad \Delta G=-19 \mathrm{kcal}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta G_{r}=3 \Delta G_{f}\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)-4 \Delta G_{f}(\mathrm{Fe}, S)}$
$\mathrm{3 \Delta G_{f}\left(F_{3} O_{4}, S\right)=-19+4 \times(-177)}$
$\mathrm{3 \Delta G_{r}\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}, S\right)=-\dfrac{727}{3}=-242.4 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: Gibbs Free Energy and Standard Free Energy Change
subtopic: Characteristics and Physical Significance of Gibb’s Free Energy
parent_topic: Gibbs Free Energy and Standard Free Energy Change

question:
If the bond energies of $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Br}-\mathrm{Br}$ and
$\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Br}$ are 433, 192 and $364 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
respectively, then $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ for the reaction :
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Br}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HBr}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ is
explanation:
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=\sum$ B.E. $_{._{(\text {Rectan ts })}}-\sum$ B.E. $_{._{(\text {products })}}$
$\begin{aligned}=\left[\mathrm{B}_{\cdot \mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}}\right. & \left.+\mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\cdot\left(\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right)}\right]-\left[2 \mathrm{~B} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\cdot(\mathrm{HBr})}\right] \\& =[433+192-2 \times 364] \mathrm{kJ}=-103 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question:
In the reaction, $\mathrm{S}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{x kJ}$ and
$\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{3}+\mathrm{ykJ}$
heat of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is
explanation:
${\text{S}} + \frac{3}{2}{{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3} + 2{\text{xkJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad ({\text{1}})$
${\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2} + \frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3} + {\text{ykJ}}\quad \quad \quad \,(2)$
${\text{S}} + {{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {\text{qkJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
Equation (3) can be obtained by subtracting equation (2) from (1)
$ \Rightarrow {\text{q}} = 2{\text{x}} - {\text{ykJ}}$
Enthalpy of formation $\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)=-\mathrm{q}=-(2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})$
$ = {\text{y}} - 2{\text{x}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
The standard enthalpy of formation $\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\right)$ of ammonia is $-46.2 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$. What is the $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ of the following reaction?
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}^\circ = \sum {{\Delta _{\text{r}}}} {\text{H}}^\circ \,\,$ (product) $\,\,\, - \sum {{\Delta _{\text{f}}}} {\text{H}}^\circ \,\,$ (Reactants)
$=\left[2 \times \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]-\left[\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)+3 \Delta_{\mathrm{f}}
\mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)\right]$
$=[2 \times(-46.2)]-[(0.0)+3(0.0)]$
$=-92.4 \mathrm{~kJ}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: The standard enthalpy of combustion of C (graphite), $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{l})$ respectively are $-393,-286$ and $-726 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of methanol?
explanation:
$\mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=$ ?
$\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{C})+\left[2 \times \Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)\right]+$
$\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)\right]-\left[\Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}\right)\right]$
$=(-393)+(2 \times-286)+\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0\right]-[-726]$
$=-239 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
The $\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ of $\mathrm{AO}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{BO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{ABO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$ is$ - 635,\,\,{\rm{x }}\,{\rm{and }}( - 1210\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}})$ respectively.
$\mathrm{ABO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{AO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{BO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ $=175 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
What is the value of $\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{ABO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{AO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{BO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=175$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{AO}}^{\circ}+\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{BO}_{2}}^{\circ}-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}}
\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{ABO}_{3}}^{\circ}$
$175=-635+x-(-1210)$
$175+635=\mathrm{x}+1210$
$\mathrm{x}=-400 \mathrm{KJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: Match the following columns based on the definitions or characteristics of different types of enthalpy of reactions and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:

Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The enthalpy change of vaporization is equal to the enthalpy change of condensation with an opposite sign.
Vaporization and condensation at a given temperature involve the same quantity of heat.
explanation: Statement A is true because the enthalpy change of vaporization is the energy required to vaporize a liquid, while condensation releases this energy, making the two values equal in magnitude but opposite in sign. Statement B is also true as the quantity of heat involved in vaporization and condensation at a given temperature is the same, conforming to the principle of conservation of energy. Correct option is (1)

During a phase change, such as melting or boiling, the temperature of the substance remains constant.
The heat added during a phase change is used to increase the kinetic energy of the molecules of the substance.
explanation: Statement A is true because the temperature of a substance does not change during a phase transition; the heat energy goes into changing the phase, not increasing temperature. Statement B is false because the heat added during a phase change is used to overcome intermolecular forces, not to increase the kinetic energy of the molecules. Correct option is (2)

question: The enthalpies of formation of gaseous $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and NO at 298 K are 82.0 and $90.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The enthalpy change of the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ is
explanation:
Given, $\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$;
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}=82 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{NO}} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{NO})}=90 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Enthalpy of reaction
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ will be
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=2 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{NO})}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}=2 \times 90-82$
$=180-82=98 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ are $-110,-393 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The enthalpy of combustion of CO (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) is
explanation:
Given
$\underset{(\mathrm{S})}{\mathrm{C}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{CO}} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}=-110 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\underset{(\mathrm{s})}{\mathrm{C}}+\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{1}=-393 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
To find enthalpy of combustion of CO .
i.e. $\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{CO}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{CO}}=$ ?
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{CO}}=\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{1}-\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}=-393-(-110)$
$=-283 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
Given,
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$; $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-285 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(l) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-76.6 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l}) ;$ $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-348.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Calculate the $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ of $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})$.
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=-285 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also, $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}_1^\prime = 285 \times 2 = + 570\,{\text{kJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l}) ;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=-76.6 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also, $4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(l) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}_2^\prime = 76.6 \times 2 = + 153.2\,{\text{kJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l}) ;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}=-348.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also, $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+6 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow$
$4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l})$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}_3^\prime = - 348.2 \times 2 = - 696.4\,{\text{kJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
On adding Eqs. (1), (2) and (3), we get
$2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) ;$
$\Delta {\text{H}} = \Delta {\text{H}}_1^\prime + \Delta {\text{H}}_2^\prime + \Delta {\text{H}}_3^\prime $
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=570+153.2-696.4$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=723.2-696.4=26.8 \mathrm{~kJ}$.

question: The bond enthalpies of heavy hydrogen, O-O and $\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{O}$ are $+400,+498$ and $+490 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$, respectively. The $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ of the reaction to produce $\mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is
explanation:
Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ will be
$\mathrm{D}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\sum(\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{E})_{\text {Reactants }}-\sum(\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{E} .)_{\text {products }}$
$=400+\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 498\right)-(490 \times 2)$
$=400+249-980=-331 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: The correct order of enthalpy of vaporisation of noble gases is
explanation:
Enthalpy of vaporisation $\propto$ van der Waals' forces $\propto$ Atomic mass
$\therefore$ The correct order of enthalpy of vaporisation of noble gases is
$\xrightarrow[\substack{ {\text{ Molar mass }} \downarrow \\ {\text{ Enthalpy}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{vaporisation }} \downarrow } ]{{{\text{Xe}}\, > \,{\text{Kr}}\, > {\text{Ar}}\, > \,{\text{Ne}}\, > \,{\text{He}}}}$

question: Enthalpy of hydrogenation of one mole of benzene to cyclohexane is [Resonance energy   of benzene $=-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene $=-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ]
explanation:
The hydrogenation of benzene forms cyclohexane.
$\mathop {{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_6}}\limits_{{\text{ Benzene }}} + 3{{\text{H}}_2} \to \mathop {{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_{12}}}\limits_{{\text{ Cyclohexane }}} $
Enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene
$=-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\therefore$ Calculated enthalpy of benzene
$=3 \times(-119.5)=-358.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Resonance energy of benzene $=-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
As we know,
Actual value of enthalpy = Calculated enthalpy
– Resonance energy
$\therefore$ Actual value of enthalpy
$=(-358.5)-(-150.4)=-208.1 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Hence, the enthalpy of hydrogenation of benzene is $-208.1 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Hydrogenation and Resonance Energy

$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}$ values of graphite, diamond and $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ are $0,1.9$ and $38.1 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$.

question: What is the standard enthalpy of reaction (in kJ ) when two moles of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas to give Fe metal? $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ (s)   and   ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\,(l)$  are  $ - $ 824.2 and $-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
explanation:
$\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {reaction }}=3\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)-\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ} \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$
$=3(-285.83)-(-824.2)=33.29 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
For 2 moles, $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-33.29 \times 2=-66.58 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: The standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$ and $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})$ are respectively $-10,-393,81$ and $-10 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$. Enthalpy change (in kJ) of the following reaction is $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}+3 \mathrm{CO} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fN}_{2} \mathrm{O}}+3 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fCO}_{2}}\right]-\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fN}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}+3 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fCO}}\right]$
$=[81+(3 \times-393)]-[-10+(3 \times-10)]$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=-1058 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
The standard heat of formation, in $\mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ of $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ is :
[Given : standard heat of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ ion $(\mathrm{aq})=-216 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$, standard heat of crystallisation of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})=-4.5 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ standard heat of formation of $\left.\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})=-349 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}\right]$
explanation:
$\mathrm{S}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}{^\circ _{\text{f}}} = - 216\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
${\rm{B}}{{\rm{a}}^{2 + }}({\rm{g}}) + {\rm{SO}}_4^{2 - }({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{BaS}}{{\rm{O}}_4}(\,{\rm{s}})\,\,;$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}{^\circ _{{\rm{crystallisation }}}} = - 4.5{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mol}}\quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
${\rm{Ba}} + {\rm{S}} + 2{{\rm{O}}_2} \to {\rm{BaS}}{{\rm{O}}_4}(\,{\rm{s}});$
$\Delta {\text{H}}{^\circ _{{\text{f}}\left( {{\text{BaS}}{{\text{O}}_4}} \right)}} = - 349{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
$\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}(\mathrm{g})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ba}^{+2}\right)=$ ?
From equation (1), (2) and (3) we get equation (4). Applying equation $(3) - (1) - (2)\,\,;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ba}^{+2}\right)=349-(-4.5)-(-216)$
$=-349+4.5+216$
$=-349+220.5$
$=-128.5 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
Identify the incorrect statements among the following
(a) All enthalpies of fusion are positive
(b) The magnitude of enthalpy change does not depend on the strength of the intermolecular interactions in the substance undergoing phase transformations
(c) When a chemical reaction is reversed, the value of $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ is reversed in sign.
(d) The change in enthalpy is dependent of path between initial state (reactants) and final state (products)
(e) For most of the ionic compounds, $\Delta_{\text {sol }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ is negative
explanation:
The correct statements of b, d and e are as follows
(b) The magnitude of enthalpy change depend on the strength of the intermolecular interactions in the substance undergoing phase transformations.
(d) The change in enthalpy in state function, so, path independent
(e) For ionic compounds, $\Delta_{\text {soln }} \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{ve}$ or $ + \,{\rm{ve}}$

question:

Reason: Enthalpy of neutralisation is the amount of heat liberated when one mole of ${{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ ions furnished by acid combine with one mole of $^ - {\rm{OH}}$ ions furnished by base to form one mole of water.
explanation: Enthalpy of neutralisation of strong acid and strong base is always ${\mathrm{-57 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}}$ because strong monobasic acid gives one mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}^{+}}}$ions and strong monoacidic base gives one mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}}$ions which form one mole of water.So, the correct option is (1).

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: At constant T and P, which one of the following statements is correct for the reaction? $\mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$

${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}} + \frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2(g)}}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2(}}\,{\rm{g)}}}}$

question:
Consider the following processes :
$\Delta {\text{H}}({\text{kJ}}/{\text{mol}})$
$\frac{1}{2}{\text{A}} \to {\text{B}}\,;\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} + 150$
$3{\text{B}} \to 2{\text{C}} + {\text{D}}\,;\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} - 125$
${\text{E}} + {\text{A}} \to 2{\text{D}};\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} + 350$
${\rm{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{B}} + {\rm{D}} \to {\rm{E}} + 2{\rm{C}},\,\,\,{\rm{\Delta H}}$ will be
explanation:
$\frac{1}{2}{\text{A}} \to {\text{B}}\,;\Delta {\text{H}} = + 150\,{\text{kJ/mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} (1)$
$3{\text{B}} \to 2{\text{C}} + {\text{D}};\Delta H = - 125\,{\text{kJ/mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
${\text{E}} + {\text{A}} \to 2{\text{D}};\Delta {\text{H}} = + 350\,{\text{kJ/mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,(3)$
By $[2 \times (1) + (2)] - (3),$ we have ${\text{B}} + {\text{D}} \to {\text{E}} + 2{\text{C}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\Delta {\text{H}} = 150 \times 2 + ( - 125) - 350$
$ = - 175\,{\text{kJ/mol}}$

question: The enthalpy of neutralisation of oxalic acid by a strong base is $-25 \mathrm{kcalmol}^{-1}$. explanation:
The enthalpy of neutralisation of strong acid and strong base
$ = - 13.7\,{\rm{k}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cal}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{equi}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{v}}^{ - 1}}$
${{\rm{As}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{two}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{equivalents}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{oxalic}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{are}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{neutralised}}}$
${ = - 13.7 \times 2 = - 27.4{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cal}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}$
${{\rm{Enthalpy}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{neutralisation}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{oxalic}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid}}}$
${ = - 25\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{Enthalpy}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{dissociation}}$
$ = - 25 - ( - 27.4) = 2.4\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}{{\rm{K}}^{ - 1}}$
${\rm{or}} \approx 2.0\,{\rm{kcal}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$

question: The enthalpy of dissolution of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ and $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$ are 20.6 and $8.8 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The enthalpy of hydration for $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$ is.
explanation:
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ (s) + (aq) $\rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ (aq);
${\rm{\Delta H}} = - 20.6{\rm{\,kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})+(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) ;$
${\rm{\Delta H}} = + 8.8\,{\rm{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Eq. (1) can be split into two steps as,
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow$$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) ;\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})+(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow$$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$
$\Delta {{\rm{H}}_1} + \Delta {{\rm{H}}_2} = - 20.6$
$\Delta {{\rm{H}}_1} + 8.8 = - 20.6$
$\Delta {{\rm{H}}_1} = - 29.4\,{\rm{kJ}}$

question: A person inhales $640 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ per day. If all $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is used for converting sugar into $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, how much sucrose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}\right)$ is consumed in the body in one day and what is the heat evolved? [$\Delta \mathrm{H}$ for combustion of sucrose $\left.=-5645 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+12 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 12 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+11 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
12 mole $(12 \times 32 \mathrm{~g})=384 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxygen requires1 mole of sucrose $342 \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, $640 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ requires
$\left(\dfrac{342}{384} \times 640 \mathrm{~g}\right) 570 \mathrm{~g}$ of sucrose.
$640 \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{O}_{2}=20$ mole $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ will require $\dfrac{20}{12}$ mole sucrose.
$\Delta {\rm{H}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{evolved}} = - 5645 \times \frac{{20}}{{12}}$
$=-9408.33 \mathrm{KJ}$

molar mass of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}=44 \mathrm{~g}$
i.e. 1 mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on combustion releases$=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\dfrac{35.2}{44}$ mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ will release

${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}} = {\rm{ - 93\,kJ}}/{\rm{mol}}$

question: The enthalpy change for which of the following processes represents the enthalpy of formation of AgCl ?
explanation:
Enthalpy of formation means the heat released or absorbed when 1 mole of compound is formed from its constituent elements.
$\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})$
It represents $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of AgCl as 1 mole AgCl is formed from its components in stable elemental form.

question:
The equation,
$\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{HCl} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{298}$
$ = - 22.060\,\,{\rm{kcal}}$ means
explanation:
$\frac{1}{2}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} + \frac{1}{2}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{HCl}},\,\,\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 22.060{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kcal}}$
Negative sign indicates the reaction is exothermic, So this equation indicates that 22060 cal or 22.06 kcal heat is given out on formation of 1 mole of HCl from its constituent elements.

options.text: The heat absorbed when one gram molecule of HCl is formed from its elements at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is 22.060 kcal.
The heat given out when one gram molecule of HCl is formed from its elements at 298 K is 22.060 kcal.

The heat absorbed when one atom of hydrogen reacts with one atom of chlorine to form one molecule of HCl at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and one atom of chlorine to form one molecule of HCl at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and one atmosphere pressure is 22.060 kcal.
The heat absorbed when one gram equivalent of HCl is formed from its elements at 298 K is 22.060 kcal .

question: 2.2 g of acetaldehyde produced 13.95 kcal of enthalpy on combustion in $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. Calculate the enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$.
explanation:
Enthalpy of combustion means heat released by the combustion of 1 mole substance.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
${\rm{molar}}\,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{CHO}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{44\,g}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^ - }$
$2.2\,{\rm{g}}\,\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{CHO}}$ gives 13.95 kcal
44 g ____ ?
$ = \frac{{44 \times 13.95}}{{2.2}} = 279\,\,{\rm{kcal}}$

question:
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}};\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 68.39\,{\rm{kcal}}$
${\rm{K}} + {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} + {\rm{water}} \to {\rm{KOH}}({\rm{aq}}) + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}};$
$\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 48{\rm{kcal}}$
${\rm{KOH}} + {\rm{water}} \to {\rm{KOH}}({\rm{aq}});\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 14{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kcal}}$
The heat of formation of KOH is (in kcal)
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$ (1)
$\mathrm{K}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+$ water $\rightarrow \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$ (2)
$\mathrm{KOH}+$ water $\rightarrow \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{3} (3)$
By adding equation (1) & (2) and subtracting equation 3 , we get
${\rm{K}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} + {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{KOH}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta {\rm{H}}{^\circ _{\rm{f}}} = \Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{1}}} + \Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} - \Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$
$ = - 68.39 - 48 + 14 = 102.39\,{\rm{kcal}}$

subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: For the allotropic change represented by equation, C (diamond) $\rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ (graphite) ; the enthalpy change is $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-1.89 \mathrm{~kJ}$. If 6 g of diamond and 6 g of graphite are separately burnt to yield carbon dioxide, the heat liberated in the first case is
explanation:
${{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(diamond) }}}} \to {{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(graphite) }}}};\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 1.89\,{\rm{kJ}}\,\,\,(1)$
${{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(diamond) }}}} + {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}};\Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{1}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
${{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{graphite }}}} + {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}};\Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
On subtracting Eq. (3) from Eq. (2), we get Eq. (1)
$\Rightarrow-1.86=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}+1.86$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}>\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$
So, heat released in second case will be more. Heat released by 1 mole ( 12 g ) transition $=1.89 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also heat released by $\dfrac{1}{2}$ mole (6 gram) transition
$=\dfrac{1.89}{2}=0.945 \mathrm{~kJ}$

options.text: Less than in the second case by 1.89 kJ
More than in the second case by 1.89 kJ
Less than in the second case by 11.34 kJ
Less than in the second case by 0.945 kJ

question:
Assertion: Heat of neutralisation of nitric acid with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is same as that of $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{NaOH}$.

Reason: In both cases strong acid and strong bases are neutralised. $\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
explanation: The heat of neutralization remains constant for both nitric acid with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ with $\mathrm{NaOH}$, as they have identical stoichiometry in neutralization reactions, involving the donation of one mole of proton to $\mathrm{NaOH}$, ensures the same heat release despite the specific identities of the acids and bases.So, option (1) is correct.

Reason: Both $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ are strong acids.
explanation: The heat of neutralization is the energy released or absorbed when an acid reacts with a base to form water and a salt. It depends on stoichiometry, and in reactions like that of $\mathrm{HCl}$ with $\mathrm{NaOH}$, the stoichiometry is the same, making their heat of neutralization equivalent. So, option (1) is correct.

But enthalpy changes are negative when anhydrous salts capable of forming hydrates are dissolved in water.


Reason: The difference in the behaviour is due to large difference in the molecular weights of hydrated and anhydrous salts. The substance with larger molecular weights usually show positive enthalpy changes on dissolution.
explanation: The exothermic or endothermic behaviour of dissolution depends only upon the nature of the salt and not on its molecular weight. So, option (3) is correct.

question:

Reason: The combustion reactions are always endothermic.
explanation:
Combustion reactions are invariably associated with the release of heat, and thus, for such reactions, the value of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ is always negative. Combustion reactions are exothermic.
So, option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Heat of sublimation is the sum of heat of fusion and heat of vaporisation.


Reason: Heat of the reaction or enthalpy change is same whether reaction takes place in one step or in a number of steps.
explanation: Sublimation involves the transition from solid to vapor with enthalpy change of $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Sub }}$. Similarly, the transition from solid to liquid has an enthalpy change of $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Fus }}$ and from liquid to gas it is $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {vap }}$. Therefore, the overall enthalpy change $(\Delta \mathrm{H})$ can be calculated as the sum of these changes: $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Sub }}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Fus }}+\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Vap }}$. So, option (1) is correct.

question: The enthalpy of formation of ammonia is $ - 46$  $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. The enthalpy for the reaction $2\,{{\text{N}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + 6{{\text{H}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \to 4{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$ is equal to -

$\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is $-46 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole}$

question: If the heat of neutralisation for a strong acidbase reaction is $-57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}$, what would be the amount of heat released when $350 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.20}}\,{\text{M}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ is mixed with $650 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.10 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{NaOH}$ ?
explanation:
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}: 0.2=\mathrm{n} \times \dfrac{1000}{350}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=0.07 \mathrm{~mole}$
Moles of $\mathrm{NaOH}: 0.1=\mathrm{n} \times \dfrac{1000}{650}$
$\mathrm{n}=0.065 \mathrm{~mole}$
1 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ needs 2 mole $\mathrm{NaOH}$
0.07 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ needs ?
$=\dfrac{0.07 \times 2}{1}=0.14 \mathrm{~mole} \mathrm{NaOH}$
But given has only 0.065 moles $\mathrm{NaOH}$. So, it is limiting reagent.
1 mole gives $57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}$
0.065 mole gives?
$=\dfrac{0.065 \times 57.1}{1}=3.7115$
$=3.71 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: In an order to decompose $9 \mathrm{~g}$ water, $142.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$ heat is required. Hence enthalpy of formation of water is:
explanation:

$\therefore$ heat required for decomposition of $18 \mathrm{~g}$ of water (1 mole)
$=\dfrac{18}{9} \times 142.5=285 \mathrm{~kJ}$
and enthalpy of formation of water $=-285 \mathrm{KJ}$ (sign changed as decomposition and formation are reverse reactions)

question: When $50 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.2 {\text{N  }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$ is mixed with $50 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{KOH}$, the heat liberated is
explanation:
For complete neutralization of strong acid and strong base energy released is $57.32 \mathrm{KJ} /$ $\mathrm{mol}$
No. of mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\dfrac{0.2 \times 50}{1000}=10^{-2}$
No. of mole of $\mathrm{KOH}=\dfrac{1}{1000} \times 50=5 \times 10^{-2}$

So $=57.32 \times 10^{-2}=0.5732 \mathrm{~kJ}=573.2 \mathrm{~J}$

question:
What is enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ if bond enthalpies are as
$(\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N})=941 \mathrm{~kJ},(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})=436 \mathrm{~kJ}$,
$(\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})=389 \mathrm{~kJ}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{0}=\sum \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}($ reactant $)-\sum \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}($ Product $)$
$=\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N})+3 \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})\right]$
$-\left[6 \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}(\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})\right]=941+3(436)-6(389)$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=-85 \mathrm{~kJ}$ for 2 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
$\therefore$ for 1 mole of ammonia, i.e.,enthalpy of formation of
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}=-\dfrac{85}{2}=-42.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$

subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: Enthalpy of formation of methane is $-75 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. What is the enthalpy change for formation of $24 \mathrm{~g}$ of methane?
explanation:
Molar mass of methane $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)=16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ For $16 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$, enthalpy of formation is $=-75 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ For $24 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$, enthalpy change $=\dfrac{24 \times(-75)}{16}=-112.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: Calculate the heat of formation of $\mathrm{HCl}$ gas from following reaction.$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-194 \mathrm{~kJ}$
explanation:
Heat of formation is the heat released or absorbed when 1 mole of compound is formed from its constituent elements.
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-194 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Heat given for 2 moles of
$\mathrm{HCl}=-194 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ Heat of formation for 1 mole of $\mathrm{HCl}=-97 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: Calculate change in enthalpy when $39 \mathrm{~g}$ acetylene is completely burnt with oxygen and enthalpy of combustion of acetylene is $\quad-1300 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. (At. mass $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{H}=1$ )
explanation:
$\mathop {{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}}\limits_{{\text{Acetylene}}} + \frac{5}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} + {{\text{H}}_2}{{\text{O}}_{({\text{l}})}}$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=-1300 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Acetylene molar mass is $26 \mathrm{~g} /$ mole.
When $26 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetylene is completely burnt, the change in enthalpy $=-1300 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ For $39 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetylene, the change in enthalpy $=\dfrac{-1300 \times 39}{26}=-1950 \mathrm{~kJ}$
