subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
If at $298 \mathrm{~K}$, the bond energies of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds are respectively $414,347,615$ and $435 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the value of enthalpy change for the reaction;
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ at 298K, will be
explanation:
For the reaction,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \\& \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{R}}=\sum(\mathrm{BE})_{\text {Reactants }}-\sum(\mathrm{BE})_{\text {Products }}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\left[4(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}+(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}}+(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}\right] \\& -\left[6(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}+(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}}\right] \\& =[(4 \times 414)+616+435]-[(6 \times 414)+347] \\& =[2706-2831] \Rightarrow-125 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: Enthalpy of combustion of methane and ethane are $-210 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $-368 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The enthalpy of combusiton of decane is
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 210{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol }}(1)$
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\dfrac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 368{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol }}(2)$
On subtracting Eq. (1) from Eq. (2), we get $\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 158{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol }}(3)$
$\therefore$ Enthalpy of combustion of one $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ unit
$=-158 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {comb }}\left(\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_{22}\right)=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {comb }}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)$
$+9 \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {comb }}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)$
$=-210+(9 \times-158)=-1632 \mathrm{kcal}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: What will be the heat of formation of methane, if the heat of combustion of carbon is $-\mathrm{x} \mathrm{kJ}$, heat of formation of water is $-\mathrm{ykJ}$ and heat of combustion of methane is $\mathrm{zkJ}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{xkJ}$
II. $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{ykJ}$
III. $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{zkJ}$
Multiply Eq. II by 2, adding to Eq. I and then, subtract Eq. III to get heat of formation of methane.
$\mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}, \Delta \mathrm{H}=(-\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}) \mathrm{kJ}$
(OR) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959089/preptracker/questions/q_17602_explanation_1764959088621.png $\therefore-\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}$ is the enthalpy change.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: The enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene is $-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If resonance energy of benzene is $-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, its enthalpy of hydrogenation would be
explanation:
The enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene (having one double bond) is $-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Hence, theoretically enthalpy of hydrogenation of Kekule's structure of benzene (3 double bonds) should be
$3 \times(-119.5) \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. Since, the resonance energy of benzene is $-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the actual enthalpy of hydrogenation of benzene will be $=[3 \times(-119.5)-(-150.4)]$
$=-208.1 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Hydrogenation and Resonance Energy

question: One mole of methanol when burnt in $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, gives out $723 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ heat. If one mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is used, what will be the amount of heat evolved?
explanation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}(1)+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(1) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-723 \mathrm{~kJ}$
By burning with $\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ heat evolved $=-723 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ By burning with 1 mole
$\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})=\dfrac{-723 \times 2}{3}=-482 \mathrm{~kJ}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: $\Delta {\text{H}}{^\circ _{\text{f}}}{\text{(298 }}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{K}})$ of methanol is given by the chemical equation ,
explanation:
Heat of formation is defined as the heat released when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements at STP. In case of allotropes, most stable allotrope is taken into account. Among graphite and diamond, graphite is more stable.
Hence, heat of formation of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ is represented by the equation,
${{\text{C}}_{{\text{(graphite) }}}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{OH(l)}}$

explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {sol }}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{L}}+\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {hyd }}$
$=788+(-784)$
$=4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Br}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HBr}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$ is

question:
What is standard $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}}$ bond enthalpy from following reaction,
$\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=-83 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\left( {B \cdot E \cdot ({\rm{H - H}}){\rm{ = 435}}\,{\rm{kJ}};B \cdot E \cdot ({\rm{N - H}}){\rm{ = 389}}\,{\rm{kJ}}} \right)$
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-83 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})=435 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})=389 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
We know that, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=}$ bond enthalpy of reactant - bond enthalpy of product
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=(\mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}+3 \times \mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})-}$
$\mathrm{(6 \times \mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})}$
$\mathrm{-83=(\mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}+3 \times 435)-(6 \times 389)}$
$\mathrm{-83=\mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}+1305-2234}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}=946 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: Enthalpy change for the reaction, $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$ is $\mathrm{-869.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ the dissociation energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}$ bond is
explanation:
$\mathrm{2 H_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 4 H(g) ; \Delta H=+869.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{H_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 H(g) ; \Delta H=\dfrac{869.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}{2}=434.8 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question: The $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}$ bond energy is ${\text{430}}\,{\text{k}}\,{\text{J}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$ and ${\text{Cl - Cl}}$ bond energy is $\mathrm{240 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1} . \Delta H}$ for $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ is$ - \,{\rm{90}}\,{\rm{kJ}}$. The $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Cl}}$ bond energy is about
explanation:
$\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCl}}$; $\mathrm{\Delta H=-90 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad \Delta H=\dfrac{1}{2} e_{H-H}+\dfrac{1}{2} e_{C l-C l}}$
or $\mathrm{-90=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 430+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 240-e_{H-C l}}$ $\mathrm{\therefore \quad e_{H-C l}=425 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}}$

question: When $\mathrm{6.0 \mathrm{~g}}$ of graphite reacts with dihydrogen to give methane gas, $\mathrm{37.4 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat is liberated. What is standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g}) ?}$
explanation:
Given,
When $\mathrm{6.0 \mathrm{~g}}$ of graphite reacts with dihydrogen to gives methane gas.
$\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{{\text{6}}\,{\text{g}}} {\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}};\Delta {\text{H = - 37}}.{\text{4}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
Standard enthalpy of formation
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}(\,{\text{g}})\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{{\text{12}}\,{\text{g}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} \to \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits_{{\text{16}}\,{\text{g}}} ;{\text{ - }}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{std }}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
On comparing both reactions (1) and (2), we find that reaction (1) is half of reaction (2), So on multiplying by half.
$\rm{ \underset{(12 / 2=6 \mathrm{~g})}{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}}+\dfrac{1}{2} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right) ; \\ =\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {std }}}$
Comparing the (1) and (3) equations,
$\rm{\begin{gathered}-37.4=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {std }} \\\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {std }}=-74.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{gathered}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: The $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HI}}$ are 142.2, $\mathrm{-393.3,-46.2}$ and $\mathrm{+25.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ per mol respectively. The order of their increasing stabilities will be
explanation:
Energy absorbed $\mathrm{\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { stability of compound }}}$ Energy released $\mathrm{\propto}$ stability of compound
Thus, the order of stability is
$\mathrm{142.2>25.9>-46.2>-393.2}$
i.e. $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{3}>\mathrm{HI}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: Heat of neutralisation of oxalic acid is ${\rm{ -  53}}{\rm{.35}}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$ using $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$. Hence, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}}$is
explanation:
Heat of neutralization of oxalic acid with $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ is,
${\text{1/2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + NaOH}} \to {\text{1/2N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}};$
$\Delta {\text{H = - 53}}.{\text{35}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
${\text{1/2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}} \to {\text{1/2}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}^{{\text{2 - }}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
$\Delta {\text{H = - 53}}.{\text{35}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
${{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}{\text{ + O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}} \to {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\quad \Delta {\text{H = - 57}}.{\text{3}}\,{\text{kJ}}$
Subtracting equation (2) from (1) we get
$\rm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow 1 / 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+H^{+} \quad \Delta H=-3.95}$
$\rm{ \therefore \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+ 2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \\ \Delta H=2 \times 3.95=7.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Neutralisation

question: The heat of combustion of carbon to $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ is $\mathrm{393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$. The heat released upon formation of $\mathrm{35.2 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ from carbon and oxygen gas is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \Delta \mathrm{H}=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
It means heat of formation of 1 mole $\mathrm{(44 \mathrm{~g})}$ of
$\rm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat of formation of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ of
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}=\dfrac{-393.5}{44} \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
Heat of formation of $\mathrm{35.2 \mathrm{~g}}$ of
$\rm{\mathrm{CO}_{2} =\dfrac{-393.5}{44} \times 35.2 \\ =-314.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \simeq-315 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question: Bond dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ are 434, 242 and $\mathrm{431 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively. Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{\Delta H_{H-H}=434 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\rm{ \Delta H_{C l-C l}=242 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}, \Delta H_{H-C l}=431 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ \dfrac{1}{2} H_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow H C l ; \Delta H_{r}=? \\ \Delta H_{r}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta H_{H-H}+\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta H_{C l-C l}-\Delta H_{H-C l} \\ =\dfrac{1}{2} \times 434+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 242-431=-93 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$

question:
From the following bond energies:
$\mathrm{H-H}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{431.37 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{C=C}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{606.10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{C-C}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{336.49 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{C-H}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{410.50 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Enthalpy for the reaction will be :
explanation: For reaction https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957285/preptracker/questions/q_17170_explanation_1764957284726.png $\rm{ \Delta H_{r}=-\left[4 \times B E_{(C-H)}+1 \times B E_{(H-H)}\right] \\ -\left[6 \times B E_{(C-H)}+1 \times B E_{(S-C)}\right] \\ =(4 \times 410.50+1 \times 606.10+1 \times 431.37) \\ -[6 \times 410.50)+(1 \times 336.49)] \\ =-120.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} .}$

question:
If $\mathrm{38.55 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat is absorbed, when 6.0 of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ react $\mathrm{C l F}$ according to reaction.
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{CIF}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{OF}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$
What is the standard enthalpy of reaction?
explanation:
Given, Enthalpy change for a given mass $\mathrm{=38.55 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ Mass of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}=6.0 \mathrm{~g}}$
Number of moles of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}=\dfrac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{O}_{2}}{\text { Molar mass of } \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{6 \mathrm{~g}}{32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=0.1875 \mathrm{~mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Enthalpy change for 1 mole $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{38.55 \mathrm{~kJ}}{0.1875}=205.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
From the reaction, 2 moles of CIF react with 1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$.
So, the standard enthalpy of reaction is +205.6 $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Standard Heat of Reaction

question:
The enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is $\mathrm{-46 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$. The enthalpy change for the reaction :
$\rm{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
explanation:
The amount of heat absorbed or evolved when 1 mole of the substance is directly obtained from its constituent elements is called heat or enthalpy of formation.
The reaction for the formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is :
$\rm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Heat of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}=-46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
The enthalpy change for the given reaction,
$\rm{ 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=? \\ \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=-(2 \times \Delta \mathrm{H})=-(2 \times-46)=92 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
The ratio of heats liberated at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ from the combustion of one $\mathrm{\mathrm{kg}}$ of coke and by burning water gas obtained from $\mathrm{\mathrm{kg}}$ of coke is
(Assume coke to be $\mathrm{100 \%}$ carbon)
(Given : enthalpies of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ as $\mathrm{393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}, 285 \mathrm{~kJ}, 285 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ respectively all at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K})}$.
explanation:
Given information, the enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ is wrong it should be enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}}$.
$\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~kg}}$ of coke $\mathrm{=\dfrac{1000}{12}=83.33 \mathrm{moles}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{83.33 \times 396.5 \mathrm{~kJ}}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}$
$\mathrm{\underbrace{\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}}_{\text {Water gas }}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{83.33 \times(285+285) \\ 83.33 \times 570 \mathrm{~kJ}}}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
Now, the ratio of
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{coke}}}&{\rm{:}}&{{\rm{water}}\,\,{\rm{gas}}}\\{{\rm{83}}{\rm{.33 \times 393}}{\rm{.5}}}&{\rm{:}}&{{\rm{83}}{\rm{.33 \times 570}}}\\{ \Rightarrow {\rm{393}}{\rm{.5}}}&{\rm{:}}&{{\rm{570}}}\\{ \Rightarrow {\rm{0}}{\rm{.69}}}&{\rm{:}}&{\rm{1}}\end{array}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: The bond dissociation energies of $\mathrm{X_{2}, Y_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{X Y}$ are in the ratio of $\mathrm{1: 0.5: 1 . \Delta H}$ for the formation of $\mathrm{X Y}$ is $\mathrm{-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$. The bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{X_{2}}$ will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{X_{2}: Y_{2}: X Y}$
Let B.E. $\mathrm{\mathrm{x} \quad \dfrac{x}{2} \quad x}$
Formation of $\mathrm{X Y}$
$\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} X_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} Y_{2} \rightarrow X Y ; \Delta H=-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ $\mathrm{\therefore \Delta H_{\text {reaction }}=B . E}$. of reactant ${\rm{ - B}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{.}}$ of product
$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {reaction }}=\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{x}+\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{4}-\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}=\dfrac{2 x+x-4 x}{4}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad-800=2 x+x-4 x}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad x=800 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ mole
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Bond dissociation energy is $\mathrm{800 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mole}^{-1}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question:
At $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ and 1 atm pressure, the enthalpies of combustion are as given below:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{Substance}}}&{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}&{{\rm{C}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{\rm{graphite}}} \right)}&{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{6}}}\left( {\rm{g}} \right)}\\{\frac{{{\Delta _{\rm{C}}}{\rm{H}}^\circ }}{{{\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{J}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}}&{{\rm{ - 286}}{\rm{.0}}}&{{\rm{ - 394}}{\rm{.0}}}&{{\rm{ - 1560}}{\rm{.0}}}\end{array}$
The enthalpy of formation of ethane is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)=2 \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})\right)+3 \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, l\right)}$
$\mathrm{-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{~g}\right)}$
$\mathrm{-1560=2(-394)+3(-286)-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{~g}\right)}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{~g}\right)=-86 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: The heat of atomisation of methane and ethane are $\mathrm{360 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ and $\mathrm{620 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, respectively. The longest wavelength of light capable of breaking the $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}}$ bond is (Avogadro number $\rm{\left.=6.02 \times 10^{23}, h=6.62 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \text { : }}$
explanation:
In $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}, 4 \times B E_{(C-H)}=360 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore B E_{(C-H)}=90 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
In $\mathrm{C_{2} H_{6}, B E_{(C-C)}+6 \times B E_{(C-H)}=620 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore B E_{(C-C)}=80 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\therefore {\rm{B}}{{\rm{E}}_{({\rm{C - C}})}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{80}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}}}{{{\rm{6}}.{\rm{023}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}}}\,\,{\rm{J/}}molecule$
${\rm{Now}},{\rm{E = }}\frac{{{\rm{hc}}}}{\lambda }$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad \lambda=\dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{80 \times 10^{3}}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad \lambda=1.49 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~nm}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Atomization

question: The standard enthalpy of formation $\mathrm{\left(\Delta_{f} H_{298}^{o}\right)}$ for methane, $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}$ is $\mathrm{-74.9 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$. In order to calculate the average energy given out in the formation of a $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}$ bond from this it is necessary to know which one of the following?
explanation:
To calculate average enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}$ bond in methane following informations are needed
(i) dissociation energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ i.e.,
$\rm{\dfrac{1}{2} H_{2}(g) \longrightarrow H(g) ; \Delta H=x(\text { suppose })}$
(ii) Sublimation energy of C(graphite) to C(g)
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}}$ (graphite $\mathrm{) \longrightarrow C(g) ; \Delta H=y(}$ Suppose $\mathrm{)}$
Given
${\rm{C}}({\rm{ graphite }}){\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}(\,{\rm{g}}){\rm{;}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H = 75}}\,\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$

options.text: The dissociation energy of the hydrogen molecule, $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$.
The first four ionisation energies of carbon.
The dissociation energy of $\mathrm{H_{2}}$ and enthalpy of sublimation of carbon (graphite).
The first four ionisation energies of carbon and electron affinity of hydrogen.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: The standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is $\mathrm{-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$. If the enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ from its atoms is $\mathrm{-436 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ and that of $\mathrm{N_{2}}$ is $\mathrm{-712 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, the average bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}}$ bond in $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is:
explanation:
Given
$\rm{ \dfrac{1}{2} N_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} H_{2} \rightarrow N_{3} \Delta H=-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ 2 H \rightarrow H_{2} \Delta H=-436.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ 2 N \rightarrow N_{2} \Delta H=-712 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ \Delta H_{r}=\sum B E_{R}-\sum B E_{P} \\-46= {\left[\dfrac{1}{2}(+712)+\dfrac{3}{2}(436)\right]-3 B E_{N-H} } \\-46=(356+3 \times 218)-3 B E_{N-H} \\ 3 B E_{N-H}=(356+654)+46 \\=1010+46}$

$\rm{B E_{N-H}=\dfrac{1056}{3}=+352 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question:
Given that:
(i) $\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}}$ of $\mathrm{N_{2} O}$ is $\mathrm{82 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(ii) Bond energies of $\mathrm{N \equiv N, N=N, O=O}$ and $\mathrm{N=O}$ are $\mathrm{946,418,498}$ and $\mathrm{607 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively,
The resonance energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$ is :
explanation:
$\mathrm{N_{2}(g)+\dfrac{1}{2} O_{2} \rightarrow N_{2} O(g)}$
or $\mathrm{N \equiv N(g)+\dfrac{1}{2}(O=O) \rightarrow \ddot{N}=\stackrel{+}{N}=\ddot{O}(g)}$
$\mathrm{\Delta H_{f}^{o}=}$ [Energy required for breaking of bonds]
- [Energy released for forming of bonds]
$\mathrm{=\left(\Delta H_{N \equiv N}+\dfrac{1}{2} \Delta H_{O=O}\right)-\left(\Delta H_{N=N}+\Delta H_{N=O}\right)}$
$\mathrm{=\left(946+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 498\right)-(418+607)=170 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Resonance energy
${{\rm{\Delta }}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{H}}{{\rm{^\circ }}_{\left( {{\rm{experimental}}} \right)}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{\Delta }}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{H}}{{\rm{^\circ }}_{\left( {{\rm{theoretical}}} \right)}}$
${\rm{ = 82 - 170 = - 88}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Hydrogenation and Resonance Energy

question: Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to
explanation:
Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to enthalpy of fusion + enthalpy of vapourisation.
Sublimation is direct conversion of solid to vapour, i.e., solid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ Vapour
Writing in two steps, we have solid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ liquid
$\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ Vapour
Solid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ liquid requires enthalpy of fusion
Liquid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ vapour requires enthalpy of vapourisation.

options.text: enthalpy of fusion + enthalpy of vapourisation

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy Changes During Phase Transformation

question: If the enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}}$are $\mathrm{-92.3 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ and $\mathrm{-167.44 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, find the enthalpy of solution of hydrogen chloride gas.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}_{(g)} \longrightarrow H_{(a q)}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}_{(a q)}^{-}}$
$\rm{\Delta_{\text {Sol }} H_{(H C l, g)} =[0+(-167.44)-(-92.3)] \\ =-74.14 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Solution

question: The heats of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{x} \mathrm{H}_{y}, \mathrm{C}}$, and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ are $\mathrm{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{c}}$ cal, respectively. The heat of formation $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{x} \mathrm{H}_{y}}$ of will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{x C_{(S)}+\dfrac{y}{2} H_{2}(g) \rightarrow C_{x} H_{y}}$
${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^\circ }\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{y}}}} \right){\text{ = }}\Delta {\text{H}^\circ}_{{{\text{C}_\text{x}}{\text{H}_\text{y}}}}{\text{ - x}} \cdot \Delta {\text{H}^\circ}{_\text{C}}{\text{ - }}\frac{{\text{y}}}{{\text{2}}}\Delta {\text{H}^\circ}{_{{\text{H}_2}}}$
$ = a - xb - \frac{y}{2} \cdot c = - \left( {bx + \frac{{yc}}{2} - a} \right){\text{cal}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
Enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$, $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})}$ are $\mathrm{-110,-393,81}$ and ${\rm{9}}{\rm{.7}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ respectively. The value of $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}}$ for the reaction is
$\rm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
explanation:
Heat of reaction
$\rm{\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}=\sum \Delta_{r} H_{\text {products }}^{o}-\sum \Delta_{r} H_{\text {reactants }}^{o}}$
$\mathrm{=\left[\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}\left(N_{2} O\right)+3 \Delta_{r} H^{\circ}\left(C O_{2}\right)\right]}$
$\mathrm{-\left[\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}\left(N_{2} O_{4}\right)+3 \Delta_{r} H^{\circ}(C O)\right]}$
$\mathrm{=[81+(3 \times-393)]-[9.7+(3 \times-110)] k J}$
$\mathrm{=-777.7 \mathrm{~kJ} \approx-778 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
From the following data of $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ of the following reactions,
${\rm{C}}({\rm{s}}){\rm{ + }}{\raise0.5ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 1$}\kern-0.1em/\kern-0.15em\lower0.25ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 2$}}\,{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{CO}}({\rm{g}});\Delta {\rm{H = - 110kJ}}$ and
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=132 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
calculate the mole composition of the mixture of oxygen and steam on being passed over coke at $\mathrm{1273 \mathrm{~K}}$, keeping the reaction temperature constant.
explanation:
From the question we see that the first reaction is exothermic and the second one is endothermic. Thus, if a mixture of oxygen and steam $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}$ is passed over coke and at the same time, temperature does not change, the composition should be such that $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ of both the reactions are numerically equal.
In the first reaction, consumption of $\mathrm{1 / 2}$ mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ evolves $\mathrm{110 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ energy while in the second reaction, for 1 mole of steam $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right), 132 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of energy is absorbed.
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ needed to evolve
$\mathrm{132 \mathrm{~kJ}=\dfrac{0.5}{110} \times 132=0.6}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Mole ratio of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ and steam $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)=0.6: 1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
A cooking gas cylinder is assumed to contain $\mathrm{11.2 \mathrm{~kg}}$ is obutane. The combustion of isobutane is given by
${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{10}}}}({\rm{g}}){\rm{ + }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{13}}}}{{\rm{2}}}} \right){{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{4C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}){\rm{ + 5}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{l}});$
$\Delta {\rm{H = - 2658}}\,{\rm{kJ}}$
If a family needs $\mathrm{15000 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of energy per day for cooking, how long would the cylinder last?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\because 58 \mathrm{~g}}$ isobutane provides energy $\mathrm{=2658}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad 11.2 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~g}}$ isobutane provides energy
$\rm{=\dfrac{2658 \times 11.2 \times 10^{3}}{58} k J=513268.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
The daily requirement of energy $\mathrm{=15000 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Cylinder will last in $\mathrm{\dfrac{513268.9}{15000}=34}$ days
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
At a particular temperature
$\rm{\mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
The approximate heat evolved when $\mathrm{400 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is mixed with $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{\rm{KOH}}$ solution will be
explanation:
Number of equivalents $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ taken
$\rm{=\dfrac{0.2 \times 2 \times 400}{1000}=0.16}$
Number of equivalents $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ added $\mathrm{=\dfrac{600 \times 0.1}{1000}=0.06}$
Number of equivalent of acid and bases neutralised each other $\mathrm{=0.06}$.
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat evolved $\mathrm{=0.06 \times 57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}=3.426 \mathrm{~kJ}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Neutralisation

question:
The standard heats of formation for $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(g)}, \quad \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}_{(g)}}$ are $\mathrm{-25.5,-57.8,-94.1}$ and ${\rm{ - 22}}{\rm{.1}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}$ respectively. Calculate $\mathrm{\Delta H_{(298 K)}}$ (in kcal) for the reaction :
$\rm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(g)}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}+4 \mathrm{HCl}_{(g)}}$
explanation:
At $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}, \Delta H}$ values are $\mathrm{\Delta H^{\circ}}$, i.e., standard heat of formation. For
${\rm{CC}}{{\rm{l}}_{{\rm{4(g)}}}}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2(g)}}}}{\rm{ + 4HC}}{{\rm{l}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}}{\rm{;}}\quad {\rm{\Delta H}}^\circ {\rm{ = ?}}$
${\rm{\Delta H}}_{{\rm{Reaction }}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ = \Delta H}}_{{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ + 4 \times \Delta H}}_{{\rm{HCl}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ - \Delta H}}_{{\rm{CC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{4}}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ - 2 \times \Delta H}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}$
${\rm{ = - 94}}{\rm{.1 + 4 \times ( - 22}}{\rm{.1) - ( - 25}}{\rm{.5) - 2 \times ( - 57}}{\rm{.8)}}$
${\rm{ = - 41}}{\rm{.4kcal}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
In the reaction :
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{l}})}}{\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{g}})}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{g}})}}{\rm{ + 2S}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{g}})}};\Delta {\rm{H = - 265}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}$
The enthalpies of formation for both $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ are negative and are in the ratio $\mathrm{4: 3}$. The enthalpy of formation for ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{2}}}\,\,{\rm{is + 26}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$. The enthalpy of formation for $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CS}_{2(1)}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H = - 265}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{o}}\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\,{\rm{g}}} \right){\rm{ = 4x}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{o}}\left( {{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\,{\rm{g}}} \right){\rm{ = 3x}}$
${\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H = }}\sum {{\Delta _{\rm{f}}}} {\rm{H}}_{{\rm{products }}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ - }}\sum {{\Delta _{\rm{f}}}} {\rm{H}}_{{\rm{reactants }}}^{\rm{o}}$
${\rm{ - 265 = 4x + 6x - 26}}$
${\rm{10x = 239}}$
${\rm{x = - 23}}.{\rm{9}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{o}}\left( {{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\,{\rm{g}}} \right){\rm{ = - 71}}.{\rm{7}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
What will be the enthalpy change for the following reaction?
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}){\rm{ + B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{2HBr}}({\rm{g}})$
Given that Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Br}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HBr}}$ is $\mathrm{435 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, 192 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{368 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}=\sum B . E_{r e a}-\sum B . E_{p r o}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=}$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}+}$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}_{2}-2 \times}$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HBr}}$
$\mathrm{=435+192-(2 \times 368) \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$ $\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-109 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: The specific heats of iodine vapours and solid are 0.031 and ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.055}}\,{\rm{Cal/g}}$, respectively. If heat of sublimation of iodine is ${\rm{24}}\,{\rm{Cal/g}}$ at $\mathrm{200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$, what is its value at $\mathrm{250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ ?
explanation:
${{\rm{I}}_{{\rm{2}}\left( {{\rm{solid}}} \right)}} \to {{\rm{I}}_{{\rm{2 (vapour }})}};\Delta {\rm{H = 24}}\,{\rm{Cal/g}}$ at $\mathrm{200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta C_{p}=C_{p}}$ of product $\mathrm{-C_{p}}$ of reactant
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.031 - 0}}{\rm{.055 = - 0}}{\rm{.024}}\,{\rm{Cal/g}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta H_{2}-\Delta H_{1}=\Delta C_{p}\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad \Delta H_{2}-24=-0.024(523-473)}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta H_{2}=22.8 \mathrm{Cal} / \mathrm{g}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Factors Influencing Heat of Reaction

question:
Calculate $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ at $\mathrm{85^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$.
Given : ${\rm{\Delta H}}_{{\rm{(298}}\,{\rm{K)}}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ = - 33}}{\rm{.29\,}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J/mol}}$ and
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{Substance}}}&{{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(s)}}}&{{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}_{{\rm{(s)}}}}}&{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{(1)}}}}}&{{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{(2)g}}}}}\\{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{P}}^{\rm{O}}\,\left( {{\rm{J/K}}\,{\rm{ - }}\,{\rm{mol}}} \right)}&{{\rm{103}}{\rm{.8}}}&{{\rm{25}}{\rm{.1}}}&{{\rm{75}}{\rm{.3}}}&{{\rm{28}}{\rm{.8}}}\end{array}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$
$\rm{ \dfrac{\Delta H_{T_{2}}-\Delta H_{T_{1}}}{T_{2}-T_{1}}=\Delta C_{p} \\ \therefore \quad \Delta C_{p}=\sum C_{p}(\text { products })-\sum C_{p}(\text { reactants }) \\ =(2 \times 25.1+3 \times 75.3)-(103.8+3 \times 28.8) \\ =276.1-190.2=85.9 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}=85.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{K} \\ \therefore \quad \dfrac{\Delta H_{(358 \mathrm{~K})}-(-33.29)}{358-298}=85.9 \times 10^{-3} \\ \Delta H_{358 \mathrm{~K}}=-28.14 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Factors Influencing Heat of Reaction

question:
In Harber's process of ammonia manufacture :
$\rm{\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(g)} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}^{o}=-92.2 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{Molecule}}}&{{{\rm{N}}_{{\rm{2(g)}}}}}&{{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{2(g)}}}}}&{{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{3(g)}}}}}\\{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{P}}}{\rm{J/}}\left( {{\rm{K}}\,{\rm{mol}}} \right)}&{{\rm{29}}{\rm{.1}}}&{{\rm{28}}{\rm{.8}}}&{{\rm{35}}{\rm{.1}}}\end{array}$
If $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}$ is independent of temperature, then reaction at $\mathrm{100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ as compared to that of $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ will be :
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-92.2 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\rm{\Delta \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}= 2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{~g}\right)-\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{~g}\right)-3 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right) \\ =2 \times 35.1-29.1-3 \times 28.8 \\ =70.2-29.1-86.4 \\ =-45.3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}}$

$\rm{ \Delta H_{100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}^{o}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}^{o}+\Delta \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}(\Delta \mathrm{T}) \\ =-92.2-\dfrac{45.3 \times 75}{1000} \\ \Delta \mathrm{H}_{100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}^{o}=-92.2-3.3975 \\ \mathrm{H}_{100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}^{o}=-95.59 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
Then the reaction at $\mathrm{100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ will be more exothermic compared to that at $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Factors Influencing Heat of Reaction

question: The standard heat of formation values of $\mathrm{\mathrm{SF}_{6}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{s})}}$, and $\mathrm{\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{g})}$ are $\mathrm{-1100,275}$, and 80 $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, respectively. Then the average $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}}$ bond energy in $\mathrm{\mathrm{SF}_{6}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{g})}+6 \mathrm{~F}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{SF}_{6(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{f}=-1100 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{s})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=+275 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
${\raise0.5ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 1$} \kern-0.1em/\kern-0.15em\lower0.25ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 2$}}$ ${{\rm{F}}_{{\rm{2}}(\,{\rm{g}})}} \to {{\rm{F}}_{({\rm{g}})}};\quad \Delta {\rm{H = 80}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{J/mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat of formation $\mathrm{=}$ Bond energy of reactant
- Bond energy of product
$\mathrm{-1100=[275+6 \times 80]-[6 \times \mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}]}$
Thus, bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}=309 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question:
The enthalpy change for the process $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+4 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$ and bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}}$ in $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})}$ is
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\text {vap }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=30.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=-135.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}(\mathrm{C})=715.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, where $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} \quad}$ is enthalpy of atomisation, $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)=242 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation:
Given,
(i) $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(1)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta_{\text {vap }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=+30.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
(ii) $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4(l)} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-135.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
(iii) $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=715.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(iv) $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=242 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(iii) $\mathrm{+2 *}$ (iv) - (i) - (ii) gives
$\rm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+4 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=1304 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}}$ bond in $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}=}$ $\rm{\dfrac{1304}{4}=326 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
( $\mathrm{\because}$ There are four $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}}$ bonds in $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}}$ ).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Atomization

question: The standard enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$, $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\ell)}$ and glucose (s) at $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ are $\mathrm{-400 \mathrm{~kJ} /}$$\mathrm{\mathrm{mol},-300 \mathrm{~kJ} /\mathrm{mol}}$ and $\mathrm{-1300 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, respectively. The standard enthalpy of combustion per gram of glucose at $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ is
explanation:
${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{12}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{6}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{6C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H_{O_{2}}^{\circ}=0}$
$\mathrm{\Delta H=\sum \Delta_{f} H_{P}^{\circ}-\sum \Delta_{f} H_{R}^{\circ}}$
$\mathrm{=6 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}+6 \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)}^{\circ}}$
$\mathrm{=(6 \times-400)+6(-300)-(-1300)}$
$\mathrm{=-2400-1800+1300=-2900 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=\dfrac{-2900}{180}=-16.11 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{gm}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: The heats of combustion of rhombic and monoclinic sulphur are respectively 70.96 and $\mathrm{71.03 \mathrm{k}}$ cal. What will be the heat of conversion of rhombic sulphur to monoclinic?
explanation:

$\rm{\Delta H=-70.96 \mathrm{kcal}}$
(2) $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\text {(monoclinic) }}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2}, \quad \Delta H=-71.03 \mathrm{kcal}}$
Eqn. (1) - Eqn (2) gives $\mathrm{S_{(\text {rhombic) }} \rightarrow S_{\text {(monoclinic) }}}$
$\rm{(\Delta) \mathrm{H}=71.03-70.96=0.07 \mathrm{kcal}=70 \mathrm{cal}}$

explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958979/preptracker/questions/q_16522_explanation_1764958978981.png $\rm{\left. {{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{reaction }}}}{\rm{ = }}\sum {{\rm{ B}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{. }}} {\rm{( Reactants ) - }}\sum {{\rm{ B}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{.(Products }}} } \right)}$
${\rm{ = }}[4 \times {\rm{B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C - H}}){\rm{ + B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C = C}}){\rm{ + B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{H - H}})]$
${\rm{ - }}[6 \times {\rm{B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C - H}}){\rm{ + B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C - C}})]$
${\rm{ = }}\left[ {\left( {{\rm{4 \times 410}}} \right){\rm{ + 600 + 400}}} \right]{\rm{ - }}\left[ {{\rm{6 \times 410 + 350}}} \right]$
${\rm{ = - 170}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$

question: A solution of $\mathrm{500 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KOH}}$ and 500 $\mathrm{\mathrm{mL}}$ of $\mathrm{0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}}$ is mixed and stirred; the rise in temperature is $\mathrm{T_{1}}$. The experiment is repeated using $\mathrm{250 \mathrm{~mL}}$ each of solution, the temperature rise is $\mathrm{T_{2}}$. Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Suppose heat evolved in ${{\rm{1}}^{{\rm{st}}}}$ case is $\mathrm{Q_{1}}$ and that in the ${{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{nd}}}}$ case it is $\mathrm{Q_{2}}$. Then $\mathrm{Q_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} Q_{1}}$.
But $\mathrm{Q_{1}=1000 T_{1}}$ and $\mathrm{Q_{2}=500 T_{2}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 500 T_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 T_{1}}$, i.e., $\mathrm{T_{2}=T_{1}}$

question: If the combustion of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ of graphite produces $\mathrm{20.7 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat, what will be molar enthalpy change ?

$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta H=-2.48 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Negative value of $\mathrm{\Delta H \Rightarrow}$ exothermic reaction.

question:
Comment on the thermodynamic stability of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})}$, given
$\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=90 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} ; \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-74 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation: $\mathrm{N O(g)}$ is unstable because formation of $\mathrm{N O}$ is endothermic (energy is absorbed), but $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$ is formed because its formation is exothermic (energy is released). Therefore, unstable $\mathrm{N O(g)}$ converts into stable $\mathrm{N_{2}(g)}$.

question:
The enthalpy of reaction $\mathrm{: 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(1)}}$ is $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-572 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
What will be standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$ ?
explanation:
According to the definition of standard enthalpy of formation, the enthalpy change for the following reaction will be standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$.
$\mathrm{H_{2(g)}+\dfrac{1}{2} O_{2(g)} \rightarrow H_{2} O_{(l)}}$ or the standard enthalpy
of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ will be half of the enthalpy of the given equation, i.e., $\mathrm{\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}}$ is also halved

${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{ - 572}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}{{\rm{2}}}$
${\rm{ = - 286}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Thermodynamics is the study of energy transformations on a macroscopic scale.
The term "macroscopic" refers to systems that contain a finite, often small, number of molecules.

explanation:

${\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{\rm{w}} = - \left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{V}}_2} - {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{V}}_1}} \right) = - (2 \times 200 - 5 \times 100)}\\ {{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = - 10.13\,{\rm{kJ}}}\\ {{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{q}} = 30.2{\rm{6}}\,{\rm{kJ}}}\\ {{\rm{\Delta U}} = {\rm{q}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{w}} = 30.26 - 10.13}\\ {{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = 20.13\,{\rm{kJ}}} \end{array}}$

question: For the reaction of one mole of zinc dust with one mole of sulphuric acid in a bomb calorimeter, $\Delta \mathrm{E}$ and $\mathrm{W}$ corresponds to
explanation: Bomb calorimeter is commonly used to find the heat of combustion of organic substances which consist of a sealed combustion chamber called a bomb. If a process is run in a sealed container then no expansion or compression is allowed, so, $\mathrm{W}=0$ and $\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}$. Thus, it has $\Delta \mathrm{E} < 0, \mathrm{~W}=0$.
subject: Chemistry

question: One mole of an ideal gas at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is expanded isothermally from initial volume of $1 \mathrm{~L}$ to $10 \mathrm{~L}$. The $\Delta \mathrm{E}$ for this process is $\left( {{\rm{R}} = 2} \right.{\rm{cal}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
From first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}$
For isothermal process, $\Delta \mathrm{T}=0$
Hence, $\Delta \mathrm{E}=0$
This means that for all isothermal process of an ideal gas, the value of $\Delta \mathrm{E}=0$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Internal Energy and Its Characteristics

question:
Assertion: The increase in internal energy $(\Delta \mathrm{U})$ for the vaporization of one mole of water at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ and $373 \mathrm{~K}$ is zero.


Reason: For all isothermal processes, $\Delta \mathrm{U}=0$.
explanation: At $373 \mathrm{~K}$ ( or $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), water begins to boil, and at this temperature, the vaporization of water is considered as an isothermal process. The change in internal energy $(\Delta \mathrm{U})$ depends solely on temperature. Under constant temperature conditions, the internal energy of the gas remains constant, meaning that $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ is zero. So, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Absolute values of internal energy of substances cannot be determined.


Reason: It is impossible to determine exact values of constituent energies of the substances.
explanation: Internal energy $({\text{U}})$ is the sum of various energy components, including translational, rotational, vibrational, bonding, electronic, potential, and nuclear energy. It is represented as $\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Et}+\mathrm{Er}+\mathrm{Ev}+\mathrm{Eb}+\mathrm{Ee}+\mathrm{Ep}+\mathrm{En}$, with the difference between initial and final states denoted as $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ for accurate measurement due to challenges in isolating individual energy components. So, option (1) is correct.

question:

(1) $\mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=119.5$ $\mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$
(2) $\frac{1}{2}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_2}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{Cl}}({\rm{g}});\Delta {\rm{H}} = {\rm{x}}\,\,{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mol}}$
(3) ${\text{Na}}({\text{s}}) \to {\text{Na}}({\text{g}});\Delta {\text{H}} = {\text{y}}\,\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}$
(4) $\mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-87.3 \mathrm{kcal} /$ mol
$(5)\,\,{\rm{Na}}({\rm{s}}) + \frac{1}{2}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_2}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{NaCl}}({\rm{s}});$
$\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 98.3\,{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mol}}$
$(6)\,\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^ \oplus }({\rm{g}}) + {\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}^ - }({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{NaCl}}({\rm{s}});$
$\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 185.3\,\,{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mol}}$

$ - 98.3 = {\rm{y}} + 119.5 + {\rm{x}} - 87.3 - 185.3$
${\text{x}} + {\text{y}} = 54.8\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}$ and ${\rm{x}} - {\rm{y}} = 3\,\,{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mole}}$
$\therefore {\text{x}} = 28.9\,\,{\text{k}}{\text{.}}\,{\text{cal}}/{\text{mole}}$ and ${\rm{y}} = 25.9\,\,{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mole}}$

The heat of formation of $\mathrm{NCl}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$ in terms of
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}, \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}$ is:

To get $\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+\dfrac{3}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{NCl}_{3}}$
$[\mathrm{eq}(1)]+\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{eq}(2)}{2}\right]+\left[\mathrm{eq}(3) \times \dfrac{3}{2}\right]$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}=-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}-\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}}{2}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3} \times \dfrac{3}{2}$

question:

${\text{2C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{2CO + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}},\Delta {\text{H}}^\circ {\text{ = + y}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{J}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(2)}}$
or ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{CO + 1/2}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\Delta {\rm{ H}}^\circ {\rm{ = + y/2}}\,{\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{J}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(3)}}$
By adding no. (1) and (3) eq.
$\rm{ \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \\ \mathrm{C}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}, \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\mathrm{y} / 2-\mathrm{x}=\dfrac{\mathrm{y}-2 \mathrm{x}}{2} \mathrm{~kJ}}$

${\rm{C}}({\rm{s}}){\rm{ + }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}});\Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = - x}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$

Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ is

heat of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ is

${\rm{S + }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + qkJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(3)}}$

$\mathrm{\Delta H_{298 K}^{o}=-285.9 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$

we have to calculate

question:

(ii) $\mathrm{C(}$ graphite $\mathrm{)+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=y \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(iii) $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g) ; \Delta_{r} \mathrm{H}=z \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{\text {(graphite) }}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{xkJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
Reverse the eq (ii), we get
(ii) ${\rm{CO}}({\rm{g}}) \to {{\rm{C}}_{({\rm{graphite}})}}{\rm{ + }}{\raise0.5ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 1$}\kern-0.1em/\kern-0.15em\lower0.25ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 2$}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}};{\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H = - y}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{J/mol}}$
(iii) ${\rm{CO}}({\rm{g}}){\rm{ + }}{\raise0.5ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 1$}\kern-0.1em/\kern-0.15em\lower0.25ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 2$}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}});{\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H = zkJ/mol}}$
By adding eq (ii) and eq (i), we get eq (iii)
$\mathrm{\mathrm{z}=\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}$
$\mathrm{x=y+z}$

question:
Oxidising power of chlorine in aqueous solution can be determined by the parameters indicated below:
$\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - }}}}({\rm{aq}})$
(using the data,
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\text {diss }} H_{C l_{2}}^{o}=240 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \Delta_{e g} H_{C l}^{o}=-349 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$,
$\mathrm{\left.\Delta_{h y d} H_{C l^{-}}^{o}=-381 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)}$, will be
explanation:
The energy involved in the conversion of $\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$ to $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{-1}(\mathrm{aq})}$ is given by
$\mathrm{\Delta H=\dfrac{1}{2} \Delta_{\text {diss }} H_{C l_{2}}^{(-)}+\Delta_{e g} H_{C l}^{(-)}+\Delta_{h y l} H_{C l}^{(-)}}$
Substituting various values from given data, we get
$\mathrm{\Delta H=9\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 240\right)+(-349)+(-381) k J m o l^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{=(120-349-381) \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}=-610 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$

question:
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO} ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-616 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

$\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-408 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
This $\mathrm{\Delta H^{\circ}}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{ZnS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ can be obtained by adding equations (1), (2) and (3).

$\Delta {\rm{H = - 393}}.{\rm{5}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)$

$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}=}$ - eqn (1) - eqn (2)
$\rm{ =-393.5-(-283.5) \\ =-110 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

${\rm{2C + 3}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{OH}};\Delta {\rm{H = - 66}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ - O - C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{2C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}};\Delta {\rm{H}}$

question: The combustion of one mole of benzene takes place at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ and $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~atm}}$. After combustion, $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)}$ are produced and $\mathrm{3267.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat is liberated. The standard enthalpy of formation, $\mathrm{\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ of benzene is, Standard enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ are $\mathrm{-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, respectively.
explanation:
The formation reaction of benzene is given by
$\rm{6 C_{(\text {graphite })}+3 H_{2(g)} \rightarrow C_{6} H_{6(l)} ; \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=?}$
The enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of benzene is:
$\rm{ C_{6} H_{6(l)}+\dfrac{15}{2} O_{2(g)} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)} ; \\ \Delta_{c} H^{\circ}=-3267 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \quad \ldots . \text { (ii) }}$
The enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}}$; $\mathrm{C_{(g r a p h i t)}+O_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}}$ $\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=-393.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
The enthalpy formation of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ is
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \quad \ldots .(\mathrm{iv})}$
$\mathrm{6 \times(i i i)+3 \times(i v)-(i i)}$
we get $\mathrm{6 \mathrm{C}_{(\text {graphite })}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6(l)}}$;
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=48.51 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.

subtopic: Hess’s Law of Constant Summation and its Applications

question: The enthalpy of formation of ammonia is $\mathrm{-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$. The enthalpy change for the reaction $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3}, \Delta H=-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}}$,
$\mathrm{\Delta H=2 \times(-46.0)=-92.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
Hence for the reverse reaction, $\mathrm{\Delta H=+92.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

subtopic: Lavoisier and Laplace Law

explanation:

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A reaction can have a positive $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}$ even if it occurs spontaneously.
A reaction's spontaneity is determined solely by its $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}$.
explanation: Statement A is true because spontaneous reactions can occur with a positive $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}$ if they are accompanied by a sufficiently large increase in entropy (positive  $\Delta {\text{S}}).$  Statement  B  is false as the spontaneity of a reaction depends on both $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}$, as described by Gibbs free energy   ($\Delta \mathrm{G}$). Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

explanation: Statement A is true because enthalpy changes can be endothermic (positive $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ ) or exothermic (negative  $\Delta {\text{H}}).$  Statement  B  is false because a negative enthalpy change does not guarantee spontaneity; spontaneity also depends on entropy changes according to Gibbs free energy. Correct option is (2)

question: Which of the following statements is correct for the spontaneous adsorption of a gas?
explanation:
For adsorption $\mathrm{\Delta S < 0}$ and for a spontaneous change $\mathrm{\Delta G=-v e}$. Hence $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ should be highly negative which is clear from the equation
$\rm{ \Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S \\ =-\Delta H-T(-\Delta S)=-\Delta H+T \Delta S}$
So, if $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ is highly negative, $\mathrm{\Delta G}$ will also be ${\rm{ - ve}}{\rm{.}}$

options.text: $\mathrm{\Delta S}$ is negative and therefore, $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ should be highly positive.
$\mathrm{\Delta S}$ is negative and therefore, $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ should be highly negative.
$\mathrm{\Delta S}$ is positive and therefore, $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ should be negative.
$\mathrm{\Delta S}$ is positive and therefore, $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ should also be highly positive.

question:
Consider the following statements for the given reactions:
(I) $\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})}$;
$\rm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=90 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}}$
(II) $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$;
$\rm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-74 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}}$
I. In reaction (I), $\mathrm{N O(g)}$ is unstable because the energy is released and unstable $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})}$ changes into the stable $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$.
(II) The energy is absorbed in reaction (i) , $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})}$ is unstable and unstable $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})}$ changes into the stable $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$.
Select the correct statements and choose the correct option
explanation:
Statement II is correct, while the other statements are incorrect.
Corrected form are as follows:
In reaction (i) energy is absorbed, hence NO become unstable.
So, in reaction (ii) transform to stable $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}$ by releasing energy.

subtopic: Exothermic Reactions and Endothermic Reactions

${\rm{\Delta H = 22}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$

question: The combustion of one mole of benzene takes place at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ and $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~atm}}$. After combustion, $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)}$ are produced and $\mathrm{3267.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat is liberated. The standard enthalpy of formation, $\mathrm{\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ of benzene is, Standard enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ are $\mathrm{-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, respectively.
explanation:
The formation reaction of benzene is given by
$\rm{6 C_{(\text {graphite })}+3 H_{2(g)} \rightarrow C_{6} H_{6(l)} ; \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=?}$
The enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of benzene is:
$\rm{ C_{6} H_{6(l)}+\dfrac{15}{2} O_{2(g)} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)} ; \\ \Delta_{c} H^{\circ}=-3267 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \quad \ldots . \text { (ii) }}$
The enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}}$; $\mathrm{C_{(g r a p h i t)}+O_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}}$ $\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=-393.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
The enthalpy formation of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ is
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \quad \ldots .(\mathrm{iv})}$
$\mathrm{6 \times(i i i)+3 \times(i v)-(i i)}$
we get $\mathrm{6 \mathrm{C}_{(\text {graphite })}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6(l)}}$;
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=48.51 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Hess’s Law of Constant Summation and its Applications

question: The enthalpy of formation of ammonia is $\mathrm{-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$. The enthalpy change for the reaction $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3}, \Delta H=-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}}$,
$\mathrm{\Delta H=2 \times(-46.0)=-92.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
Hence for the reverse reaction, $\mathrm{\Delta H=+92.0 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Lavoisier and Laplace Law

question: When 1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}$ is decomposed by platinum black, the heat evolved is $\mathrm{96.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$. The heat of formation of 1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}, \Delta H=-96.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}, \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=+96.6 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

explanation:
$\mathrm{\quad}$ (i) $\mathrm{I_{2}(s) \rightarrow I_{2}(g), \Delta H_{1}=+57.3 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(ii) $\mathrm{I_{2}(s) \rightarrow I_{2}(g), \Delta H_{2}=+15.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(iii) $\mathrm{I_{2}(l) \rightarrow I_{2}(g), \Delta H_{3}=}$ ?
By simplifying Eq (iii) - Eq (i) - Eq (ii),
$\mathrm{\Delta H=57.3-15.5}$
$\mathrm{=+41.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$

question: Heat of neutralisation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ is $\mathrm{-57.3 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$. The heat of ionisation of water will be

question:
Calculate $\mathrm{\Delta_{\text {lattice }} H^{\circ}}$ for $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaBr}}$ using the following data, $\mathrm{\Delta_{\text {sub }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ for sodium metal $\mathrm{=108.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$,
Ionisation enthalpy of sodium $\mathrm{=496 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Electron gain enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}=-325 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Bond dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}=192 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ for $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaBr}_{(\mathrm{s})}=-360.1 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=\Delta_{s u b} H^{\circ}+I \cdot E+\dfrac{1}{2} \Delta_{D i s} H^{\circ}}$
$\rm{+\Delta_{e g} H^{\circ}+\Delta_{\text {lattice }} H^{\circ}}$
${\rm{ - 360}}{\rm{.1 = 108}}{\rm{.4 + 496 + }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 192}}\left( {{\rm{ - 325}}} \right){\rm{ + }}{{\rm{\Delta }}_{{\rm{lattice}}}}{\rm{H^\circ }}$
${{\rm{\Delta }}_{{\rm{lattice}}}}{\rm{H^\circ = 735}}{\rm{.5}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$

question:
If the bond energies of $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Br}-\mathrm{Br}$ and
$\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Br}$ are 433, 192 and $364 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
respectively, then $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ for the reaction :
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Br}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HBr}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ is
explanation:
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=\sum$ B.E. $_{._{(\text {Rectan ts })}}-\sum$ B.E. $_{._{(\text {products })}}$
$\begin{aligned}=\left[\mathrm{B}_{\cdot \mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}}\right. & \left.+\mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\cdot\left(\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right)}\right]-\left[2 \mathrm{~B} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\cdot(\mathrm{HBr})}\right] \\& =[433+192-2 \times 364] \mathrm{kJ}=-103 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question:
In the reaction, $\mathrm{S}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{x kJ}$ and
$\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{3}+\mathrm{ykJ}$
heat of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is
explanation:
${\text{S}} + \frac{3}{2}{{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3} + 2{\text{xkJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad ({\text{1}})$
${\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2} + \frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3} + {\text{ykJ}}\quad \quad \quad \,(2)$
${\text{S}} + {{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {\text{qkJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
Equation (3) can be obtained by subtracting equation (2) from (1)
$ \Rightarrow {\text{q}} = 2{\text{x}} - {\text{ykJ}}$
Enthalpy of formation $\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)=-\mathrm{q}=-(2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})$
$ = {\text{y}} - 2{\text{x}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
The standard enthalpy of formation $\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\right)$ of ammonia is $-46.2 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$. What is the $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ of the following reaction?
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}^\circ = \sum {{\Delta _{\text{r}}}} {\text{H}}^\circ \,\,$ (product) $\,\,\, - \sum {{\Delta _{\text{f}}}} {\text{H}}^\circ \,\,$ (Reactants)
$=\left[2 \times \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]-\left[\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)+3 \Delta_{\mathrm{f}}
\mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)\right]$
$=[2 \times(-46.2)]-[(0.0)+3(0.0)]$
$=-92.4 \mathrm{~kJ}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: The standard enthalpy of combustion of C (graphite), $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{l})$ respectively are $-393,-286$ and $-726 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of methanol?
explanation:
$\mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=$ ?
$\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{C})+\left[2 \times \Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)\right]+$
$\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)\right]-\left[\Delta_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}\right)\right]$
$=(-393)+(2 \times-286)+\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0\right]-[-726]$
$=-239 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
The $\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ of $\mathrm{AO}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{BO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{ABO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$ is$ - 635,\,\,{\rm{x }}\,{\rm{and }}( - 1210\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}})$ respectively.
$\mathrm{ABO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{AO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{BO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ $=175 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
What is the value of $\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{ABO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{AO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{BO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=175$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{AO}}^{\circ}+\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{BO}_{2}}^{\circ}-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}}
\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{ABO}_{3}}^{\circ}$
$175=-635+x-(-1210)$
$175+635=\mathrm{x}+1210$
$\mathrm{x}=-400 \mathrm{KJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: Match the following columns based on the definitions or characteristics of different types of enthalpy of reactions and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A - R) Standard enthalpy of combustion refers to the heat released under standard conditions when a substance combusts completely.
(B - S) Bond enthalpy is the energy change associated with chemical bond reactions, either breaking or forming.
(C - P) Standard enthalpy of formation is calculated from forming a compound directly from its elements.
(D - Q) Enthalpy of atomization involves the energy required to break a compound into its atomic components. Correct option is (1).

question: The enthalpies of formation of gaseous $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and NO at 298 K are 82.0 and $90.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The enthalpy change of the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ is
explanation:
Given, $\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$;
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}=82 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{NO}} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{NO})}=90 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Enthalpy of reaction
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ will be
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=2 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{NO})}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}=2 \times 90-82$
$=180-82=98 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ are $-110,-393 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The enthalpy of combustion of CO (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) is
explanation:
Given
$\underset{(\mathrm{S})}{\mathrm{C}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{CO}} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}=-110 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\underset{(\mathrm{s})}{\mathrm{C}}+\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{1}=-393 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
To find enthalpy of combustion of CO .
i.e. $\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{CO}}+\dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{CO}}=$ ?
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{CO}}=\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{1}-\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}=-393-(-110)$
$=-283 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
Given,
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$; $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-285 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(l) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-76.6 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l}) ;$ $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-348.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Calculate the $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ of $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})$.
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=-285 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also, $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}_1^\prime = 285 \times 2 = + 570\,{\text{kJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l}) ;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=-76.6 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also, $4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(l) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}_2^\prime = 76.6 \times 2 = + 153.2\,{\text{kJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l}) ;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}=-348.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also, $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+6 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow$
$4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{l})$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}_3^\prime = - 348.2 \times 2 = - 696.4\,{\text{kJ}}\quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
On adding Eqs. (1), (2) and (3), we get
$2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) ;$
$\Delta {\text{H}} = \Delta {\text{H}}_1^\prime + \Delta {\text{H}}_2^\prime + \Delta {\text{H}}_3^\prime $
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=570+153.2-696.4$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=723.2-696.4=26.8 \mathrm{~kJ}$.
