question: The bond enthalpies of heavy hydrogen, O-O and $\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{O}$ are $+400,+498$ and $+490 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$, respectively. The $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ of the reaction to produce $\mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is
explanation:
Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ will be
$\mathrm{D}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\sum(\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{E})_{\text {Reactants }}-\sum(\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{E} .)_{\text {products }}$
$=400+\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 498\right)-(490 \times 2)$
$=400+249-980=-331 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

question: Enthalpy of hydrogenation of one mole of benzene to cyclohexane is [Resonance energy   of benzene $=-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene $=-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ]
explanation:
The hydrogenation of benzene forms cyclohexane.
$\mathop {{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_6}}\limits_{{\text{ Benzene }}} + 3{{\text{H}}_2} \to \mathop {{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_{12}}}\limits_{{\text{ Cyclohexane }}} $
Enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene
$=-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\therefore$ Calculated enthalpy of benzene
$=3 \times(-119.5)=-358.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Resonance energy of benzene $=-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
As we know,
Actual value of enthalpy = Calculated enthalpy
– Resonance energy
$\therefore$ Actual value of enthalpy
$=(-358.5)-(-150.4)=-208.1 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Hence, the enthalpy of hydrogenation of benzene is $-208.1 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Hydrogenation and Resonance Energy

$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}$ values of graphite, diamond and $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ are $0,1.9$ and $38.1 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$.
Hence, option (1) is the correct answer.

question: What is the standard enthalpy of reaction (in kJ ) when two moles of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas to give Fe metal? $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ (s)   and   ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\,(l)$  are  $ - $ 824.2 and $-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
explanation:
$\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {reaction }}=3\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)-\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ} \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$
$=3(-285.83)-(-824.2)=33.29 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
For 2 moles, $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-33.29 \times 2=-66.58 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: The standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$ and $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})$ are respectively $-10,-393,81$ and $-10 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$. Enthalpy change (in kJ) of the following reaction is $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}+3 \mathrm{CO} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fN}_{2} \mathrm{O}}+3 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fCO}_{2}}\right]-\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fN}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}+3 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{fCO}}\right]$
$=[81+(3 \times-393)]-[-10+(3 \times-10)]$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=-1058 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
The standard heat of formation, in $\mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ of $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ is :
[Given : standard heat of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ ion $(\mathrm{aq})=-216 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$, standard heat of crystallisation of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})=-4.5 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ standard heat of formation of $\left.\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})=-349 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}\right]$
explanation:
$\mathrm{S}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}}{^\circ _{\text{f}}} = - 216\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
${\rm{B}}{{\rm{a}}^{2 + }}({\rm{g}}) + {\rm{SO}}_4^{2 - }({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{BaS}}{{\rm{O}}_4}(\,{\rm{s}})\,\,;$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}{^\circ _{{\rm{crystallisation }}}} = - 4.5{\rm{kcal}}/{\rm{mol}}\quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
${\rm{Ba}} + {\rm{S}} + 2{{\rm{O}}_2} \to {\rm{BaS}}{{\rm{O}}_4}(\,{\rm{s}});$
$\Delta {\text{H}}{^\circ _{{\text{f}}\left( {{\text{BaS}}{{\text{O}}_4}} \right)}} = - 349{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
$\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}(\mathrm{g})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ba}^{+2}\right)=$ ?
From equation (1), (2) and (3) we get equation (4). Applying equation $(3) - (1) - (2)\,\,;$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ba}^{+2}\right)=349-(-4.5)-(-216)$
$=-349+4.5+216$

question:
Consider the following processes :
$\Delta {\text{H}}({\text{kJ}}/{\text{mol}})$
$\frac{1}{2}{\text{A}} \to {\text{B}}\,;\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} + 150$
$3{\text{B}} \to 2{\text{C}} + {\text{D}}\,;\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} - 125$
${\text{E}} + {\text{A}} \to 2{\text{D}};\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} + 350$
${\rm{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{B}} + {\rm{D}} \to {\rm{E}} + 2{\rm{C}},\,\,\,{\rm{\Delta H}}$ will be
explanation:
$\frac{1}{2}{\text{A}} \to {\text{B}}\,;\Delta {\text{H}} = + 150\,{\text{kJ/mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} (1)$
$3{\text{B}} \to 2{\text{C}} + {\text{D}};\Delta H = - 125\,{\text{kJ/mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
${\text{E}} + {\text{A}} \to 2{\text{D}};\Delta {\text{H}} = + 350\,{\text{kJ/mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,(3)$
By $[2 \times (1) + (2)] - (3),$ we have ${\text{B}} + {\text{D}} \to {\text{E}} + 2{\text{C}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\Delta {\text{H}} = 150 \times 2 + ( - 125) - 350$
$ = - 175\,{\text{kJ/mol}}$

question: The enthalpy of neutralisation of oxalic acid by a strong base is $-25 \mathrm{kcalmol}^{-1}$. The enthalpy of dissociation of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$ is
explanation:
The enthalpy of neutralisation of strong acid and strong base
$ = - 13.7\,{\rm{k}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cal}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{equi}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{v}}^{ - 1}}$
${{\rm{As}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{two}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{equivalents}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{oxalic}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{are}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{neutralised}}}$
${ = - 13.7 \times 2 = - 27.4{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cal}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}$
${{\rm{Enthalpy}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{neutralisation}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{oxalic}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid}}}$
${ = - 25\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{Enthalpy}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{dissociation}}$
$ = - 25 - ( - 27.4) = 2.4\,{\rm{cal}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}{{\rm{K}}^{ - 1}}$
${\rm{or}} \approx 2.0\,{\rm{kcal}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$

question: The enthalpy of dissolution of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ and $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$ are 20.6 and $8.8 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The enthalpy of hydration for $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$ is.
explanation:
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ (s) + (aq) $\rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ (aq);
${\rm{\Delta H}} = - 20.6{\rm{\,kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})+(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) ;$
${\rm{\Delta H}} = + 8.8\,{\rm{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Eq. (1) can be split into two steps as,
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow$$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) ;\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})+(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow$$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$
$\Delta {{\rm{H}}_1} + \Delta {{\rm{H}}_2} = - 20.6$
$\Delta {{\rm{H}}_1} + 8.8 = - 20.6$
$\Delta {{\rm{H}}_1} = - 29.4\,{\rm{kJ}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Solution

question: A person inhales $640 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ per day. If all $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is used for converting sugar into $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, how much sucrose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}\right)$ is consumed in the body in one day and what is the heat evolved? [$\Delta \mathrm{H}$ for combustion of sucrose $\left.=-5645 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+12 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 12 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+11 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
12 mole $(12 \times 32 \mathrm{~g})=384 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxygen requires1 mole of sucrose $342 \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, $640 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ requires
$\left(\dfrac{342}{384} \times 640 \mathrm{~g}\right) 570 \mathrm{~g}$ of sucrose.
$640 \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{O}_{2}=20$ mole $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ will require $\dfrac{20}{12}$ mole sucrose.
$\Delta {\rm{H}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{evolved}} = - 5645 \times \frac{{20}}{{12}}$
$=-9408.33 \mathrm{KJ}$

subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: Enthalpy of combustion of carbon to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is $-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. What amount of heat will be released upon formation of $35.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ from carbon and oxygen gas?
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {combustion }}=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
molar mass of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}=44 \mathrm{~g}$
i.e. 1 mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on combustion releases$=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\dfrac{35.2}{44}$ mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ will release
$=-\dfrac{393.5 \times 35.2}{44}=314.8 \mathrm{KJ}$

explanation:

${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}} = {\rm{ - 93\,kJ}}/{\rm{mol}}$
$\because$ In this reaction, one mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$ is formed$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of the reaction will remain same,$\text { i.e. }-93 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

question:
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}};\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 68.39\,{\rm{kcal}}$
${\rm{K}} + {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} + {\rm{water}} \to {\rm{KOH}}({\rm{aq}}) + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}};$
$\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 48{\rm{kcal}}$
${\rm{KOH}} + {\rm{water}} \to {\rm{KOH}}({\rm{aq}});\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 14{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kcal}}$
The heat of formation of KOH is (in kcal)
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$ (1)
$\mathrm{K}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+$ water $\rightarrow \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$ (2)
$\mathrm{KOH}+$ water $\rightarrow \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{3} (3)$
By adding equation (1) & (2) and subtracting equation 3 , we get
${\rm{K}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} + {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{KOH}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta {\rm{H}}{^\circ _{\rm{f}}} = \Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{1}}} + \Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} - \Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$
$ = - 68.39 - 48 + 14 = 102.39\,{\rm{kcal}}$

subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: For the allotropic change represented by equation, C (diamond) $\rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ (graphite) ; the enthalpy change is $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-1.89 \mathrm{~kJ}$. If 6 g of diamond and 6 g of graphite are separately burnt to yield carbon dioxide, the heat liberated in the first case is
explanation:
${{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(diamond) }}}} \to {{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(graphite) }}}};\Delta {\rm{H}} = - 1.89\,{\rm{kJ}}\,\,\,(1)$
${{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(diamond) }}}} + {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}};\Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{1}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
${{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{graphite }}}} + {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}};\Delta {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
On subtracting Eq. (3) from Eq. (2), we get Eq. (1)
$\Rightarrow-1.86=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}+1.86$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}>\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}$
So, heat released in second case will be more. Heat released by 1 mole ( 12 g ) transition $=1.89 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Also heat released by $\dfrac{1}{2}$ mole (6 gram) transition
$=\dfrac{1.89}{2}=0.945 \mathrm{~kJ}$

options.text: Less than in the second case by 1.89 kJ
More than in the second case by 1.89 kJ
Less than in the second case by 11.34 kJ
Less than in the second case by 0.945 kJ

question:
Assertion: Heat of neutralisation of perchloric acid, $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is same as that of $\mathrm{HCl}$ with $\mathrm{NaOH}$.


Reason: Both $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ are strong acids.
explanation: The heat of neutralization is the energy released or absorbed when an acid reacts with a base to form water and a salt. It depends on stoichiometry, and in reactions like that of $\mathrm{HCl}$ with $\mathrm{NaOH}$, the stoichiometry is the same, making their heat of neutralization equivalent. So, option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(1)}$ is greater than that of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}$.


Reason: Enthalpy change is negative for the condensation reaction, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(1)}$
explanation: Difference between the heat of reaction of liquid water and steam is the heat of condensation, which is negative. Hence standard heat of formation is more negative for liquid water than steam. Some amount of energy is used in the vapourization. So, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Enthalpy changes are positive when $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and salt like $\mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{KCl}$ etc. which do not form hydrates, are dissolved in water. But enthalpy changes are negative when anhydrous salts capable of forming hydrates are dissolved in water.


Reason: The difference in the behaviour is due to large difference in the molecular weights of hydrated and anhydrous salts. The substance with larger molecular weights usually show positive enthalpy changes on dissolution.
explanation: The exothermic or endothermic behaviour of dissolution depends only upon the nature of the salt and not on its molecular weight. So, option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: For the combustion reactions, the value of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ is always negative.


Reason: The combustion reactions are always endothermic.
explanation:
Combustion reactions are invariably associated with the release of heat, and thus, for such reactions, the value of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ is always negative. Combustion reactions are exothermic.
So, option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Heat of sublimation is the sum of heat of fusion and heat of vaporisation.


Reason: Heat of the reaction or enthalpy change is same whether reaction takes place in one step or in a number of steps.
explanation: Sublimation involves the transition from solid to vapor with enthalpy change of $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Sub }}$. Similarly, the transition from solid to liquid has an enthalpy change of $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Fus }}$ and from liquid to gas it is $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {vap }}$. Therefore, the overall enthalpy change $(\Delta \mathrm{H})$ can be calculated as the sum of these changes: $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Sub }}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Fus }}+\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {Vap }}$. So, option (1) is correct.

question:

Reason: Enthalpy of atomisation is the heat of the reaction, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}$.
explanation:
The enthalpy of atomization is defined as the enthalpy change that occurs when one mole of a substance is broken up into its individual atoms in the gas phase. Consider the following example of atomization of dihydrogen
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=435.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} .$
$\mathrm{H}$ atoms are formed by breaking $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds in dihydrogen by absorbing $435.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. So, option (3) is correct.

question: The enthalpy of formation of ammonia is $ - 46$  $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. The enthalpy for the reaction $2\,{{\text{N}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + 6{{\text{H}}_2}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \to 4{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$ is equal to -
explanation:
Enthalpy of formation of 1 mole
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is $-46 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\therefore$ for 4 mole $\mathrm{NH}_{3}=-4 \times 46$
$=-184 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: If the heat of neutralisation for a strong acidbase reaction is $-57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}$, what would be the amount of heat released when $350 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.20}}\,{\text{M}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ is mixed with $650 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.10 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{NaOH}$ ?
explanation:
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}: 0.2=\mathrm{n} \times \dfrac{1000}{350}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=0.07 \mathrm{~mole}$
Moles of $\mathrm{NaOH}: 0.1=\mathrm{n} \times \dfrac{1000}{650}$
$\mathrm{n}=0.065 \mathrm{~mole}$
1 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ needs 2 mole $\mathrm{NaOH}$
0.07 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ needs ?
$=\dfrac{0.07 \times 2}{1}=0.14 \mathrm{~mole} \mathrm{NaOH}$
But given has only 0.065 moles $\mathrm{NaOH}$. So, it is limiting reagent.
1 mole gives $57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}$
0.065 mole gives?
$=\dfrac{0.065 \times 57.1}{1}=3.7115$
$=3.71 \mathrm{~kJ}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Neutralisation

question: In an order to decompose $9 \mathrm{~g}$ water, $142.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$ heat is required. Hence enthalpy of formation of water is:
explanation:
For the decomposition of ${\text{9 g}}$ of water, heat required $=142.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ heat required for decomposition of $18 \mathrm{~g}$ of water (1 mole)
$=\dfrac{18}{9} \times 142.5=285 \mathrm{~kJ}$
and enthalpy of formation of water $=-285 \mathrm{KJ}$ (sign changed as decomposition and formation are reverse reactions)
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: When $50 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.2 {\text{N  }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$ is mixed with $50 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{KOH}$, the heat liberated is
explanation:
For complete neutralization of strong acid and strong base energy released is $57.32 \mathrm{KJ} /$ $\mathrm{mol}$
No. of mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\dfrac{0.2 \times 50}{1000}=10^{-2}$
No. of mole of $\mathrm{KOH}=\dfrac{1}{1000} \times 50=5 \times 10^{-2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is limiting reagent as 1 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ needs 2 moles $\mathrm{KOH}$
So $=57.32 \times 10^{-2}=0.5732 \mathrm{~kJ}=573.2 \mathrm{~J}$

question:
If ${\text{C + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 94}}{\text{.2}}\,\,{\text{kcal}}$
${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \to {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O + 68}}{\text{.3}}\,\,{\text{kcal & }}$
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O + 210}}{\text{.8}}\,\,{\text{kcal}}$
then the possible heat of formation of methane will be

To get $\mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}$

question:
What is enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ if bond enthalpies are as
$(\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N})=941 \mathrm{~kJ},(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})=436 \mathrm{~kJ}$,
$(\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})=389 \mathrm{~kJ}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{0}=\sum \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}($ reactant $)-\sum \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}($ Product $)$
$=\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N})+3 \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})\right]$
$-\left[6 \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}(\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})\right]=941+3(436)-6(389)$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=-85 \mathrm{~kJ}$ for 2 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
$\therefore$ for 1 mole of ammonia, i.e.,enthalpy of formation of
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}=-\dfrac{85}{2}=-42.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: Enthalpy of formation of methane is $-75 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. What is the enthalpy change for formation of $24 \mathrm{~g}$ of methane?
explanation:
Molar mass of methane $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)=16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ For $16 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$, enthalpy of formation is $=-75 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ For $24 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$, enthalpy change $=\dfrac{24 \times(-75)}{16}=-112.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: Calculate the heat of formation of $\mathrm{HCl}$ gas from following reaction.$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-194 \mathrm{~kJ}$
explanation:
Heat of formation is the heat released or absorbed when 1 mole of compound is formed from its constituent elements.
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-194 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Heat given for 2 moles of
$\mathrm{HCl}=-194 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ Heat of formation for 1 mole of $\mathrm{HCl}=-97 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: Calculate change in enthalpy when $39 \mathrm{~g}$ acetylene is completely burnt with oxygen and enthalpy of combustion of acetylene is $\quad-1300 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. (At. mass $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{H}=1$ )
explanation:
$\mathop {{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}}\limits_{{\text{Acetylene}}} + \frac{5}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} + {{\text{H}}_2}{{\text{O}}_{({\text{l}})}}$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=-1300 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Acetylene molar mass is $26 \mathrm{~g} /$ mole.
When $26 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetylene is completely burnt, the change in enthalpy $=-1300 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ For $39 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetylene, the change in enthalpy $=\dfrac{-1300 \times 39}{26}=-1950 \mathrm{~kJ}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
If at $298 \mathrm{~K}$, the bond energies of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds are respectively $414,347,615$ and $435 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the value of enthalpy change for the reaction;
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ at 298K, will be
explanation:
For the reaction,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \\& \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{R}}=\sum(\mathrm{BE})_{\text {Reactants }}-\sum(\mathrm{BE})_{\text {Products }}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\left[4(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}+(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}}+(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}\right] \\& -\left[6(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}+(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}}\right] \\& =[(4 \times 414)+616+435]-[(6 \times 414)+347] \\& =[2706-2831] \Rightarrow-125 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: Enthalpy of combustion of methane and ethane are $-210 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $-368 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The enthalpy of combusiton of decane is
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 210{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol }}(1)$
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\dfrac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 368{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol }}(2)$
On subtracting Eq. (1) from Eq. (2), we get $\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 158{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol }}(3)$
$\therefore$ Enthalpy of combustion of one $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ unit
$=-158 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {comb }}\left(\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_{22}\right)=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {comb }}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)$
$+9 \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {comb }}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)$
$=-210+(9 \times-158)=-1632 \mathrm{kcal}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: What will be the heat of formation of methane, if the heat of combustion of carbon is $-\mathrm{x} \mathrm{kJ}$, heat of formation of water is $-\mathrm{ykJ}$ and heat of combustion of methane is $\mathrm{zkJ}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{xkJ}$
II. $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{ykJ}$
III. $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{zkJ}$
Multiply Eq. II by 2, adding to Eq. I and then, subtract Eq. III to get heat of formation of methane.
$\mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}, \Delta \mathrm{H}=(-\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}) \mathrm{kJ}$
(OR) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959089/preptracker/questions/q_17602_explanation_1764959088621.png $\therefore-\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}$ is the enthalpy change.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: The enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene is $-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If resonance energy of benzene is $-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, its enthalpy of hydrogenation would be
explanation:
The enthalpy of hydrogenation of cyclohexene (having one double bond) is $-119.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Hence, theoretically enthalpy of hydrogenation of Kekule's structure of benzene (3 double bonds) should be
$3 \times(-119.5) \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. Since, the resonance energy of benzene is $-150.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the actual enthalpy of hydrogenation of benzene will be $=[3 \times(-119.5)-(-150.4)]$
$=-208.1 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Hydrogenation and Resonance Energy

question: One mole of methanol when burnt in $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, gives out $723 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ heat. If one mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is used, what will be the amount of heat evolved?
explanation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}(1)+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(1) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-723 \mathrm{~kJ}$
By burning with $\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ heat evolved $=-723 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ By burning with 1 mole
$\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})=\dfrac{-723 \times 2}{3}=-482 \mathrm{~kJ}$

question: $\Delta {\text{H}}{^\circ _{\text{f}}}{\text{(298 }}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{K}})$ of methanol is given by the chemical equation ,

question:
What is standard $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}}$ bond enthalpy from following reaction,
$\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}=-83 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\left( {B \cdot E \cdot ({\rm{H - H}}){\rm{ = 435}}\,{\rm{kJ}};B \cdot E \cdot ({\rm{N - H}}){\rm{ = 389}}\,{\rm{kJ}}} \right)$
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-83 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})=435 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})=389 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
We know that, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=}$ bond enthalpy of reactant - bond enthalpy of product
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=(\mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}+3 \times \mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H})-}$
$\mathrm{(6 \times \mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H})}$
$\mathrm{-83=(\mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}+3 \times 435)-(6 \times 389)}$
$\mathrm{-83=\mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}+1305-2234}$
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \mathrm{BE}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}=946 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question: When $\mathrm{6.0 \mathrm{~g}}$ of graphite reacts with dihydrogen to give methane gas, $\mathrm{37.4 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat is liberated. What is standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g}) ?}$
explanation:
Given,
When $\mathrm{6.0 \mathrm{~g}}$ of graphite reacts with dihydrogen to gives methane gas.
$\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{{\text{6}}\,{\text{g}}} {\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}};\Delta {\text{H = - 37}}.{\text{4}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
Standard enthalpy of formation
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}(\,{\text{g}})\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{{\text{12}}\,{\text{g}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} \to \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits_{{\text{16}}\,{\text{g}}} ;{\text{ - }}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{std }}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
On comparing both reactions (1) and (2), we find that reaction (1) is half of reaction (2), So on multiplying by half.
$\rm{ \underset{(12 / 2=6 \mathrm{~g})}{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}}+\dfrac{1}{2} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right) ; \\ =\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {std }}}$
Comparing the (1) and (3) equations,
$\rm{\begin{gathered}-37.4=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {std }} \\\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {std }}=-74.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{gathered}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: The $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HI}}$ are 142.2, $\mathrm{-393.3,-46.2}$ and $\mathrm{+25.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ per mol respectively. The order of their increasing stabilities will be
explanation:
Energy absorbed $\mathrm{\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { stability of compound }}}$ Energy released $\mathrm{\propto}$ stability of compound
Thus, the order of stability is
$\mathrm{142.2>25.9>-46.2>-393.2}$
i.e. $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{3}>\mathrm{HI}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: Heat of neutralisation of oxalic acid is ${\rm{ -  53}}{\rm{.35}}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$ using $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$. Hence, $\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}}$is
explanation:
Heat of neutralization of oxalic acid with $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ is,
${\text{1/2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + NaOH}} \to {\text{1/2N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}};$
$\Delta {\text{H = - 53}}.{\text{35}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
${\text{1/2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}} \to {\text{1/2}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}^{{\text{2 - }}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
$\Delta {\text{H = - 53}}.{\text{35}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
${{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}{\text{ + O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}} \to {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\quad \Delta {\text{H = - 57}}.{\text{3}}\,{\text{kJ}}$
Subtracting equation (2) from (1) we get
$\rm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow 1 / 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+H^{+} \quad \Delta H=-3.95}$
$\rm{ \therefore \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+ 2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \\ \Delta H=2 \times 3.95=7.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question: The heat of combustion of carbon to $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ is $\mathrm{393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$. The heat released upon formation of $\mathrm{35.2 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ from carbon and oxygen gas is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \Delta \mathrm{H}=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
It means heat of formation of 1 mole $\mathrm{(44 \mathrm{~g})}$ of
$\rm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat of formation of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ of
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}=\dfrac{-393.5}{44} \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
Heat of formation of $\mathrm{35.2 \mathrm{~g}}$ of
$\rm{\mathrm{CO}_{2} =\dfrac{-393.5}{44} \times 35.2 \\ =-314.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \simeq-315 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question: Bond dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ are 434, 242 and $\mathrm{431 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively. Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{\Delta H_{H-H}=434 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\rm{ \Delta H_{C l-C l}=242 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}, \Delta H_{H-C l}=431 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ \dfrac{1}{2} H_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow H C l ; \Delta H_{r}=? \\ \Delta H_{r}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta H_{H-H}+\dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta H_{C l-C l}-\Delta H_{H-C l} \\ =\dfrac{1}{2} \times 434+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 242-431=-93 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$

question:
From the following bond energies:
$\mathrm{H-H}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{431.37 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{C=C}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{606.10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{C-C}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{336.49 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{C-H}$ bond energy: $\mathrm{410.50 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Enthalpy for the reaction will be :
explanation: For reaction https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957285/preptracker/questions/q_17170_explanation_1764957284726.png $\rm{ \Delta H_{r}=-\left[4 \times B E_{(C-H)}+1 \times B E_{(H-H)}\right] \\ -\left[6 \times B E_{(C-H)}+1 \times B E_{(S-C)}\right] \\ =(4 \times 410.50+1 \times 606.10+1 \times 431.37) \\ -[6 \times 410.50)+(1 \times 336.49)] \\ =-120.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} .}$

question:
The enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is $\mathrm{-46 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$. The enthalpy change for the reaction :
$\rm{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
explanation:
The amount of heat absorbed or evolved when 1 mole of the substance is directly obtained from its constituent elements is called heat or enthalpy of formation.
The reaction for the formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is :
$\rm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Heat of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}=-46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
The enthalpy change for the given reaction,
$\rm{ 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=? \\ \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=-(2 \times \Delta \mathrm{H})=-(2 \times-46)=92 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question:
The ratio of heats liberated at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ from the combustion of one $\mathrm{\mathrm{kg}}$ of coke and by burning water gas obtained from $\mathrm{\mathrm{kg}}$ of coke is
(Assume coke to be $\mathrm{100 \%}$ carbon)
(Given : enthalpies of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ as $\mathrm{393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}, 285 \mathrm{~kJ}, 285 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ respectively all at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K})}$.
explanation:
Given information, the enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ is wrong it should be enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}}$.
$\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~kg}}$ of coke $\mathrm{=\dfrac{1000}{12}=83.33 \mathrm{moles}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{83.33 \times 396.5 \mathrm{~kJ}}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}$
$\mathrm{\underbrace{\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}}_{\text {Water gas }}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{83.33 \times(285+285) \\ 83.33 \times 570 \mathrm{~kJ}}}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
Now, the ratio of
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{coke}}}&{\rm{:}}&{{\rm{water}}\,\,{\rm{gas}}}\\{{\rm{83}}{\rm{.33 \times 393}}{\rm{.5}}}&{\rm{:}}&{{\rm{83}}{\rm{.33 \times 570}}}\\{ \Rightarrow {\rm{393}}{\rm{.5}}}&{\rm{:}}&{{\rm{570}}}\\{ \Rightarrow {\rm{0}}{\rm{.69}}}&{\rm{:}}&{\rm{1}}\end{array}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question: The bond dissociation energies of $\mathrm{X_{2}, Y_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{X Y}$ are in the ratio of $\mathrm{1: 0.5: 1 . \Delta H}$ for the formation of $\mathrm{X Y}$ is $\mathrm{-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$. The bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{X_{2}}$ will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{X_{2}: Y_{2}: X Y}$
Let B.E. $\mathrm{\mathrm{x} \quad \dfrac{x}{2} \quad x}$
Formation of $\mathrm{X Y}$
$\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} X_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} Y_{2} \rightarrow X Y ; \Delta H=-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ $\mathrm{\therefore \Delta H_{\text {reaction }}=B . E}$. of reactant ${\rm{ - B}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{.}}$ of product
$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {reaction }}=\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{x}+\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{4}-\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}=\dfrac{2 x+x-4 x}{4}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad-800=2 x+x-4 x}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad x=800 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ mole
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Bond dissociation energy is $\mathrm{800 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mole}^{-1}}$

The enthalpy of formation of ethane is

question: The heat of atomisation of methane and ethane are $\mathrm{360 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ and $\mathrm{620 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, respectively. The longest wavelength of light capable of breaking the $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}}$ bond is (Avogadro number $\rm{\left.=6.02 \times 10^{23}, h=6.62 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \text { : }}$
explanation:
In $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}, 4 \times B E_{(C-H)}=360 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore B E_{(C-H)}=90 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
In $\mathrm{C_{2} H_{6}, B E_{(C-C)}+6 \times B E_{(C-H)}=620 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore B E_{(C-C)}=80 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\therefore {\rm{B}}{{\rm{E}}_{({\rm{C - C}})}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{80}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}}}{{{\rm{6}}.{\rm{023}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}}}\,\,{\rm{J/}}molecule$
${\rm{Now}},{\rm{E = }}\frac{{{\rm{hc}}}}{\lambda }$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad \lambda=\dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{80 \times 10^{3}}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad \lambda=1.49 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~nm}}$

Given
${\rm{C}}({\rm{ graphite }}){\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}(\,{\rm{g}}){\rm{;}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H = 75}}\,\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$

The first four ionisation energies of carbon.

question: The standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is $\mathrm{-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$. If the enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ from its atoms is $\mathrm{-436 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$ and that of $\mathrm{N_{2}}$ is $\mathrm{-712 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, the average bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}}$ bond in $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is:
explanation:
Given
$\rm{ \dfrac{1}{2} N_{2}+\dfrac{3}{2} H_{2} \rightarrow N_{3} \Delta H=-46.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ 2 H \rightarrow H_{2} \Delta H=-436.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ 2 N \rightarrow N_{2} \Delta H=-712 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ \Delta H_{r}=\sum B E_{R}-\sum B E_{P} \\-46= {\left[\dfrac{1}{2}(+712)+\dfrac{3}{2}(436)\right]-3 B E_{N-H} } \\-46=(356+3 \times 218)-3 B E_{N-H} \\ 3 B E_{N-H}=(356+654)+46 \\=1010+46}$

$\rm{B E_{N-H}=\dfrac{1056}{3}=+352 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$

question:
Given that:
(i) $\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}}$ of $\mathrm{N_{2} O}$ is $\mathrm{82 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(ii) Bond energies of $\mathrm{N \equiv N, N=N, O=O}$ and $\mathrm{N=O}$ are $\mathrm{946,418,498}$ and $\mathrm{607 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively,
The resonance energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$ is :
explanation:
$\mathrm{N_{2}(g)+\dfrac{1}{2} O_{2} \rightarrow N_{2} O(g)}$
or $\mathrm{N \equiv N(g)+\dfrac{1}{2}(O=O) \rightarrow \ddot{N}=\stackrel{+}{N}=\ddot{O}(g)}$
$\mathrm{\Delta H_{f}^{o}=}$ [Energy required for breaking of bonds]
- [Energy released for forming of bonds]
$\mathrm{=\left(\Delta H_{N \equiv N}+\dfrac{1}{2} \Delta H_{O=O}\right)-\left(\Delta H_{N=N}+\Delta H_{N=O}\right)}$
$\mathrm{=\left(946+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 498\right)-(418+607)=170 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Resonance energy
${{\rm{\Delta }}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{H}}{{\rm{^\circ }}_{\left( {{\rm{experimental}}} \right)}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{\Delta }}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{H}}{{\rm{^\circ }}_{\left( {{\rm{theoretical}}} \right)}}$
${\rm{ = 82 - 170 = - 88}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$

question: Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to
explanation:
Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to enthalpy of fusion + enthalpy of vapourisation.
Sublimation is direct conversion of solid to vapour, i.e., solid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ Vapour
Writing in two steps, we have solid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ liquid
$\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ Vapour
Solid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ liquid requires enthalpy of fusion
Liquid $\mathrm{\rightarrow}$ vapour requires enthalpy of vapourisation.

question:
Enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$, $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})}$ are $\mathrm{-110,-393,81}$ and ${\rm{9}}{\rm{.7}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ respectively. The value of $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}}$ for the reaction is
$\rm{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
explanation:
Heat of reaction
$\rm{\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}=\sum \Delta_{r} H_{\text {products }}^{o}-\sum \Delta_{r} H_{\text {reactants }}^{o}}$
$\mathrm{=\left[\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}\left(N_{2} O\right)+3 \Delta_{r} H^{\circ}\left(C O_{2}\right)\right]}$
$\mathrm{-\left[\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}\left(N_{2} O_{4}\right)+3 \Delta_{r} H^{\circ}(C O)\right]}$
$\mathrm{=[81+(3 \times-393)]-[9.7+(3 \times-110)] k J}$
$\mathrm{=-777.7 \mathrm{~kJ} \approx-778 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
From the following data of $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ of the following reactions,
${\rm{C}}({\rm{s}}){\rm{ + }}{\raise0.5ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 1$}\kern-0.1em/\kern-0.15em\lower0.25ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 2$}}\,{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{CO}}({\rm{g}});\Delta {\rm{H = - 110kJ}}$ and
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=132 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
calculate the mole composition of the mixture of oxygen and steam on being passed over coke at $\mathrm{1273 \mathrm{~K}}$, keeping the reaction temperature constant.
explanation:
From the question we see that the first reaction is exothermic and the second one is endothermic. Thus, if a mixture of oxygen and steam $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}$ is passed over coke and at the same time, temperature does not change, the composition should be such that $\mathrm{\Delta H}$ of both the reactions are numerically equal.
In the first reaction, consumption of $\mathrm{1 / 2}$ mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ evolves $\mathrm{110 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ energy while in the second reaction, for 1 mole of steam $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right), 132 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of energy is absorbed.
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ needed to evolve
$\mathrm{132 \mathrm{~kJ}=\dfrac{0.5}{110} \times 132=0.6}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Mole ratio of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ and steam $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)=0.6: 1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
A cooking gas cylinder is assumed to contain $\mathrm{11.2 \mathrm{~kg}}$ is obutane. The combustion of isobutane is given by
${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{10}}}}({\rm{g}}){\rm{ + }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{13}}}}{{\rm{2}}}} \right){{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{4C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}){\rm{ + 5}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{l}});$
$\Delta {\rm{H = - 2658}}\,{\rm{kJ}}$
If a family needs $\mathrm{15000 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of energy per day for cooking, how long would the cylinder last?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\because 58 \mathrm{~g}}$ isobutane provides energy $\mathrm{=2658}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad 11.2 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~g}}$ isobutane provides energy
$\rm{=\dfrac{2658 \times 11.2 \times 10^{3}}{58} k J=513268.9 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
The daily requirement of energy $\mathrm{=15000 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Cylinder will last in $\mathrm{\dfrac{513268.9}{15000}=34}$ days
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
At a particular temperature
$\rm{\mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
The approximate heat evolved when $\mathrm{400 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is mixed with $\mathrm{600 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{\rm{KOH}}$ solution will be
explanation:
Number of equivalents $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ taken
$\rm{=\dfrac{0.2 \times 2 \times 400}{1000}=0.16}$
Number of equivalents $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ added $\mathrm{=\dfrac{600 \times 0.1}{1000}=0.06}$
Number of equivalent of acid and bases neutralised each other $\mathrm{=0.06}$.
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat evolved $\mathrm{=0.06 \times 57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}=3.426 \mathrm{~kJ}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Neutralisation

question:
The standard heats of formation for $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(g)}, \quad \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}_{(g)}}$ are $\mathrm{-25.5,-57.8,-94.1}$ and ${\rm{ - 22}}{\rm{.1}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}$ respectively. Calculate $\mathrm{\Delta H_{(298 K)}}$ (in kcal) for the reaction :
$\rm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(g)}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}+4 \mathrm{HCl}_{(g)}}$
explanation:
At $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}, \Delta H}$ values are $\mathrm{\Delta H^{\circ}}$, i.e., standard heat of formation. For
${\rm{CC}}{{\rm{l}}_{{\rm{4(g)}}}}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2(g)}}}}{\rm{ + 4HC}}{{\rm{l}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}}{\rm{;}}\quad {\rm{\Delta H}}^\circ {\rm{ = ?}}$
${\rm{\Delta H}}_{{\rm{Reaction }}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ = \Delta H}}_{{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ + 4 \times \Delta H}}_{{\rm{HCl}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ - \Delta H}}_{{\rm{CC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{4}}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{ - 2 \times \Delta H}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}$
${\rm{ = - 94}}{\rm{.1 + 4 \times ( - 22}}{\rm{.1) - ( - 25}}{\rm{.5) - 2 \times ( - 57}}{\rm{.8)}}$
${\rm{ = - 41}}{\rm{.4kcal}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
In the reaction :
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{S}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{l}})}}{\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{g}})}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{g}})}}{\rm{ + 2S}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}({\rm{g}})}};\Delta {\rm{H = - 265}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}$
The enthalpies of formation for both $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ are negative and are in the ratio $\mathrm{4: 3}$. The enthalpy of formation for ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{2}}}\,\,{\rm{is + 26}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$. The enthalpy of formation for $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CS}_{2(1)}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H = - 265}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{o}}\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\,{\rm{g}}} \right){\rm{ = 4x}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{o}}\left( {{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\,{\rm{g}}} \right){\rm{ = 3x}}$
${\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H = }}\sum {{\Delta _{\rm{f}}}} {\rm{H}}_{{\rm{products }}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ - }}\sum {{\Delta _{\rm{f}}}} {\rm{H}}_{{\rm{reactants }}}^{\rm{o}}$
${\rm{ - 265 = 4x + 6x - 26}}$
${\rm{10x = 239}}$
${\rm{x = - 23}}.{\rm{9}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$
$\Delta {\rm{H}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{o}}\left( {{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}},\,{\rm{g}}} \right){\rm{ = - 71}}.{\rm{7}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{cal/mol}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question:
What will be the enthalpy change for the following reaction?
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}){\rm{ + B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{2HBr}}({\rm{g}})$
Given that Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Br}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{HBr}}$ is $\mathrm{435 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, 192 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{368 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}=\sum B . E_{r e a}-\sum B . E_{p r o}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=}$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}+}$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}_{2}-2 \times}$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HBr}}$
$\mathrm{=435+192-(2 \times 368) \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$ $\mathrm{\Rightarrow \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-109 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question: The standard heat of formation values of $\mathrm{\mathrm{SF}_{6}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{s})}}$, and $\mathrm{\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{g})}$ are $\mathrm{-1100,275}$, and 80 $\mathrm{\mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, respectively. Then the average $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}}$ bond energy in $\mathrm{\mathrm{SF}_{6}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{g})}+6 \mathrm{~F}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{SF}_{6(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{f}=-1100 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{s})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{S}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=+275 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
${\raise0.5ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 1$} \kern-0.1em/\kern-0.15em\lower0.25ex\hbox{$\scriptstyle 2$}}$ ${{\rm{F}}_{{\rm{2}}(\,{\rm{g}})}} \to {{\rm{F}}_{({\rm{g}})}};\quad \Delta {\rm{H = 80}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{k}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{J/mol}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Heat of formation $\mathrm{=}$ Bond energy of reactant
- Bond energy of product
$\mathrm{-1100=[275+6 \times 80]-[6 \times \mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}]}$
Thus, bond energy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}=309 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Bond Enthalpy or Bond Energy

question:
The enthalpy change for the process $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+4 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})}}$ and bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}}$ in $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})}$ is
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\text {vap }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=30.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=-135.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
$\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}(\mathrm{C})=715.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, where $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} \quad}$ is enthalpy of atomisation, $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)=242 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation:
Given,
(i) $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(1)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta_{\text {vap }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=+30.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
(ii) $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4(l)} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-135.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
(iii) $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=715.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(iv) $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=242 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
(iii) $\mathrm{+2 *}$ (iv) - (i) - (ii) gives
$\rm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+4 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=1304 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
Bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}}$ bond in $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}=}$ $\rm{\dfrac{1304}{4}=326 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
( $\mathrm{\because}$ There are four $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}}$ bonds in $\mathrm{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}}$ ).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Enthalpy of Atomization

question: The standard enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$, $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\ell)}$ and glucose (s) at $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ are $\mathrm{-400 \mathrm{~kJ} /}$$\mathrm{\mathrm{mol},-300 \mathrm{~kJ} /\mathrm{mol}}$ and $\mathrm{-1300 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$, respectively. The standard enthalpy of combustion per gram of glucose at $\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ is
explanation:
${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{12}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{6}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{6C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta_{f} H_{O_{2}}^{\circ}=0}$
$\mathrm{\Delta H=\sum \Delta_{f} H_{P}^{\circ}-\sum \Delta_{f} H_{R}^{\circ}}$
$\mathrm{=6 \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}+6 \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)}^{\circ}}$
$\mathrm{=(6 \times-400)+6(-300)-(-1300)}$
$\mathrm{=-2400-1800+1300=-2900 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}}$
$\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{H}=\dfrac{-2900}{180}=-16.11 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{gm}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

Given that the standard states for iodine and chlorine are $\mathrm{\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$, the standard enthalpy of formation for $\mathrm{\mathrm{ICl}(\mathrm{g})}$ is

question: The heats of combustion of rhombic and monoclinic sulphur are respectively 70.96 and $\mathrm{71.03 \mathrm{k}}$ cal. What will be the heat of conversion of rhombic sulphur to monoclinic?
explanation:
Given: (1) $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{(\text {rhombic) }}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2}}$
$\rm{\Delta H=-70.96 \mathrm{kcal}}$
(2) $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\text {(monoclinic) }}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2}, \quad \Delta H=-71.03 \mathrm{kcal}}$
Eqn. (1) - Eqn (2) gives $\mathrm{S_{(\text {rhombic) }} \rightarrow S_{\text {(monoclinic) }}}$
$\rm{(\Delta) \mathrm{H}=71.03-70.96=0.07 \mathrm{kcal}=70 \mathrm{cal}}$

question: The bond energies of $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}$ linkages are $\mathrm{350,600,400}$ and $\mathrm{410 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ per mole respectively. The heat of hydrogenation of ethylene is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958979/preptracker/questions/q_16522_explanation_1764958978981.png $\rm{\left. {{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{reaction }}}}{\rm{ = }}\sum {{\rm{ B}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{. }}} {\rm{( Reactants ) - }}\sum {{\rm{ B}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{.(Products }}} } \right)}$
${\rm{ = }}[4 \times {\rm{B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C - H}}){\rm{ + B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C = C}}){\rm{ + B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{H - H}})]$
${\rm{ - }}[6 \times {\rm{B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C - H}}){\rm{ + B}}.{\rm{E}}.({\rm{C - C}})]$
${\rm{ = }}\left[ {\left( {{\rm{4 \times 410}}} \right){\rm{ + 600 + 400}}} \right]{\rm{ - }}\left[ {{\rm{6 \times 410 + 350}}} \right]$
${\rm{ = - 170}}\,{\rm{k}}\,{\rm{J}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$

explanation: Heat of dilution is the heat change when a solution containing one mole of the solute is diluted from one concentration to another, $\mathrm{=4.40-3.80=0.60 \mathrm{kcal}}$.

question: A solution of $\mathrm{500 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KOH}}$ and 500 $\mathrm{\mathrm{mL}}$ of $\mathrm{0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}}$ is mixed and stirred; the rise in temperature is $\mathrm{T_{1}}$. The experiment is repeated using $\mathrm{250 \mathrm{~mL}}$ each of solution, the temperature rise is $\mathrm{T_{2}}$. Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Suppose heat evolved in ${{\rm{1}}^{{\rm{st}}}}$ case is $\mathrm{Q_{1}}$ and that in the ${{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{nd}}}}$ case it is $\mathrm{Q_{2}}$. Then $\mathrm{Q_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} Q_{1}}$.
But $\mathrm{Q_{1}=1000 T_{1}}$ and $\mathrm{Q_{2}=500 T_{2}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 500 T_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 T_{1}}$, i.e., $\mathrm{T_{2}=T_{1}}$

The heat of hydrogenation of cyclohexene is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{6} H_{10}+H_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} H_{12}}$
${\Delta _{\left( {{\rm{Hydrogenation}}} \right)}}{\rm{H = }}{\Delta _{\rm{c}}}{{\rm{H}}_{({\rm{Reactants}})}}{\rm{ - }}{\Delta _{\rm{c}}}{{\rm{H}}_{({\rm{Products}})}}$
$ = {\Delta _{\rm{C}}}{\rm{H}}\left( {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{10}}}}} \right){\rm{ + }}{\Delta _{\rm{C}}}{\rm{H}}\left( {{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right){\rm{ - }}{\Delta _{\rm{C}}}{\rm{H}}\left( {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{12}}}}} \right)$
${\rm{ = }}\left( {{\rm{ - 3800}}} \right){\rm{ + }}\left( {{\rm{ - 241}}} \right){\rm{ - }}\left( {{\rm{ - 3920}}} \right)$
${\rm{ = - 121k}}\,{\rm{J}}$

question: The enthalpy of combustion of methane, graphite and dihydrogen at $\mathrm{298 \mathrm{~K}}$ are, $\mathrm{-890.3 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{-285.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$ respectively. Required equation for the formation of 1 mole of methane, $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}$ is given by

question: If the combustion of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ of graphite produces $\mathrm{20.7 \mathrm{~kJ}}$ of heat, what will be molar enthalpy change ?

Molar enthalpy change of graphite $\mathrm{=}$ enthalpy change for $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ carbon $\mathrm{\times}$ molar mass of carbon

$\mathrm{\therefore \Delta H=-2.48 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$

question:
Comment on the thermodynamic stability of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})}$, given
$\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=90 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} ; \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-74 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
explanation: $\mathrm{N O(g)}$ is unstable because formation of $\mathrm{N O}$ is endothermic (energy is absorbed), but $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$ is formed because its formation is exothermic (energy is released). Therefore, unstable $\mathrm{N O(g)}$ converts into stable $\mathrm{N_{2}(g)}$.

question: The enthalpy of dissolution of $\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} .2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})}$ are -20.6 and $\mathrm{8.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$, respectively. The enthalpy of hydration for $\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2} .2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})}$ is
explanation:
Given (i) $\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(s)+a q \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(a q)}$
$\rm{\Delta H=-20.6 k J}$
(ii) $\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s)+a q \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(a q)}$
$\rm{\Delta H=+8.8 \mathrm{~kJ}}$
Eqn. (i) - Eqn. (ii) gives
$\rm{ \mathrm{BaCl}_{(2)}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s) \\ \Delta H=-20.6-8.8=-29.4 \mathrm{~kJ}}$

question:

question:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{OH}}\,{\rm{(l)}}$ from the following data:
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}_{({\rm{1}})}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{\rm{3}}}{{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}(\,{\rm{g}})}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}(\,{\rm{g}})}}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{({\rm{1}})}};$
${\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H}}^\circ {\rm{ = - 726}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
${{\rm{C}}_{({\rm{graphite}})}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}(\,{\rm{g}})}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{2}}(\,{\rm{g}})}};$
${\Delta _{\rm{c}}}{\rm{H}}^\circ {\rm{ = - 393}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
explanation:
Required reaction for the formation of methanol is as follows
$\rm{C_{(s)}+ 2 H_{2(g)}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}_{(l)} ; \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=? \\\Delta_{f} H^{\circ} =\sum \Delta_{c} H_{\text {products }}^{o}-\sum \Delta_{c} H_{\text {reactants }}^{o} \\ =726-(-393+2(-286)) \\ =-239 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

explanation: Calorific value is the heat of combination per gram. Calorific value for $\mathrm{H_{2}=-285 / 2}$, for $\mathrm{C O=-284 / 28}$, for $\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}=-890 / 16}$.

subtopic: Heat of Combustion and Standard Heat of Combustion

question:
The enthalpy of reaction $\mathrm{: 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(1)}}$ is $\mathrm{\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-572 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$.
What will be standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$ ?
explanation:
According to the definition of standard enthalpy of formation, the enthalpy change for the following reaction will be standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(l)}}$.
$\mathrm{H_{2(g)}+\dfrac{1}{2} O_{2(g)} \rightarrow H_{2} O_{(l)}}$ or the standard enthalpy
of formation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$ will be half of the enthalpy of the given equation, i.e., $\mathrm{\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}}$ is also halved
${\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}^{\rm{o}}({\rm{l}}){\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}} \times {\Delta _{\rm{r}}}{\rm{H}}^\circ $
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{ - 572}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}{{\rm{2}}}$
${\rm{ = - 286}}\,{\rm{kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Formation

question: The gas in a refrigerator causes cooling on expansion because
explanation: Imagine the gas was in a cylinder and expanded by pushing against a piston. The movement would involve work being done. This work would need energy, which would come from the kinetic energy of the gas molecules hitting the piston- reducing their average speed. Taking KE away corresponds to lowering the temperature of the gas.

options.text: Work done by the gas is converted into heat
Heat of the gas is lost as work done by the gas
The heat is spread over a large space

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Work and Heat

question:
Assertion: Zeroth law can also be termed as law of thermal equilibrium.


Reason: Two objects in thermal equilibrium with the third one, are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
explanation: The Zeroth Law states that if two objects are each in thermal equilibrium with a third object, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. So, option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
